title: Pilot readiness: proposed schedule, success metrics & owners
preview:
From: Alex Martinez <alex@redwood.com>
To: Ellen Park <ellen@novusretail.com>, Carlos Ruiz <c.ruiz@novusretail.com>
Cc: Marissa Cole <marissa@redwood.com>
Date: 2026-03-04T15:12:00-07:00
Subject: Follow-up & draft pilot plan (post-demo)

Hi Ellen / Carlos —

Thanks again for the demo time today. Good conversation — I captured the key outcomes and a short draft pilot plan below so we can move to a concrete kickoff next week.

Quick summary from my notes:
- Goal: validate Redwood inference for recommendation reranking and customer search relevance for the holiday catalog
- Primary success metrics you called out: relevance uplift vs current baseline (precision@10), API latency (p95), and total inference cost per 1k queries
- Stakeholders: Ellen (product), Carlos (ML/ops), Procurement TBD

Draft pilot (high level):
- Scope: integrate Redwood API for a single classification/reranking endpoint, run production-like load on a 3-week trial window
- Timeline: Kickoff workshop (week 0), 2 full weeks of running evaluation + weekly check-ins, final readout in week 3
- Proposed kickoff window: Mar 9–13 — I suggest Mar 11 at 10:00 AM PT for the initial workshop
- Weekly cadence: 30-minute check-ins (status, blockers, metrics)
- Success criteria (proposal):
  1) +5% precision@10 on the test prompt set compared to baseline
  2) API p95 latency <= 300ms for the test distribution
  3) Cost projection aligns with your $/1k target (we'll produce a burn estimate)

Next steps I need from you to finalize schedule:
1) Confirm the success criteria above (add/remove items)
2) Confirm stakeholders who should be at kickoff (prod/ops/procurement)
3) Any non-functional constraints (data residency, firewall, VPN) we should know about

I attached a lightweight POC template (Novus_POC_Template.xlsx) with milestones and owner slots — please fill the owner column where you can.

If that plan looks roughly right I’ll loop in our eng leads (Marissa and Jordan) to assign owners and lock dates.

Thanks —
Alex Martinez
Account Executive | Redwood Inference
alex@redwood.com | +1 415-555-0123

Attachment: Novus_POC_Template.xlsx (spreadsheet, 1 sheet)


From: Ellen Park <ellen@novusretail.com>
To: Alex Martinez <alex@redwood.com>
Cc: Carlos Ruiz <c.ruiz@novusretail.com>
Date: 2026-03-05T09:47:00-07:00
Subject: Re: Follow-up & draft pilot plan (post-demo)

Thanks Alex — this is really helpful and aligned with our thinking. A few quick notes and confirmations from our side:

Success criteria (confirmed / clarifications):
- Business: target +5% precision@10 on the test set (we'll provide the labeled eval set)
- Performance: p95 latency target to start at 350ms (we can tighten once we see data) — is that reasonable on your end?
- Cost: we need a projected burn estimate for a 2k qps equivalent during peak, broken out by model and instance type

Stakeholders for kickoff:
- Ellen Park (product)
- Carlos Ruiz (ML/ops)
- Lydia Chen (security) — she needs to attend the kickoff to sign off on data handling
- Procurement: I will loop in Kara after we finalize the scope

Constraints / notes:
- Data residency: all data during the pilot must remain in us-west region — no cross-region backups without approval
- We will not provide PII in the eval set; use synthetic PII placeholders if needed

Kickoff: Mar 11 at 10:00 AM PT works for Carlos and me. If that’s also valid for your team we can lock it.

I’ve filled the primary owner column in the attached POC template with the product-side owners where known.

Ellen
Ellen Park
Senior Product Manager, Search & Recommendations
Novus Retail
ellen@novusretail.com | +1 212-555-0987



From: Alex Martinez <alex@redwood.com>
To: Marissa Cole <marissa@redwood.com>, Jordan Blake <jordan@redwood.com>
Date: 2026-03-06T11:05:00-07:00
Subject: Fwd: Follow-up & draft pilot plan (post-demo) — Novus pilot kickoff confirmation + asks

Marissa / Jordan — forwarding Ellen’s reply with a few asks. TL;DR:
- Kickoff confirmed by the customer for Mar 11, 10:00 AM PT
- Success criteria: +5% precision@10, p95 latency target 350ms (customer ask), cost projection for 2k-qps peak
- Constraints: us-west residency for pilot data; no PII in eval set

Can you review and confirm:
1) Engineering lead availability for Mar 11 kickoff and weekly check-ins
2) Who will own monitoring/telemetry (sample dashboards + telemetry hooks)
3) A quick estimate of the infra footprint for a realistic 2k-qps sim so we can give them burn numbers on day 1

Quoted message follows:
> From: Ellen Park <ellen@novusretail.com>
> To: Alex Martinez <alex@redwood.com>
> Date: 2026-03-05T09:47:00-07:00
> Subject: Re: Follow-up & draft pilot plan (post-demo)
> 
> Thanks Alex — this is really helpful and aligned with our thinking...

Appreciate a quick ping back — customer is ready to lock dates so we should get owners assigned and the evaluation matrix instrumented before kickoff.

— Alex


From: Marissa Cole <marissa@redwood.com>
To: Alex Martinez <alex@redwood.com>, Jordan Blake <jordan@redwood.com>
Date: 2026-03-08T08:22:00-07:00
Subject: Re: Fwd: Follow-up & draft pilot plan — proposed schedule & owner assignments

Thanks Alex — I can cover the engagement lead and run the weekly check-ins, Jordan will act as infra lead for deployment and perf tuning. Quick plan below (feel free to forward to Ellen/Carlos):

Proposed pilot calendar (finalized):
- Kickoff workshop: Mar 11, 2026 — 10:00–11:15 AM PT (agenda: scope,-responsibilities, data handling, initial runbook)
- Check-in #1: Mar 18, 2026 — 10:00–10:30 AM PT (initial metrics & first perf observations)
- Check-in #2: Mar 25, 2026 — 10:00–10:30 AM PT (mid-run tuning + cost projection)
- Final readout: Apr 1, 2026 — 10:00–11:00 AM PT (results, next steps, procurement handoff)

Owner assignments:
- Program lead / customer engagement: Marissa Cole (marissa@redwood.com)
- Infra / perf & tuning: Jordan Blake (jordan@redwood.com)
- Sales / commercial: Alex Martinez (alex@redwood.com)
- Customer-side liaisons: Ellen Park (product), Carlos Ruiz (ML/ops), Lydia Chen (security)

Instrumentation & evaluation notes:
- We’ll pre-load the evaluation-matrix.csv (attached) with the customer’s labeled test set and synthetic edge cases; this will drive the weekly precision@10 calculations
- Jordan will enable telemetry hooks for p95/p99 and token-level cost reporting; Marissa will set up a shared dashboard in the Drive link
- We will provide a burn estimate for 2k-qps using a standard config; Jordan says he can get a conservative estimate by EOD Mar 9

Attachments: evaluation-matrix.csv (CSV with prompt ids, expected labels, owner column)

I’ll add the Drive folder and the HubSpot record once Alex confirms — I want to make sure the customer has the template and metrics in place before kickoff.

— Marissa
Marissa Cole
Customer Success Manager | Redwood Inference
marissa@redwood.com | +1 415-555-0166


From: Alex Martinez <alex@redwood.com>

Cc: Marissa Cole <marissa@redwood.com>, Jordan Blake <jordan@redwood.com>
Date: 2026-03-09T09:32:00-07:00
Subject: Confirmed: Novus pilot kickoff Mar 11 + weekly cadence + pre-kickoff checklist

Ellen / Carlos — thanks for confirming availability. We’re locked for kickoff and here are the concrete next steps and owners.

Confirmed calendar:
- Kickoff: Mar 11, 2026 — 10:00–11:15 AM PT (Zoom invite to follow)
- Weekly check-ins: Mar 18 & Mar 25 — 10:00–10:30 AM PT
- Final readout: Apr 1 — 10:00–11:00 AM PT

Owners & immediate asks:
- Novus: please confirm Lydia Chen for the kickoff (security) and Kara (procurement) to be looped before the final readout
- Novus: please upload the labeled eval set (no PII) and confirm the stakeholder list in the POC template
- Redwood: Jordan will send a conservative burn estimate for a 2k-qps sim by EOD Mar 9; Marissa will populate the evaluation matrix and create the shared Drive folder

Links & attachments:
- HubSpot record: https://app.hubspot.com/contacts/123456/company/7890
- Shared folder (will be populated): https://drive.google.com/drive/folders/1a2b3cPOCdocs

If this all looks good I’ll send the calendar invites and the Zoom link. We’ll also prepare a short runbook for the kickoff (data handling checklist, telemetry hooks, success metrics definitions).

Thanks — excited to get started.

Alex Martinez
Account Executive | Redwood Inference
alex@redwood.com | +1 415-555-0123

title: Aligning demo time across US/EU/APAC during DST mismatch
preview:
From: Alice Murray <alice.murray@zenithpay.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>
Cc: Tim Sloan <tim.sloan@zenithpay.com>
Date: 2026-03-08T09:12:00+00:00
Subject: Re: Aligning demo time across US/EU/APAC during DST mismatch

Hi Ben —

Thanks for confirming the demo. Quick heads up: my calendar shows the invite for Monday 10:00 AM, but Tim (in Boston) is getting a calendar notice for 6:00 AM his time. We realized the US spring forward happens this weekend and our systems sometimes show inconsistent local translations when EU hasn't shifted yet.

Can we nudge the slot by an hour or propose an alternative that works cleanly for London, Boston, and Mumbai without folks losing sleep? Our leadership (Tim & I) want to make sure we have the product team present — moving earlier than 08:00 ET is tricky for them.

Attached: a quick note from our IT team about calendar behavior (they prefer invite times in UTC to avoid DST translation bugs).

Thanks,
Alice Murray
Head of Product Integrations
ZenithPay

---
Attachment: it-calendar-note.txt (text)


From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>
To: Alice Murray <alice.murray@zenithpay.com>
Cc: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.ai>, Vivek Kulkarni <vivek.kulkarni@redwood.ai>
Date: 2026-03-08T11:20:00-07:00
Subject: Re: Aligning demo time across US/EU/APAC during DST mismatch

Hi Alice —

Appreciate the flag, and sorry for the confusion. This is a classic DST mismatch week (US springs forward this weekend, EU/London stays the same until later in March). A few thoughts:

- We can publish the meeting in UTC and include explicit local translations in the invite body to avoid client-side calendar conversions.
- Proposed UTC anchors that avoid painful times:
  * 14:00 UTC (09:00 ET / 14:00 GMT / 19:30 IST) — early for Boston but still reasonable
  * 16:00 UTC (11:00 ET / 16:00 GMT / 21:30 IST) — mid morning in US, late evening in India (may be tough)
  * 12:30 UTC (07:30 ET / 12:30 GMT / 18:00 IST) — earlier for Boston but keeps Mumbai within business hours

If the leadership team wants to avoid earlier-than-08:00 ET, 14:00 UTC is the sweet spot. I copied Marissa and Vivek so we can confirm support coverage and make sure we have APAC representation if we go late.

I attached a short mutual action plan and an .ics with the three proposed slots so you can drop them into your calendar and check translations. If you'd prefer, we can also run the demo twice (one APAC-friendly, one Americas/EU-friendly) — we can scope that quickly in the MAP.

Proposed next step: pick the slot that works for Tim & team and I'll send an explicit UTC-anchored invite with local times in the description.

Best,
Ben Carter
Enterprise Sales
Redwood Inference

---
Attachments: mutual_action_plan_v2.docx, proposed_demo_slots.ics


From: Vivek Kulkarni <vivek.kulkarni@redwood.ai>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>, Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.ai>
Cc: Alice Murray <alice.murray@zenithpay.com>
Date: 2026-03-09T00:45:00+05:30
Subject: Re: Aligning demo time across US/EU/APAC during DST mismatch

Ben / Marissa / Alice —

Quick confirm from APAC side: 14:00 UTC (19:30 IST) is doable for our engineer (Neha can join during that window). If you pick 16:00 UTC, our coverage will be thin — we can rotate but it may be late for Mumbai.

One operational note we learned helping other customers during mismatch weeks: add a one-line UTC anchor at the top of the invite (e.g., "Demo: 2026-03-16 14:00 UTC — local times below") and list local times for common cities. That prevents calendar clients that auto-convert from showing ambiguous times.

I can also attend and cover the product deep-dive if Marissa needs to hand off.

-Vivek
Solutions Engineer, Redwood Inference

> On 2026-03-08 11:20 -07:00 Ben Carter wrote:
> > If the leadership team wants to avoid earlier-than-08:00 ET, 14:00 UTC is the sweet spot. I copied Marissa and Vivek so we can confirm support coverage and make sure we have APAC representation if we go late.
> >
> > Proposed next step: pick the slot that works for Tim & team and I'll send an explicit UTC-anchored invite with local times in the description.


From: Alice Murray <alice.murray@zenithpay.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>
Cc: Tim Sloan <tim.sloan@zenithpay.com>
Date: 2026-03-09T03:22:00+05:30
Subject: Re: Aligning demo time across US/EU/APAC during DST mismatch

Ben / Vivek —

Thanks, that helps a lot. Tim and I checked and 14:00 UTC works for us (09:00 ET / 14:00 GMT). We'll take that slot. Please send the UTC-anchored calendar invite and include the mutual action plan you attached.

Two quick asks:
- Can you include a one-page runbook (what to expect during the demo + who to contact if we lose audio) in the invite?
- Can you confirm whether recordings are OK — our CISO prefers no recordings without a signed DPA in place. I'll loop in procurement separately if needed.

Appreciate the responsiveness. Look forward to the demo on 2026-03-16 14:00 UTC.

Regards,
Alice Murray
Head of Product Integrations
ZenithPay

---
Attachment: zenith-it-note.pdf (referenced)

title: POC plan & risk log — success criteria, timeline, owners
preview:
From: Aditya Rao <aditya@redwood.com>
To: Samantha Cole <samantha.cole@brightai.com>, Peter Holt <p.holt@brightai.com>
Cc: Laura Bennett <laura@redwood.com>
Date: Sun, 1 Mar 2026 15:10:00 -0700
Subject: POC kickoff — proposed success criteria & 2-week timeline

Hi Samantha & Peter,

Thanks again for the call today — good alignment on the evaluation goals. Per our discussion, I put together a compact POC plan so BrightAI's engineering and product teams can validate whether Redwood meets your production latency and cost goals. Please review and flag anything missing.

Top-level proposal (attached: poc-success-criteria.xlsx):
- Duration: 2 weeks active evaluation (Mon 2026-03-08 to Fri 2026-03-19) with light steady-state week following.
- Scope: 1 critical flow (customer-facing chat), dataset subset (approx. 10k prompts), model candidate: redwood-7B-quant (we can swap to 13B if you want quality-first).
- Success criteria (quantified):
  - 95th pct tail latency <= 220ms for inference (under target concurrency)
  - Generation cost <= $0.XX per 1k tokens (TBD based on token mix)
  - Functional parity on 6 OEM prompt categories (pass/fail automated tests)
  - No data exfil risk; audit logs enabled and retained for 30 days
- Evaluation plan: end-to-end test harness + synthetic load + subset of live traffic (can be toggled). We'll provide a small harness and observability dashboard.

Risks & initial mitigations (full risk log attached as redwood-risk-log-v1.csv):
- Environment access delays: need IAM roles for your VPC or Dev account; action: Laura to coordinate SRE checklist.
- Model mismatch: we'll do a quick quality sweep during week 0 and propose model/quant configs.
- Billing/quotas: need to confirm Dedicated vs Hosted billing for trial — Marcus to confirm procurement steps.

Ownership recommended:
- Redwood lead (technical): Laura Bennett — infra onboarding, debugging, observability
- Redwood commercial: Aditya Rao — timelines, escalation, commercial terms
- BrightAI technical lead: Peter Holt — dataset and routing for live traffic slice

Next steps (action items):
1) Confirm kickoff date and who will be the BrightAI engineers assigned (reply by Wed 2026-03-03).
2) Share IAM/connection details or confirm if we should run in Hosted mode.
3) Marcus will confirm billing approach and any procurement constraints.

Attachments: poc-success-criteria.xlsx (spreadsheet), redwood-risk-log-v1.csv (risk tracker).

Thanks — I’ll schedule a 30-min kickoff once we have owners confirmed.

Best,
Aditya Rao
Account Executive | Redwood Inference
aditya@redwood.com | +1 (415) 555-0192

---
Note: Attachment stubs included above.


From: Samantha Cole <samantha.cole@brightai.com>
To: Aditya Rao <aditya@redwood.com>
Cc: Peter Holt <p.holt@brightai.com>
Date: Mon, 2 Mar 2026 09:22:00 -0800
Subject: Re: POC kickoff — proposed success criteria & 2-week timeline

Hi Aditya — thanks for the clear write-up. This looks good overall. A few quick items from our side: 

1) Team & kickoff: We can start on Mon 2026-03-08. I'll assign two engineers: Peter (cc'd) and Nina Park (nina.park@brightai.com). Can you add Nina to future threads?

2) Security: Our security team requires SOC2 evidence and the answers to a short questionnaire before we allow any VPC peering or connection. I'll send the questionnaire to Marcus today.

3) Blocking issue observed: when we attempted to spin up the sample harness in our staging account yesterday we hit an immediate dependency failure in your runtime image (glibc version mismatch). That prevented us from running the quick smoke tests. Can you confirm a remediation plan?

4) Success criteria: we're ok with the 95th pct latency target, but would like to add "no functional regressions on our regression suite (0 critical failures)" as a hard stop.

For now, please loop Nina and Laura on the onboarding and let us know who will own the runtime patch / hotfix.

Thanks,
Samantha
Director, Product (BrightAI)
samantha.cole@brightai.com

---
Quoted message follows below.


From: Aditya Rao <aditya@redwood.com>
To: Samantha Cole <samantha.cole@brightai.com>, Nina Park <nina.park@brightai.com>
Cc: Laura Bennett <laura@redwood.com>, Marcus Lin <marcus@redwood.com>, Peter Holt <p.holt@brightai.com>
Date: Mon, 2 Mar 2026 11:05:00 -0700
Subject: Re: POC kickoff — proposed success criteria & 2-week timeline

Hi Samantha & team — appreciate the fast turnaround and the added regression requirement (we agree on 0 critical failures).

On the dependency failure: Laura — can you confirm the root cause and quick mitigation? If glibc mismatch is accurate we can push a patched runtime image to a sandbox repo within 24 hours. Marcus — I'll need a quick confirmation on whether BrightAI wants the Hosted fallback (we can run the POC on Redwood Hosted to avoid the peering step) or if you prefer us to remediate in your staging account.

Immediate plan:
- Laura to reproduce dependency failure and publish a patched image (target: 2026-03-03 18:00 PST).
- If patch requires longer work, Redwood will provide a Hosted sandbox with identical config (availability: within 4 hours of notification).
- Marcus to pick up procurement/billing clarification and SOC2 artifacts.

I've attached an updated risk log (redwood-risk-log-v1.csv) with the glibc issue marked Critical and an initial mitigation column filled. We'll keep the log updated.

Laura / Marcus — can one of you confirm ownership on this thread and update the risk log with estimated ETA?

Aditya
Account Executive | Redwood Inference

[Attachment: redwood-risk-log-v1.csv (csv)]


From: Laura Bennett <laura@redwood.com>
To: Samantha Cole <samantha.cole@brightai.com>, Aditya Rao <aditya@redwood.com>, Marcus Lin <marcus@redwood.com>
Date: Wed, 3 Mar 2026 08:44:00 -0700
Subject: Re: POC kickoff — proposed success criteria & 2-week timeline

Thanks all — I reproduced the staging failure this morning. Root cause: base runtime image we documented still references glibc 2.28; BrightAI staging uses a glibc 2.34-based host. The binary we ship was linked against the older ABI.

Mitigation plan (short/medium term):
- Short term (24 hours): build and publish a compatibility image (tag: rinf-compat-glibc-2.34) that bundles the correct runtime libs. I'll push to our sandbox registry and share pull URL. I expect this to be usable by your engineers within ~6 hours after I push.
- Medium term (by 2026-03-12): produce multi-arch / multi-glibc images and add integration tests in our CI to avoid regressions.

Escalation path:
- If the compatibility image fails, escalate to Marcus (commercial) and Aditya (AE) for priority support; we will open a Sev with the infra team and commit a hotfix within the business day.

Updated risk log entry: 
- risk_id: RL-003
- title: Runtime glibc mismatch
- severity: Critical
- owner: Laura Bennett
- eta: 2026-03-03T14:00:00-07:00 (publish)
- mitigation: publish compat image, provide Hosted sandbox fallback

Marcus — I'm marking this as assigned to me for technical ownership. Marcus, please confirm procurement path for Hosted fallback.

Laura Bennett
Senior Solutions Engineer | Redwood Inference
laura@redwood.com

[Attachment stub: registry-pull-url.txt]


From: Marcus Lin <marcus@redwood.com>
To: Samantha Cole <samantha.cole@brightai.com>, Aditya Rao <aditya@redwood.com>, Laura Bennett <laura@redwood.com>
Date: Sun, 8 Mar 2026 10:42:00 -0700
Subject: Re: POC kickoff — proposed success criteria & 2-week timeline

Hi Samantha — quick update on procurement & SOC2: 

1) SOC2 artifacts: I uploaded the SOC2 summary and evidence bundle to the HubSpot record and shared the Drive link here: https://drive.google.com/file/d/1a2b3c. Samantha, please confirm your security team can accept the bundle for the trial onboarding.

2) Procurement / billing: For a short 2-week POC we can run on Hosted with a temporary trial billing profile (no contract required). If you want Dedicated reserved capacity for the test, we will need a short order form (estimate 2–3 business days). For speed, I recommend Hosted sandbox to avoid procurement friction — we'll apply a credit cap and monthly invoicing terms if you convert after the POC.

3) On the runtime issue: Laura confirmed the compat image is published and available. Laura: can you paste the pull command and a one-liner here so I can forward it to Nina & Peter?

If the image doesn't resolve the smoke tests, we will open a Sev and escalate to infra immediately. Aditya — happy to take point on commercial fallback and run the Hosted sandbox onboarding if BrightAI opts for the quicker path.

Thanks,
Marcus Lin
Senior Customer Ops | Redwood Inference
marcus@redwood.com

---
Attached: poc-success-criteria.xlsx (referenced earlier)

title: Biannual check-ins: manager notes & confidential org change
preview:
From: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com>
To: Ava Chen <ava@redwood.com>
Cc: Stephanie Nguyen <stephanie@redwood.com>
Date: Thu, 5 Mar 2026 08:03:00 -0800
Subject: Biannual check-ins: manager notes & confidential org change

Ava —

I want to start the thread for biannual check-ins this week and surface an org alignment I need to coordinate with HR before we roll anything out. High-level: two ICs on my team (one senior engineer, one lead) will see role/level adjustments tied to a new product scope. Before any manager notes are finalized or 1:1 conversations happen, I need HR to sign off on communication templates and timing.

Action items for you:
- Please block 30–45 minutes for a quick calibration on Tuesday (my suggested times below).
- Review the attached manager_notes_template.docx — I used it to draft talking points for the two directs.
- Confirm if you want to loop in Joanna (external change management consultant) for messaging review.

I’ve cc’d Stephanie for HR policy guidance — Stephanie, can you confirm the cooling-off window for any compensation changes and advise on whether we need exec approval ahead of manager conversations? Also: is there a preferred order (e.g., notify person A first and then person B)?

Attachments: [Attachment: manager_notes_template.docx (DOCX, 24KB)], [Attachment: org_change_draft.pdf (PDF, 110KB)]

Proposed calibration slots (PST): Tue 3/9 10:00–10:45, Tue 3/9 14:00–14:45, Wed 3/10 09:00–09:45.

Thanks — looking to keep this tight and confidential.

Ben Carter
Engineering Manager | Redwood Inference
ben@redwood.com | (415) 555-0123



From: Ava Chen <ava@redwood.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com>
Cc: Stephanie Nguyen <stephanie@redwood.com>
Date: Thu, 5 Mar 2026 09:26:00 -0800
Subject: Re: Biannual check-ins: manager notes & confidential org change

Ben —

Thanks for starting this. I read the manager template and made a few inline notes (will bring to the calibration). My initial thoughts:

- Prefer to notify the senior engineer first (less dependency ripple) and then the lead, but happy to pivot based on HR advice.
- Communication should emphasize scope change and growth opportunities rather than title/level raw numbers.
- Recommend a 1:1 follow-up playbook: manager conversation -> written summary -> optional calibration with people ops if questions arise.

I can do Tue 3/9 at 14:00 PST — save that slot on my calendar. I think looping Joanna for a quick messaging sanity-check would be valuable, especially for the lead role. If Joanna is ok, we can send the draft messaging and open one hour in her schedule for a review.

Quick note: I added a short \"manager talking points\" section to the template to keep manager language consistent across both directs.

Ava Chen
Staff Engineering Manager | Redwood Inference
ava@redwood.com

From: Stephanie Nguyen <stephanie@redwood.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com>, Ava Chen <ava@redwood.com>
Date: Thu, 5 Mar 2026 11:12:00 -0800
Subject: Re: Biannual check-ins: manager notes & confidential org change

Thanks for the ping and for keeping HR in the loop. A few things from people ops perspective:

- Compensation / level movement: if there is any comp adjustment above 6% we will need VP approval and finance sign-off. Any leveling changes that affect banding should be routed through People Systems for payroll alignment.
- Confidentiality: keep conversations one-on-one until both manager and HR have signed off on the final messaging. No calendar invites or Slack group mentions about \"org changes\" until after notices are sent.
- Sequence advice: I agree with Ava — notify the senior engineer first to minimize customer-facing risk; then the lead. If there's risk of retention impact, we should be prepared with a retention counter-offer option (documented and pre-approved).
- Documentation: please upload final manager notes (redacted version) to the private folder in Drive and share the link with HR before 1:1s. I can review within 24 hours.

I added comments to org_change_draft.pdf and included a short checklist below. I can also join the calibration on Tue 3/9 at 14:00 if needed.

HR checklist (short):
- Confirm compensation thresholds and approvals needed
- Verify system changes (title/role fields) and target effective date
- Prepare communications FAQ for the team and for cross-functional partners
- Confirm required exec notifications and timing

Attachments referenced: org_change_draft.pdf (annotated)

Stephanie Nguyen
People Partner | Redwood Inference
stephanie@redwood.com

> 
> Quick note: I added a short \"manager talking points\" section to the template to keep manager language consistent across both directs.
> 
> Ava Chen

From: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com>
To: Ava Chen <ava@redwood.com>, Stephanie Nguyen <stephanie@redwood.com>; Joanna Erickson <joanna@talentpartners.io>
Date: Fri, 6 Mar 2026 12:22:00 -0800
Subject: Re: Biannual check-ins: manager notes & confidential org change

Thanks both — and Joanna, welcome to the thread.

Agreed on the sequence and the checklist — Ava, thanks for taking the lead on the draft talking points. Stephanie, please take a look at the redlines in org_change_draft.pdf and confirm if we need exec outreach before Tuesday's manager conversations. If not, we'll proceed with 1:1s on Wed 3/11 morning (A: Senior engineer at 9:30; B: Lead at 11:00) pending final HR approval.

I uploaded the revised notes to the Drive folder and shared it with you both: https://drive.google.com/drive/folders/1XyZ-ExampleLink

Final quick asks:
- Stephanie: confirm any approval blockers by EOD Monday 3/9.
- Joanna: if you can skim the talking points and provide 2–3 phrasing suggestions for the lead role by Monday, that would be great.

I’ll hold the 9:30 and 11:00 slots tentatively but will only send calendar notices once we have HR sign-off.

Ben Carter
Engineering Manager | Redwood Inference
ben@redwood.com | (415) 555-0123

- Compensation / level movement: if there is any comp adjustment above 6% we will need VP approval and finance sign-off. Any leveling changes that affect banding should be routed through People Systems for payroll alignment.

title: Recap & next steps — private VPC vs hyperscaler comparison
preview:
From: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com>
To: Elaine Summers <elaine@dataforge.com>
Cc: Marissa Cole <marissa@redwood.com>, Rishi Malhotra <rishi@redwood.com>
Date: 2026-03-03T14:10:00-07:00
Subject: Recap & next steps — private VPC vs hyperscaler comparison

Hi Elaine —

Thanks again for taking the time today. Quick recap from our discovery so we have the same set of priorities and proposed next steps. Please correct me if I missed anything:

Key requirements you emphasized:
- Air-gapped/on-prem option for regulated workloads (data cannot leave site)
- Customer-managed control plane or an isolated control plane option for VPC deployments
- KMS/HSM integration for keys and audit-level logs for tokenization and request metadata
- Predictable latency and throughput for real-time features (p95 target ~200ms for short prompts)
- Clear data flow: no PII leaves the environment unless explicitly allowed
- Comparison vs hyperscaler-managed endpoints for perf/cost — you want a short benchmark plan

Our proposed immediate next steps:
1) Share a short private deployment checklist (attached) and an MVP mutual-action-plan (attached).
2) Two PoC tracks we recommend:
   a) Private VPC PoC (customer VPC + Redwood private control plane in customer-managed VPC or peered subnet) — target: 2 weeks for basic network + KMS integration, 1 week of perf runs.
   b) Air-gapped on-prem PoC (isolated package + offline install) — target: 4–6 weeks due to logistics, HSM provisioning, and acceptance tests.
3) We need your network diagram (subnets, NAT/egress points) and a short list of compliance constraints (where audits will look) — network-zones.png was mentioned on the call.
4) Rishi (cc'd) will scope the install constraints for air-gapped and expected perf delta versus hosted/hyperscaler flows.

Attachments: Redwood-private-checklist.pdf, MVP-MAP.xlsx

I can set up a 30-min kick-off next week to align on PoC success criteria and access. What times on Tue/Wed work for you?

Thanks,
Ben Carter
Senior Sales Engineer | Redwood Inference
ben@redwood.com | +1 (415) 555-0124



From: Elaine Summers <elaine@dataforge.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com>
Cc: Marissa Cole <marissa@redwood.com>, Rishi Malhotra <rishi@redwood.com>
Date: 2026-03-03T16:45:00-07:00
Subject: Re: Recap & next steps — private VPC vs hyperscaler comparison

Hi Ben —

Thanks for the clear summary — this matches our notes. A couple of clarifying Qs before we kick off the PoC so we scope right:

1) Control plane: when you say "customer-managed control plane", do you mean the entire Redwood control plane runs inside our VPC (we own the compute/VMs), or is it a lightweight control proxy with Redwood-hosted management? We need the option where logs/metadata never flow to Redwood-hosted management.
2) For air-gapped installs: what are your constraints on HSMs (do you require a particular vendor) and installer size? We have strict procurement windows.
3) Regarding latency: our frontend calls are sub-100ms round trip to our backend; can you give a ballpark of what to expect for a pinned model vs hosted Redwood/Google endpoints for short prompts (<=64 tokens)?

I attached a basic network zones diagram we sketched during the call (network-zones.png). Happy to meet Tue 10–11am PT for the kick-off — that works for our infra lead.

Elaine Summers
Director of Platform Engineering | DataForge
elaine@dataforge.com



From: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com>
To: Rishi Malhotra <rishi@redwood.com>
Cc: Marissa Cole <marissa@redwood.com>
Date: 2026-03-04T09:20:00-07:00
Subject: Fwd: Recap & next steps — private VPC vs hyperscaler comparison

---------- Forwarded message ---------
From: Elaine Summers <elaine@dataforge.com>
Date: 2026-03-03T16:45:00-07:00
Subject: Re: Recap & next steps — private VPC vs hyperscaler comparison
To: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com>
Cc: Marissa Cole <marissa@redwood.com>, Rishi Malhotra <rishi@redwood.com>

Rishi — can you confirm the following for the PoC scoping:
- Precise control-plane deployment options (fully customer-hosted vs proxy) and whether any telemetry is sent back to Redwood by default.
- HSM vendor constraints and offline installer package size/steps for air-gapped installs.
- Ballpark latency differential for a pinned Llama-like 7B model on-prem vs Redwood hosted API and vs GCP/Vertex AI endpoints for short prompts (<=64 tokens).

If you can sketch the test steps we should run for a fair hyperscaler comparison that Elaine can run on their side (simple curl-based perf harness), that would be super helpful.

Thanks — Ben



From: Rishi Malhotra <rishi@redwood.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com>, Elaine Summers <elaine@dataforge.com>
Cc: Marissa Cole <marissa@redwood.com>
Date: 2026-03-04T11:05:00-07:00
Subject: Re: Recap & next steps — private VPC vs hyperscaler comparison

Hi all —

Quick answers and an initial test plan. High level first, then specifics.

Control plane options
- Option A (Fully customer-managed): We ship the Redwood control plane components as containers/VM images; you deploy into your VPC. No control-plane telemetry leaves your network by default. You retain admin access and we provide an offline license bundle. This meets the strictest compliance ask.
- Option B (Isolated proxy): Lightweight proxy lives in your VPC and talks to Redwood-managed control plane over mutual-TLS and customer-configured egress points. Telemetry can be turned off; metadata can be redacted. Faster time-to-ship but less isolation than Option A.

Air-gapped/HSM constraints
- We support PKCS#11-compatible HSMs (YubiHSM2, Thales, AWS CloudHSM in connected configs). For fully air-gapped installs we require a local HSM that can perform signing/unwrap ops; Redwood can integrate with vendor cert bundles.
- Installer: typical offline bundle for our air-gap package is ~1.2GB compressed; we provide checksums and a step-by-step install playbook.

Networking & ports (minimum)
- Control plane deploy: outbound TLS (443) only for Option B; Option A requires no outbound.
- Data-plane worker nodes (on-prem): internal port 9000-9010 for inter-worker RPC, 2379 if using etcd, and optional metrics port 9100. We provide a hardened checklist.

Latency/perf ballpark (short prompts <=64 tokens, p95)
- Redwood hosted API (our optimized runtime): ~70–120ms depending on model size and pooling.
- Customer-hosted (private VPC, same infra size as Redwood): ~80–140ms — expect ~10–20% delta depending on networking and instance types.
- On-prem air-gapped (typical enterprise rack, slightly older GPUs): ~120–200ms — expect ~25–40% delta vs hosted due to hardware generation and lack of our autoscaling pooling.
- Hyperscaler managed endpoint (Vertex/Azure ML): varies; with similar-sized instances expect comparable median but often higher p95 due to cold-starts and less optimized kernels — we'd expect 10–30% variance depending on model quantization and runtime.

Proposed fair benchmark test (we can provide a script):
1) Use a pinned model artifact and run 1k single-request samples with concurrency=1, prompt length=32 tokens, record median and p95.
2) Run a burst test: concurrency=8 for 5 minutes to observe tail latency and CPU/GPU utilization.
3) Capture token-level breakdown with logs (time-to-first-byte, decode time).

Timing and resourcing
- VPC PoC (Option B or A with infra): 2 weeks to validate networking, KMS, and perform perf runs.
- Air-gapped on-prem PoC: 4–6 weeks (procurement, HSM setup, acceptance tests).

Next steps I recommend for this week:
- Elaine: share the network diagram (done) and procurement calendar for HSMs.
- Ben/Marissa: propose kick-off time (Ben suggested Tue 10am PT).
- Redwood: we will prepare a short perf script (curl-based) and a checklist for the two PoC tracks.

I can attach an initial perf script and ports checklist to the Drive folder once Ben confirms.

Thanks,
Rishi Malhotra
Principal Systems Architect | Redwood Inference
rishi@redwood.com



From: Elaine Summers <elaine@dataforge.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com>
Cc: Rishi Malhotra <rishi@redwood.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa@redwood.com>
Date: 2026-03-04T15:30:00-07:00
Subject: Re: Recap & next steps — private VPC vs hyperscaler comparison

Ben/Rishi —

This is great and very helpful. Tue 2026-03-10 10:00am PT is confirmed for the kick-off (I'll send calendar invites with our infra lead and procurement on the call).

I'll also open a short ticket with procurement for HSM quotes; they expect vendor responses within 5 business days — will share timing.

Action items I have noted:
- Share procurement window and HSM vendor shortlist (Elaine) — due 2026-03-11
- Redwood to post perf script and ports checklist to Drive and HubSpot (Ben/Rishi) — target 2026-03-05
- Quick measurement: we'll run initial VPC perf script (concurrency=1) and share results in HubSpot deal notes (Elaine) — target 2026-03-17

HubSpot deal: https://app.hubspot.com/deal/1234567

Thanks — looking forward to the kick-off.

Subject: Streaming: responses end early with no error

Hi Ben,
Were seeing streaming responses sometimes *end early*. It looks like the stream finishes but the assistant output is cut off (we never get the rest of the tokens). No error on our side.

We attached a HAR capture of one case. request_id req_c41a...

Is there a known issue?

 Natalie

title: Mutual Action Plan & benchmark outcomes — NovaSearch embeddings + reranker
preview:
From: Alicia Gomez <agomez@novasearch.ai>
To: Rishi Malhotra <rishi_malhotra@redwood.com>
Cc: Kevin Liu <kevin.liu@novasearch.ai>, Marissa Cole <marissa_cole@redwood.com>
Date: Thu, 26 Feb 2026 17:08:00 -0800
Subject: MAP & performance evaluation for embeddings + reranker

Hi Rishi,

Thanks again for the demo earlier — NovaSearch is keen on validating Redwood for a production embeddings + reranker flow. We want to include a concrete MAP and a test plan that will sit inside the MAP so we can move quickly toward a PO and pilot.

High level asks:
- A mutual action plan with milestones/timeline for an evaluation and pilot (we're targeting a mid-March pilot start)
- A performance benchmarking plan for two endpoints: embeddings (128-d vectors) and reranker (top-k scoring) covering latency (p50/p95), throughput under load, TTFT, and concurrency behavior
- A quick-run set of initial load results if you have them from a smoke test

Can you share a MAP draft + evaluation plan and an initial results snapshot by EOD Friday our time? We want to review at our internal sync on Monday.

Thanks,
Alicia
Alicia Gomez
Head of Search Engineering, NovaSearch

--
Attachment: none on this first note


From: Rishi Malhotra <rishi_malhotra@redwood.com>
To: Alicia Gomez <agomez@novasearch.ai>
Cc: Ben Carter <ben_carter@redwood.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa_cole@redwood.com>
Date: Fri, 27 Feb 2026 09:30:00 -0800
Subject: Re: MAP & performance evaluation for embeddings + reranker

Alicia —

Appreciate the clear asks. Attached is a MAP draft (novasearch_map_v1.xlsx) plus a short bench plan (bench-plan-20260227.pdf) that maps tests to success criteria. High-level summary below; full details in the plan.

Proposed MAP milestones (high level):
- Mar 1: MAP finalized and internal kick-off
- Mar 4: Data handoff (sample corpus + metadata) from NovaSearch
- Mar 8–10: Run bench matrix (embedding index + reranker)
- Mar 12: Share detailed perf report + regression check
- Mar 15: Pilot sign-off decision and pilot environment allocation
- Mar 22: Pilot completion; OKR/PO evaluation meeting

Evaluation plan highlights (see bench-plan pdf for matrix):
- Endpoints: /v1/embeddings (batch sizes: 1, 8, 32), /v1/reranker (batch sizes: 1, 4, 16)
- Metrics: p50/p95 latency (ms), sustained throughput (req/s) at target concurrency, TTFT (time to first meaningful response in ms), CPU/GPU utilization, memory/KV cache hit rate
- Test harness: k6 for RPS patterns + a small Python harness to synthesize embedding vectors and reranker queries; dataset: 25k sample queries + 100k reference docs
- Success criteria (example): embeddings p95 < 60ms at 8 concurrent workers; reranker p95 < 180ms at 16 concurrent queries and end-to-end TTFT < 220ms for median requests

Initial smoke run: we kicked a lightweight load test (results below) — this was run on a single dedicated instance profile we typically propose for evaluation (g4dn-style equivalent). See Ben's note and attached CSV for the full run data.

Next steps:
1) Alicia: confirm the MAP milestones above or propose changes by Mon Mar 1
2) NovaSearch: share sample corpus access or a secure S3 link by Mar 4
3) We will run the full matrix Mar 8–10 and deliver the report on Mar 12

Thanks — attaching: novasearch_map_v1.xlsx, bench-plan-20260227.pdf

Cheers,
Rishi Malhotra
Enterprise Solutions Lead, Redwood Inference
---
Attachments: novasearch_map_v1.xlsx (MAP draft), bench-plan-20260227.pdf (bench matrix & success criteria)


From: Ben Carter <ben_carter@redwood.com>
To: Rishi Malhotra <rishi_malhotra@redwood.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa_cole@redwood.com>, Alicia Gomez <agomez@novasearch.ai>
Date: Mon, 2 Mar 2026 14:05:00 -0800
Subject: Re: MAP & performance evaluation for embeddings + reranker — initial run results

Alicia / team —

I ran the smoke / matrix subset we discussed. Full raw outputs are attached (bench-results-20260302.csv) and a handful of loadtest screenshots in loadtest-screenshots.zip. Below is a concise summary (single-instance, no caching primed vs. cache-primed where noted).

Embeddings endpoint (/v1/embeddings) — vector size 128, float16 quantization path:
- Single-request latency (batch=1): p50 = 9 ms, p95 = 28 ms
- Batch=8: p50 = 18 ms (per-request), p95 = 62 ms
- Sustained throughput (concurrency 32 simulated clients, TTL 60s): ~420 req/s (cache warm)
- TTFT (time to first vector bytes streamed): median 7 ms
- Notes: prefix/KV cache not applicable to embeddings; quantized model shows stable tail behavior in these runs.

Reranker endpoint (/v1/reranker) — top-k scoring across 50 candidates, cross-encoder style scoring:
- Single-request latency (batch=1): p50 = 34 ms, p95 = 130 ms
- Batch=4: p50 = 78 ms (per-request), p95 = 215 ms
- Sustained throughput (concurrency 16 simulated clients, TTL 60s): ~190 req/s (cache warm / KV cache hits ~55%)
- TTFT (time to first rank score): median 42 ms
- Notes: reranker is more sensitive to sequence length and candidate set size; we used 50 candidates at ~128 tokens avg per candidate for these runs.

Concurrency observations & failure modes:
- At concurrency above 48 for embeddings we see increased p95 tail due to scheduling and memory pressure; recommend pacing or sharding client-side requests into smaller concurrent pools (8–16) for predictable p95 SLAs.
- Reranker shows GPU memory pressure when candidate set > 80; in those cases we recommend either a larger instance profile or splitting the rerank into smaller chunks.

Comparative success vs target:
- Embeddings: meets goal of p95 < 60ms at 8 concurrent workers comfortably (observed p95 28ms at batch=1 and 62ms at batch=8 but cache-warmed throughput meets target).
- Reranker: slightly over the p95 target at higher batch sizes (observed p95 215ms at batch=4). We can mitigate via model variant with shorter sequence length, quantization adjustments, or bumping instance size.

See the attached CSV for per-step metrics (mean/std/p50/p95/err_rate) and the Drive doc for the full test matrix. Happy to walk through these numbers on a call tomorrow.

-Ben
Ben Carter
Senior Solutions Engineer, Redwood Inference
---
Attachments: bench-results-20260302.csv, loadtest-screenshots.zip

From: Rishi Malhotra <rishi_malhotra@redwood.com>

Date: Fri, 27 Feb 2026 09:30:00 -0800
Subject: Re: MAP & performance evaluation for embeddings + reranker

Alicia —

Appreciate the clear asks. Attached is a MAP draft (novasearch_map_v1.xlsx) plus a short bench plan (bench-plan-20260227.pdf) that maps tests to success criteria. High-level summary below; full details in the plan.

Proposed MAP milestones (high level):
- Mar 1: MAP finalized and internal kick-off
- Mar 4: Data handoff (sample corpus + metadata) from NovaSearch
- Mar 8–10: Run bench matrix (embedding index + reranker)
- Mar 12: Share detailed perf report + regression check
- Mar 15: Pilot sign-off decision and pilot environment allocation
- Mar 22: Pilot completion; OKR/PO evaluation meeting

Evaluation plan highlights (see bench-plan pdf for matrix):
- Endpoints: /v1/embeddings (batch sizes: 1, 8, 32), /v1/reranker (batch sizes: 1, 4, 16)
- Metrics: p50/p95 latency (ms), sustained throughput (req/s) at target concurrency, TTFT (time to first meaningful response in ms), CPU/GPU utilization, memory/KV cache hit rate
- Test harness: k6 for RPS patterns + a small Python harness to synthesize embedding vectors and reranker queries; dataset: 25k sample queries + 100k reference docs
- Success criteria (example): embeddings p95 < 60ms at 8 concurrent workers; reranker p95 < 180ms at 16 concurrent queries and end-to-end TTFT < 220ms for median requests

Initial smoke run: we kicked a lightweight load test (results below) — this was run on a single dedicated instance profile we typically propose for evaluation (g4dn-style equivalent). See Ben's note and attached CSV for the full run data.

Next steps:
1) Alicia: confirm the MAP milestones above or propose changes by Mon Mar 1
2) NovaSearch: share sample corpus access or a secure S3 link by Mar 4
3) We will run the full matrix Mar 8–10 and deliver the report on Mar 12

Thanks — attaching: novasearch_map_v1.xlsx, bench-plan-20260227.pdf

From: Ben Carter <ben_carter@redwood.com>
To: Rishi Malhotra <rishi_malhotra@redwood.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa_cole@redwood.com>, Alicia Gomez <agomez@novasearch.ai>
Date: Mon, 2 Mar 2026 14:05:00 -0800
Subject: Re: MAP & performance evaluation for embeddings + reranker — initial run results

Alicia / team —

I ran the smoke / matrix subset we discussed. Full raw outputs are attached (bench-results-20260302.csv) and a handful of loadtest screenshots in loadtest-screenshots.zip. Below is a concise summary (single-instance, no caching primed vs. cache-primed where noted).

Embeddings endpoint (/v1/embeddings) — vector size 128, float16 quantization path:
- Single-request latency (batch=1): p50 = 9 ms, p95 = 28 ms
- Batch=8: p50 = 18 ms (per-request), p95 = 62 ms
- Sustained throughput (concurrency 32 simulated clients, TTL 60s): ~420 req/s (cache warm)
- TTFT (time to first vector bytes streamed): median 7 ms
- Notes: prefix/KV cache not applicable to embeddings; quantized model shows stable tail behavior in these runs.

Reranker endpoint (/v1/reranker) — top-k scoring across 50 candidates, cross-encoder style scoring:
- Single-request latency (batch=1): p50 = 34 ms, p95 = 130 ms
- Batch=4: p50 = 78 ms (per-request), p95 = 215 ms
- Sustained throughput (concurrency 16 simulated clients, TTL 60s): ~190 req/s (cache warm / KV cache hits ~55%)
- TTFT (time to first rank score): median 42 ms
- Notes: reranker is more sensitive to sequence length and candidate set size; we used 50 candidates at ~128 tokens avg per candidate for these runs.

Concurrency observations & failure modes:
- At concurrency above 48 for embeddings we see increased p95 tail due to scheduling and memory pressure; recommend pacing or sharding client-side requests into smaller concurrent pools (8–16) for predictable p95 SLAs.
- Reranker shows GPU memory pressure when candidate set > 80; in those cases we recommend either a larger instance profile or splitting the rerank into smaller chunks.

Comparative success vs target:
- Embeddings: meets goal of p95 < 60ms at 8 concurrent workers comfortably (observed p95 28ms at batch=1 and 62ms at batch=8 but cache-warmed throughput meets target).
- Reranker: slightly over the p95 target at higher batch sizes (observed p95 215ms at batch=4). We can mitigate via model variant with shorter sequence length, quantization adjustments, or bumping instance size.

See the attached CSV for per-step metrics (mean/std/p50/p95/err_rate) and the Drive doc for the full test matrix. Happy to walk through these numbers on a call tomorrow.

-Ben
Ben Carter
Senior Solutions Engineer, Redwood Inference
---
Attachments: bench-results-20260302.csv, loadtest-screenshots.zip

title: Clarify subword/token differences & hosted ratecard for pilot
preview:
From: Elena Rossi <elena@brightretail.com>
To: Aisha Rahman <aisha@redwood.com>
Cc: BrightRetail Purchasing <procurement@brightretail.com>
Date: Thu, 26 Feb 2026 14:12:00 -0800
Subject: Question on token counting across models + request for hosted ratecard

Hi Aisha,

Thanks for the demo last week — the team liked Redwood's performance. Before we move to a pilot, procurement has two questions I hoped you could clarify:

1) Our engineering team noticed that different open models seem to tokenize similarly sized texts into different token counts (some use SentencePiece, others BPE). Can you confirm how Redwood bills tokens for the hosted API — is it based on the model's tokenizer, or a normalized token metric?

2) For the pilot (targeting ~5M tokens/day), could you share a baseline hosted ratecard and any standard pilot discounts for an initial 3-month trial? Procurement also asked whether you have an approach to predict billing variability given tokenizer differences — they want a conservative buffer estimate for the P.O.

I've attached a short sample of our anonymized corpus and a couple of sentences that highlighted the variance (attached as tokenization_sample_counts.csv). Procurement is looped in.

Appreciate any clarity so we can get a P.O. underway.

Best,
Elena
Head of Data Science
BrightRetail

Attachments: tokenization_sample_counts.csv (csv, 12KB)

From: Aisha Rahman <aisha@redwood.com>
To: Elena Rossi <elena@brightretail.com>
Cc: Vivek Kulkarni <vivek@redwood.com>, Marcus Lin <marcus@redwood.com>, BrightRetail Purchasing <procurement@brightretail.com>
Date: Thu, 26 Feb 2026 17:30:00 -08:00
Subject: Re: Question on token counting across models + request for hosted ratecard

Hi Elena — thanks for the clear ask and for the sample. Short answers up front: billed tokens are counted against the model's tokenizer (so different tokenizers produce different counts), and we provide both model-level token prices and a recommended normalization approach for budgeting.

Details and attachments: I've attached our standard hosted ratecard (BrightRetail_Ratecard_v1.pdf) and a short note that explains how tokenization differences show up in billing and a simple method to estimate a conservative daily buffer (Redwood_tokenization-notes.pdf). Key points:
- Redwood bills \"input + output\" tokens according to the model variant you route to (e.g., models based on SentencePiece vs BPE will yield different counts).
- For predictable budgeting we recommend deriving a per-model token multiplier using a representative sample (we ran this on your sample; see tokenization_sample_counts.csv). Use the 95th percentile token multiplier to provision a conservative buffer.
- For pilot pricing: standard hosted list is included in the ratecard. For a 3-month pilot at ~5M tokens/day, we typically offer a 20% pilot discount plus an optional monthly usage credit to offset onboarding evals.

Next steps: Vivek included here to add a couple of technical notes (tokenization edge cases, sequence length effects). If that all looks fine, I can share a draft SOW and a PO-ready summary for procurement.

Cheers,
Aisha Rahman
Senior Account Executive, Redwood Inference

Attachments: BrightRetail_Ratecard_v1.pdf (pdf, 95KB); Redwood_tokenization-notes.pdf (pdf, 18KB)

From: Vivek Kulkarni <vivek@redwood.com>
To: Elena Rossi <elena@brightretail.com>, Aisha Rahman <aisha@redwood.com>
Cc: Marcus Lin <marcus@redwood.com>, BrightRetail Purchasing <procurement@brightretail.com>
Date: Fri, 27 Feb 2026 09:05:00 -08:00
Subject: Re: Question on token counting across models + request for hosted ratecard

Hi all — quick technical addendum from infra/SE side:

1) Why counts differ: SentencePiece (unigram) and BPE split tokens differently; languages with many short subwords (e.g., compound words) will blow up counts on some tokenizers. This is entirely expected and not altered by Redwood — we bill actual tokenizer tokens the model consumes.

2) Practical recommendation: run a 10k-sample through the exact model variant you plan to use (we can host an eval run). Compute tokens_per_char and tokens_per-message distributions. For billing safety, provision at the 95th percentile tokens_per_message * expected messages per day.

3) Smoothing option to reduce billing volatility: route a portion of low-sensitivity traffic to a cheaper, compatible model variant (we can set routing policy by endpoint). That reduces average cost while maintaining quality for critical traffic.

4) For the pilot discount: we can commit to a 20% discount on hosted token pricing for 3 months and an additional one-time onboarding credit of $4,000 if you share a PO within 10 business days.

I'll run your sample through a comparable model we host and post results (I expect to share a short CSV with per-document token deltas).

— Vivek
Principal Solutions Engineer, Redwood Inference

1) Please send a one-page PO summary that shows the pilot term, committed daily tokens (5M/day), effective unit price after the 20% discount, and the onboarding credit noted. Procurement needs a single-line dollar figure to add to our PO system.

2) Any standard contract language around token inflation triggers? If our average token count during the pilot overshoots your estimate by >15% for two consecutive weeks, will you renegotiate rate or provide credits?

We reviewed the attached ratecard and token notes; Vivek, we appreciate the offer to run the sample — that will help. We'll aim to share an exec-signed PO the week after next if the PO summary looks right.

Best,
Elena

From: Dev Patel <dev.patel@redwood.ai>
To: Ellen Hayes <ellen.hayes@medisys.health>, Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>
Cc: Laura Bennett <laura.bennett@redwood.ai>
Date: 2026-03-03T09:45:00-07:00
Subject: Re: CMK operations questionnaire and SOC 2 follow-up

Hi Ellen — quick technical notes before the call (high level so procurement has context):

1) Encryption coverage
   - In transit: TLS 1.2+ for all endpoints; mutual TLS available for private deployments.
   - At rest: model artifacts, KV caches, checkpoints encrypted using envelope encryption where a DEK (data encryption key) is wrapped by a CMK.

2) CMK options we support
   - Redwood-managed KMS (default): we hold the CMK and provide full lifecycle management.
   - Customer-managed keys (BYOK): customer supplies or provisions CMKs in their cloud HSM and we reference via key ARN/ID. Supported HSM backends: AWS CloudHSM, Azure Dedicated HSM, and on-prem PKCS#11 HSMs (via gateway).

3) HSM-backed assurances and attestation artifacts
   - For AWS/Azure HSMs we can provide HSM vendor attestation copies and key-usage logs scoped to the customer project. For on-prem HSMs, we require network reachability to the HSM gateway or an outboard key wrap proxy.

4) Rotation & failover
   - Customer-initiated rotation: supported via API. We handle rewrapping of existing DEKs when provided with the new key metadata and brief downtime window (~1–2 minutes per node) for key swap; can be orchestrated for rolling update to avoid full outage.
   - Fallback model: if a CMK becomes unavailable we can automatically fall back to a pre-approved Redwood escrow key to preserve availability while you recover your CMK — requires contractual agreement and strict audit controls.

5) Audit logs & SIEM
   - KMS events (Encrypt/Decrypt/Wrap/Unwrap/CreateKey/RotateKey) are exported to customer log sinks with timestamp, principal, operation, key-id, and request id. Available formats: JSON, CSV. Retention/forwarding to SIEM can be automated.

6) SOC 2 evidence
   - Current status: Redwood is completing SOC 2 Type I scope expansion for inference in H1 2026; Type II is targeted in H2 2026. We can share interim control evidence and our control matrix prior to Type II. I’ll attach the control matrix to the follow-up meeting notes.

Happy to go deeper on any of the above on the call.

Cheers,
Dev Patel
Senior Infrastructure Engineer, Redwood Inference
dev.patel@redwood.ai

From: Camila Reyes <camila.reyes@redwoodinference.com>

Date: Mon, 02 Mar 2026 09:04:00 -08:00
Subject: Encryption / KMS follow-up and next steps

Hi Anita / Tom —

Thanks again for the demo and the great discussion this morning. Quick recap from our side focused on the encryption, key management, and data residency questions that came up, plus proposed next steps so we can keep momentum: 

What we covered (short):
- In-transit: TLS 1.2+ enforced between client and Redwood edge, mutual TLS available for Dedicated/Private deployments.
- At-rest: Model payloads and persisted KV caches are AES-256 encrypted. Customer-managed keys (BYOK) via KMS are supported for Private and Dedicated; for Hosted we offer envelope encryption and can integrate with customer KMS via a dedicated HSM-backed key proxy for enterprise contracts.
- Key rotation & access: Rotation supported via KMS integrations; RBAC and audit logs are available in Redwood Console (token-level tracing + key access logs).
- Data residency & retention: You can pin workloads to region-specific clusters (us-west/us-east/eu) and set retention policies per project. We persist logs for the time window you specify; we do not retain application-level PII longer than the retention configured.

Questions you flagged for follow-up:
1) Can BrightHealth bring-your-own-key (BYOK) for the hosted offering, or do we need a Private deployment?
2) What does the audit trail look like for key usage (who requested unwrap/usage)?
3) Is HSM-backed signing/attestation available for key import and root-of-trust?
4) Any required contract language or SOC2/pen testing evidence we should provide for procurement?

Proposed next steps:
- Rafael to send a short tech note with KMS integration options and an architecture diagram (attachment).
- Camila to share a SOC 2 summary and our standard BYOK SOW template for review.
- Schedule a 30-min follow-up with BrightHealth infra team next week to align on KMS path (I propose Wed 2026-03-10 10:00 PT; please confirm).

Attachments in this message: redwood_kms_integration_guide.pdf (short doc) and kms-arch-diagram.png

Thanks — please let me know if I missed anything. Looking forward to aligning on the KMS approach.

Best,

--
Camila Reyes
Senior Account Executive | Redwood Inference

Date: Mon, 02 Mar 2026 11:22:00 -06:00
Subject: Re: Encryption / KMS follow-up and next steps

Camila — thanks for the clear recap. A few clarifying items from our security and procurement teams: 

1) BYOK for hosted: our preference is to avoid a full Private deployment if possible. Is the HSM-backed key proxy you mentioned a managed integration where BrightHealth retains key material control? What are the limitations (latency, per-request cost, key rotation)?

2) Audit trail: we need to demonstrate to auditors that we can see a per-request unwrap/usage event including calling principal (service principal or user), timestamp, and operation type. Will Redwood supply logs suitable for ingestion into our SIEM (JSON lines preferred)?

3) HSM attestation: we need an attestation artifact proving hardware-backed key protection when keys are imported; if that requires Private deployment we need to know the delta vs Hosted.

4) Procurement: please attach the SOC2 report (redacted OK) and a short statement about physical access controls and key custodianship. Our legal will prepare standard DPIA questions as well.

Can Rafael weigh in on the BYOK proxy approach? Also — Wed 03/10 10:00 PT works for our infra lead.

Thanks,
Anita

--
Anita Shah
Head of IT Security | BrightHealth AI
anita.shah@brighthealthai.com | +1 (617) 555-2102


From: Rafael Mendes <rafael.mendes@redwoodinference.com>
To: Anita Shah <anita.shah@brighthealthai.com>, Camila Reyes <camila.reyes@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Markus Klein <markus.klein@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Mon, 02 Mar 2026 12:43:00 -08:00
Subject: Re: Encryption / KMS follow-up and next steps

Thanks Anita — quick technical clarifications below, happy to expand in the call next week.

BYOK for Hosted (HSM-backed proxy):
- Overview: For enterprise customers we offer a hosted BYOK flow where BrightHealth keeps root key material in your KMS/HSM. We exchange short-lived wrapping keys; an HSM-backed key proxy in our control plane performs envelope operations without ever storing plaintext root keys. This is similar to 'Bring Your Own Key' patterns used by major cloud vendors.
- Limitations: adds ~15-30ms median latency on token exchange paths for each new key unwrap operation (cached unwrap results in lower per-token latency). Per-request usage cost shows as a small operations surcharge in billing.
- Rotation: We support automated rotation via KMS APIs; rotations can be scheduled and rollback is supported for 7 days.

Audit trail & logs:
- We emit structured logs for key unwrap/usage events with fields: timestamp, operation, key_id (opaque), principal_id (internal service or token id), request_id. Logs are exportable as JSON-lines to customer S3/GCS or pushed to your SIEM via a secure forwarder.

HSM attestation:
- For Hosted + proxy: we can provide attestation proofs from our HSM vendor showing FIPS 140-2 Level 3 where applicable; importing customer keys into an HSM in our environment requires MOU and legal approvals.
- Private deployment delta: in Private, customer owns the HSM and key lifecycle fully — you get direct access to HSM attestation artifacts and physical custody controls.

Attachments: SOC2-Summary-2025.pdf (security summary), redwood_kms_integration_guide.pdf, kms-arch-diagram.png

Happy to walk through the SAML/mTLS auth flows during the call.

-Rafael

--
Rafael Mendes

title: Secure review packets & manager delivery plan
preview:
From: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com>
To: Monica Patel <monica_patel@redwood.com>, Laura Bennett <laura_bennett@redwood.com>, Vivek Kulkarni <vivek_kulkarni@redwood.com>, Carla Ruiz <carla@eastbridge.consulting>
Date: Tue, 10 Feb 2026 08:12:00 -0800
Subject: Secure review packet and delivery plan

Team —

As we discussed in last week's leadership sync, this year's annual review cycle needs an added privacy envelope for sensitive roles and a manager-delivery workflow that prevents bulk access to confidential scores. I sketched a lightweight operational plan and attached a manager packet (PDF) plus the redaction guidelines we agreed were necessary. High-level asks: 

- Confirm we can run the sealed packet process for ~45 privileged hires (list to follow).
- Ops: can we provision time-limited download links via SecureForms for manager packets (48h TTL, single-access)?
- Legal/Compliance: need an audit log export for every packet access event (timestamp, accessor, IP).
- Communications: I'll draft manager and IC comms once the delivery cadence is set.

Attachments: 2026-manager-packet-sealed.pdf (draft), redaction-guidelines-2026.docx

I copied Carla from Eastbridge — they're standing by to help set up the encrypted delivery if we want a vendor flow. Propose a 2-stage delivery: managers receive sealed packet + acknowledgment DocuSign, then limited HR sees full packet on escalation only.

Will take ownership on the comm draft; looking for quick thumbs-up on the ops constraints by EOD Friday.

—Ben Carter
Director, People Operations
Redwood Inference



From: Monica Patel <monica_patel@redwood.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com>, Laura Bennett <laura_bennett@redwood.com>, Vivek Kulkarni <vivek_kulkarni@redwood.com>, Carla Ruiz <carla@eastbridge.consulting>
Date: Thu, 12 Feb 2026 10:05:00 -0800
Subject: Re: Secure review packet and delivery plan

Ben — thanks for the outline. A few operational clarifications from my side: 

1) Manager delivery cadence: recommend two waves to reduce support load — Wave A (managers of sensitive roles) on Mar 18, Wave B (rest of managers) on Mar 25.
2) Packet contents: managers get a sealed packet with summary + performance evidence. Full notes remain in HR view only unless manager requests escalation (tracked).
3) Secure access: SecureForms TTL=48h single-access works; can we also add a short phone verification step for those with exec-level access?
4) Acknowledgment: DocuSign signature required that confirms manager reviewed but did not share. We should record the signature hash in the audit log.

I've annotated the attached redaction doc with a few examples (see page 3). If we get vendor support for the delivery API, I can run two test packets next week.

- Monica

Redwood Inference

title: Integration recap: SDK auth, routing, and rate-limit strategy
preview:
From: Aditya Rao <aditya@redwood.ai>
To: Sanjay Patel <sanjay.patel@finsigma.com>, Kate Liu <kate.liu@finsigma.com>
Cc: Ava Chen <ava@redwood.ai>, Neha Kapoor <neha@redwood.ai>
Date: Mon, 02 Mar 2026 15:05:00 +0000
Subject: Recap + next steps — SDK auth, routing, and rate-limit items

Hi Sanjay / Kate —

Great to meet today. Quick recap of what we covered (tech-focused items) and proposed next steps so we can get a short POC running next week:

What we covered
- SDK setup and auth: token rotation, short-lived API keys per environment, KMS integration for Private installs.
- Routing and model variants: edge routing for latency-sensitive endpoints; fallback to smaller/quantized models for high throughput.
- Structured output & tool-calling: function schema validation and deterministic output for payments/workflow calls.
- Embeddings vs reranker: embeddings for retrieval; reranker to re-rank top-N results when precision matters.
- Rate limiting / quotas / retries: per-API-key quotas, 429 handling, Retry-After, idempotency keys for mutating calls.

Immediate asks / next steps
1) Can you share a test service account + sample query patterns (tokens per request, qps, burst behavior)?
2) We'll send a short integration guide and SDK snippets (attached). Please try the token-auth example and confirm you can get a 200.
3) Engineering sync: proposed Wed 2026-03-04 at 15:00 PT — confirm availability.

Attachments: redwood_integration_guide.pdf (overview), rate_limit_matrix.csv (estimate table), sdk-snippets.zip (auth + backoff examples)

I'll loop in Ava on the engineering thread so she can answer detailed questions.

Thanks,
Aditya Rao
Enterprise Sales
Redwood Inference


From: Sanjay Patel <sanjay.patel@finsigma.com>
To: Aditya Rao <aditya@redwood.ai>
Cc: Kate Liu <kate.liu@finsigma.com>
Date: Mon, 02 Mar 2026 17:18:00 +0000
Subject: Re: Recap + next steps — SDK auth, routing, and rate-limit items

Aditya —

Thanks for the quick recap and for the attachments. Quick questions from our infra side before the POC:

1) Rate limits: do you expose both per-second and per-minute limits? We need to map to our gateway which applies burst limits per-connection. Also wondering about concurrent connection limits for streaming endpoints.
2) 429 handling: what headers do you return (Retry-After, X-Rate-Limit-Remaining, X-Rate-Limit-Reset)? We prefer graceful client-side retries with jitter.
3) Idempotency: for calls that create resources (e.g., tool-triggered payments), do you recommend an Idempotency-Key header? Any server-side dedupe windows/TTL?
4) Embeddings: for batch-upsert, what's the recommended batch size and how do you advise avoiding hitting transient rate limits during bulk imports?

I'll attach the traffic profile doc after I extract it. For the meeting Wed, 15:00 PT works for Kate and me.

—Sanjay
Sanjay Patel
Head of Platform Engineering
Finsigma


From: Ava Chen <ava@redwood.ai>
To: Sanjay Patel <sanjay.patel@finsigma.com>, Kate Liu <kate.liu@finsigma.com>
Cc: Aditya Rao <aditya@redwood.ai>, Neha Kapoor <neha@redwood.ai>
Date: Mon, 02 Mar 2026 20:35:00 +0000
Subject: Re: Recap + next steps — SDK auth, routing, and rate-limit items

Sanjay / Kate —

Thanks for the questions. I'm Ada (engineer on Integrations) — below are pragmatic recommendations you can implement in the POC and link back to for production. I'll paste a short pseudocode snippet for throttling/retries and call out headers we return.

1) Rate limits & quotas- We publish a rate-limit matrix (attached). We enforce both per-second (r/s) and per-minute (r/m) limits on the hosted SKU and per-customer quotas on Dedicated/Private. Typical hosted example: 50 r/s and 3000 r/m for medium tier; streaming endpoints have a concurrent-stream cap (default 10 concurrent streams per API key).
- Bursting: gateway allows short bursts; we track steady-state ROQ and enforce sliding-window counts.

2) Headers we return on throttled requests- Retry-After: seconds until next allowed request (if known)
- X-RateLimit-Limit: configured limit window size (e.g., 50)
- X-RateLimit-Remaining: tokens left in current window
- X-RateLimit-Reset: epoch seconds when window resets
- On 429 we also include a machine-readable body with reason and \"suggested_backoff_ms\" when applicable.

3) 429 handling recommendations- Always examine Retry-After when present. If absent, use exponential backoff with full jitter. Prefer capped backoff (max 60s) and an overall retry budget per operation (e.g., 6 attempts).

Suggested pseudocode (client-side)
- base = 200 ms
- max_backoff = 60000 ms
- for attempt in 1..max_attempts:
    sleep = min(max_backoff, base * 2^(attempt - 1))
    jitter = random(0, sleep)
    wait(jitter)
    resp = call_api()
    if resp.status == 200: return resp
    if resp.status == 429:
        if resp.headers['Retry-After']:
            wait_seconds = parse_retry_after(resp.headers['Retry-After'])
            wait(wait_seconds)
        else:
            continue // will backoff on next loop iteration
    if resp.is_transient_error(): continue else: raise

4) Idempotency and request IDs- For any non-idempotent/side-effecting calls (payments, job creation, emails, tool calls) set an \"Idempotency-Key\" header (UUID v4). Server-side dedupe window is 24 hours by default for hosted endpoints; we can configure shorter windows for high-volume endpoints on Dedicated. We also log the client-supplied idempotency key in audit trails so you can correlate retries.

5) Embeddings bulk import guidance- Use batching sized to your RPC and CPU profile. Typical safe batch: 64-256 vectors per request depending on vector size; larger batches are more efficient but can hit mem/latency thresholds. When doing large imports, use concurrency throttling (e.g., 8 concurrent workers with per-worker backoff) to avoid bursting the account quota. We provide a bulk-import helper in our SDK (see sdk-snippets.zip) that coordinates worker pools and exponential backoff with jitter.

6) Routing & structured output- If latency is critical, route to regionally-located endpoints and model variants with lower compute (quantized) for routine responses. For structured output and tool-calling, use our function-schema enforcement; SDK offers a verify_schema() helper that rejects ill-formed responses locally to avoid repeated calls.

Attachments: sdk-snippets.zip (examples: idempotency header usage, backoff helper, bulk-embed helper), rate_limit_matrix.csv

If helpful I can paste a short Node/Python snippet during the call.

—Ava Chen
Integrations Engineer, Redwood Inference

1) Rate limits & quotas- We publish a rate-limit matrix (attached). We enforce both per-second (r/s) and per-minute (r/m) limits on the hosted SKU and per-customer quotas on Dedicated/Private. Typical hosted example: 50 r/s and 3000 r/m for medium tier; streaming endpoints have a concurrent-stream cap (default 10 concurrent streams per API key).
- Bursting: gateway allows short bursts; we track steady-state ROQ and enforce sliding-window counts.

2) Headers we return on throttled requests- Retry-After: seconds until next allowed request (if known)
- X-RateLimit-Limit: configured limit window size (e.g., 50)
- X-RateLimit-Remaining: tokens left in current window
- X-RateLimit-Reset: epoch seconds when window resets
- On 429 we also include a machine-readable body with reason and \"suggested_backoff_ms\" when applicable.

3) 429 handling recommendations- Always examine Retry-After when present. If absent, use exponential backoff with full jitter. Prefer capped backoff (max 60s) and an overall retry budget per operation (e.g., 6 attempts).

Suggested pseudocode (client-side)
- base = 200 ms
- max_backoff = 60000 ms
- for attempt in 1..max_attempts:
    sleep = min(max_backoff, base * 2^(attempt - 1))
    jitter = random(0, sleep)
    wait(jitter)
    resp = call_api()
    if resp.status == 200: return resp
    if resp.status == 429:
        if resp.headers['Retry-After']:
            wait_seconds = parse_retry_after(resp.headers['Retry-After'])
            wait(wait_seconds)
        else:
            continue // will backoff on next loop iteration
    if resp.is_transient_error(): continue else: raise

4) Idempotency and request IDs- For any non-idempotent/side-effecting calls (payments, job creation, emails, tool calls) set an \"Idempotency-Key\" header (UUID v4). Server-side dedupe window is 24 hours by default for hosted endpoints; we can configure shorter windows for high-volume endpoints on Dedicated. We also log the client-supplied idempotency key in audit trails so you can correlate retries.

5) Embeddings bulk import guidance- Use batching sized to your RPC and CPU profile. Typical safe batch: 64-256 vectors per request depending on vector size; larger batches are more efficient but can hit mem/latency thresholds. When doing large imports, use concurrency throttling (e.g., 8 concurrent workers with per-worker backoff) to avoid bursting the account quota. We provide a bulk-import helper in our SDK (see sdk-snippets.zip) that coordinates worker pools and exponential backoff with jitter.

6) Routing & structured output- If latency is critical, route to regionally-located endpoints and model variants with lower compute (quantized) for routine responses. For structured output and tool-calling, use our function-schema enforcement; SDK offers a verify_schema() helper that rejects ill-formed responses locally to avoid repeated calls.

Attachments: sdk-snippets.zip (examples: idempotency header usage, backoff helper, bulk-embed helper), rate_limit_matrix.csv

If helpful I can paste a short Node/Python snippet during the call.

—Ava Chen
Integrations Engineer, Redwood Inference

Hi Sarah — thanks for the diagram and timeline. Quick technical notes to feed into the amendment and the MOP: 

- VPC peering & networking:
  - Primary control plane will remain in Redwood-managed control plane (leased control plane for Private); inference data plane nodes will be inside your VPC (customer-managed subnet).
  - Required ports: 443 (TLS) for control/management, 50051 (gRPC) optional for high-throughput internal calls; we recommend NAT for outbound telemetry only.
  - CIDR ranges: we’ll need a /26 for the initial cluster and an additional /26 for the new on-prem cluster peering block.

- Isolation model:
  - Multi-cluster topology: Hosted control plane + two isolated inference clusters (EWR on-prem, primary in IAD). Traffic routing can be pinned by policy (latency first for NY customers).
  - KV cache separation: each cluster gets dedicated KV cache and disk-backed checkpointing per retention policy.

- Capacity & SLA
  - Proposed reserved capacity: +10% token throughput over current commit (we’ll propose concrete vGPU counts after you confirm expected QPS and concurrency).
  - Cutover playbook: staged canary on a mirror traffic slice, promote after 48h stability and perf acceptance.

Can you confirm expected peak QPS and typical request size (avg tokens per request)? That’ll let us size GPUs and IOPS. I’ll assemble a short MOP and a checklist we can hand to your infra team.

—Marcus

marcus.lin@redwood.com
