title: Storica Labs — Azure private offer: rev-share & term exceptions (exec approval needed)
preview:
From: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com>
To: Laura Bennett <laura@redwood.com>, Vivek Kulkarni <vivek@redwood.com>, Karthik Iyer <karthik@redwood.com>, Elena Torres <elena@storiclabs.com>, Sasha Ivanov <sasha@aws-partners.com>
Date: Wed, 25 Feb 2026 16:14:00 -0500
Subject: Storica Labs — Azure private offer: rev-share & term exceptions (exec approval needed)

Team — quick summary and ask. Storica Labs (AI analytics for retail) is ready to commit to a 36-month private offer on Azure but they have three requests that are outside our standard private-offer template:

1) Rev-share: they are asking for an 18% rev-share to AWS Marketplace (pass-through to partner).
2) Term: customer wants a 36-month firm term with quarterly true-ups (we typically do 12–24 months for private offers).
3) Contract terms: they want liability cap and termination-for-convenience language aligned to their enterprise template (they've redlined vendor-favorable language).

Deal economics: list price for the commitment is $1.2M ARR; their requested up-front discount brings Redwood ARR to $900k annualized (25% discount) with the 18% rev-share to marketplace. Attached is the revshare_calc.xlsx that breaks down unit economics vs our baseline.

I'm escalating for executive sign-off on the non-standard combo: 25% discount + 18% rev-share + 36-month firm term. Legal and Finance have flagged the term length and termination language as the main risks.

Proposed quick asks:
- Vivek / Laura: can we approve up to 25% discount conditioned on signed mutual MOP and finance sign-off on revenue recognition?
- If exec approves discount ceiling, legal to provide one-page amendments accepting limited term exception and revised liability cap.

Attachments: Storica_Mutual_Action_Plan.pdf (MAP), Azure_Private_Offer_Draft.docx, revshare_calc.xlsx

Thanks — Ben
Ben Carter
Enterprise Sales | Redwood Inference
ben@redwood.com



From: Laura Bennett <laura@redwood.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com>, Vivek Kulkarni <vivek@redwood.com>
Cc: Karthik Iyer <karthik@redwood.com>, Elena Torres <elena@storiclabs.com>, Sasha Ivanov <sasha@aws-partners.com>
Date: Thu, 26 Feb 2026 09:05:00 -0800
Subject: Re: Storica Labs — Azure private offer: rev-share & term exceptions (exec approval needed)

Thanks for the clear write-up, Ben. A couple quick notes from GTM/AM perspective:

- Revenue impact: if we give 25% and 18% rev-share, our net seat price versus typical hosted route becomes marginal — need finance backstop.
- Risk: 36 months locks capacity planning and complicates future pricing changes; suggest we ask for an annual price reset clause tied to CPI or published list changes.

I agree this needs exec sign-off. Vivek — can you weigh in on whether up to 20–25% total discount is allowable for a strategic customer on Azure? Also legal, please confirm if we can accept a 36-month term with quarterly true-ups while preserving a standard liability cap (we can propose a mid-tier cap).

If exec approves in principle, I'll coordinate with Elena and Sasha to get the private-offer draft into Azure marketplace with the requested rev-share line item so that AWS can process the private offer flow.

- Laura
Laura Bennett
Head of Sales Operations | Redwood Inference


From: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com>
To: Laura Bennett <laura@redwood.com>, Vivek Kulkarni <vivek@redwood.com>
Cc: Karthik Iyer <karthik@redwood.com>, Elena Torres <elena@storiclabs.com>, Sasha Ivanov <sasha@aws-partners.com>, Procure Team <procure@storiclabs.com>
Date: Fri, 27 Feb 2026 11:22:00 -0800
Subject: Re: Storica Labs — Azure private offer: rev-share & term exceptions (exec approval needed)

Quick update with requested artifacts attached. I pushed the private-offer draft to the shared drive link and uploaded the MAP. Key supporting notes:

- revshare_calc.xlsx: shows net take to Redwood after rev-share and discounts across 36 months, includes churn assumptions and uplift scenarios.
- Azure_Private_Offer_Draft.docx: their procurement redlines are highlighted (notable asks: termination for convenience with 90-day notice, 36-month firm term, and elimination of quarterly price resets).
- Storica_Mutual_Action_Plan.pdf: exec sponsor, rollout milestones, and an expected go-live date of 2026-06-15.

Elena (cc): can procurement confirm that the 36-month term is a must-have from their side, or would Storica accept a 24 + 12 optional extension?

Ben


From: Vivek Kulkarni <vivek@redwood.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com>, Laura Bennett <laura@redwood.com>, Karthik Iyer <karthik@redwood.com>
Cc: Elena Torres <elena@storiclabs.com>, Sasha Ivanov <sasha@aws-partners.com>
Date: Sun, 01 Mar 2026 14:40:00 -0800
Subject: Re: Storica Labs — Azure private offer: rev-share & term exceptions (exec approval needed)

Ben / Laura — reviewed the package. Appreciate the work getting this far. High-level decision: I can approve a commercial package that includes up to a 25% effective discount if the following guardrails are satisfied prior to signing:

1) Finance sign-off: CFO or delegated finance lead must confirm revenue recognition and gross-to-net treatment for 36-month commitments with quarterly true-ups.
2) Rev-share ceiling: accept 18% to AWS only if Marketing/Partner ops confirms no additional platform fees; legal to include a clause ensuring the rev-share applies only to Marketplace-managed transactions.
3) Term/termination: we will accept 36 months only if we get (a) an annual price reset tied to a <3% floor or index OR (b) an explicit opt-out at 24 months conditioned on 90 days' notice and an early-termination settlement for Redwood to recover minimum committed revenue.
4) Liability cap: legal to propose a mid-tier cap (2x fees paid in 12 months) with carve-outs for data breaches.

Operational asks: set up a 30-minute sync with Finance (cc Karthik) and Legal to get the exact language. If Finance signs off on the revenue schedule and Legal agrees to the limited term carve, I approve routing this to AWS for private offer creation.

Please move this to "Exec approval" queue and include the specific doc links to the redlines to speed review.

- Vivek
Chief Revenue Officer | Redwood Inference


From: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com>
To: Vivek Kulkarni <vivek@redwood.com>, Laura Bennett <laura@redwood.com>, Karthik Iyer <karthik@redwood.com>, Elena Torres <elena@storiclabs.com>, Sasha Ivanov <sasha@aws-partners.com>
Date: Mon, 02 Mar 2026 09:03:00 -0800
Subject: Re: Storica Labs — Azure private offer: rev-share & term exceptions (exec approval needed)

Thanks, Vivek — very helpful. Next steps from my side:

1) Schedule 30-min sync with Finance + Legal (proposed: Tue 03 Mar 10:00 PT). Karthik / Laura, please confirm availability.
2) I'll ask Legal to draft the mid-tier liability cap amendment and the 24-month opt-out language as outlined.
3) Elena / Sasha, please stand by while we finalize internal sign-offs; we will aim to have a final private offer to AWS by the end of next week (2026-03-09).

Open items for the sync: finance confirmation on revenue recognition schedule, final rev-share language (AWS-specific), and the precise opt-out mechanics. Ben will bring the MAP and the revshare_calc.xlsx to the call.

Elena — appreciate your patience, and happy to run the call with procurement on.

Ben

title: RetailCo checkout failures — cascading timeouts after DNS surge
preview:
From: Lisa Tran <lisa.tran@retailco.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben_carter@redwood.com>
Date: Fri, 27 Feb 2026 08:12:00 -0800
Subject: Checkout failing — 5xx/timeout spike impacting payments

Hi Ben,

We're seeing a sudden spike in checkout failures starting ~07:55 PST. Customers get intermittent 5xx and long timeouts (20s+); our front-end is retrying aggressively which is compounding traffic. Cart abandonment has climbed sharply.

Errors from our logs (sample):
- POST /v1/generate -> 502 Bad Gateway (ALB)
- downstream timeouts to api.redwood.example: context deadline exceeded

I've attached a short log excerpt (error_log_0227.txt). Our ops team also saw elevated DNS lookup times right before the errors started. Can you confirm whether you see a regional issue or any throttling on your side? We have a large promo running and this is customer-facing — need urgent help.

Lisa
Lisa Tran
Head of Platform, RetailCo
api-ops@retailco.com

Attachment: error_log_0227.txt (text, 12KB)

From: Ben Carter <ben_carter@redwood.com>
To: Lisa Tran <lisa.tran@retailco.com>
Cc: Marissa Cole <marissa_cole@redwood.com>, Sean Gallagher <sean_gallagher@redwood.com>
Date: Fri, 27 Feb 2026 08:43:00 -0800
Subject: Re: Checkout failing — 5xx/timeout spike impacting payments

Thanks Lisa — sorry you're seeing this. We are treating as high priority.

Initial notes / immediate asks:
- Can you confirm the first observed timestamp (you listed ~07:55 PST) and whether errors are concentrated in specific client regions?
- Please share any request IDs you can (we can trace from our edge).
- We'll stand up a quick triage call if you can join. I'm looping in Sean (Ops) and Marissa (CS lead).

Proposed immediate actions from Redwood:
1) We will pull request traces and edge logs for 07:45–08:30 PST and check recent routing changes.
2) Sean will check whether the 429/5xx surge correlates with upstream DNS latency or AWS health events.
3) If needed, we can temporarily route RetailCo traffic to a dedicated capacity pool to avoid noisy neighbor impacts.

Can you hold for a 15-min sync? I'll be available.

—
Ben Carter
Senior Account Executive, Redwood Inference
ben_carter@redwood.com
Phone: +1 415-555-0142

Attachment: grafana_snapshot_checkout_0227.png (image, 240KB)

From: Sean Gallagher <sean_gallagher@redwood.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben_carter@redwood.com>, Lisa Tran <lisa.tran@retailco.com>
Cc: Marissa Cole <marissa_cole@redwood.com>
Date: Fri, 27 Feb 2026 09:05:00 -0800
Subject: Re: Checkout failing — checkout 5xx/429 spike triage notes

On Fri, Feb 27, 2026 at 8:43 AM Ben Carter <ben_carter@redwood.com> wrote:
> Thanks Lisa — sorry you're seeing this. We are treating as high priority.

Quick triage summary from infra:
- We are observing a ~6–9% increase in 429s and 5xx starting 07:52 PST tied to RetailCo's token stream. Peak at 08:01 PST.
- Upstream signs point to elevated DNS resolution latency for api.redwood.example observed from our edge NPM probes (median DNS latency jumped from 18ms -> 450ms for a short window).
- AWS ALB returned 503s when the backend timed out; individual model servers reported blocked/waiting goroutines — likely retry amplification exhaustion on the node.

Short-term mitigations applied now:
- Reduced client-side retry window for RetailCo via feature-flag (if your frontend uses our client SDK) to avoid amplification.
- Failed over a subset of RetailCo traffic to our us-west dedicated pool to reduce likelihood of noisy-neighbor effects.
- Opened an AWS support case (ID: 1234567890) referencing elevated ALB 503s in us-west-2.

Next steps:
- We'll correlate RetailCo request IDs with our trace logs and share a timeline.
- If DNS latency persists, we recommend RetailCo switch to a secondary resolver (we'll provide config) temporarily.

Sean Gallagher
SRE, Redwood Inference
sean_gallagher@redwood.com

Related dashboard: https://grafana.redwood.example/d/checkout-errors

Attachment: slo_rollforward_plan.pdf (PDF, 48KB)

From: Marissa Cole <marissa_cole@redwood.com>
To: Hannah Schmitt <hannah_schmitt@redwood.com>, Jonas Weber <jonas_weber@redwood.com>, Ben Carter <ben_carter@redwood.com>, Sean Gallagher <sean_gallagher@redwood.com>
Date: Fri, 27 Feb 2026 09:40:00 -0800
Subject: RetailCo — escalation request and customer impact summary

Hannah / Jonas — looping you in for executive visibility. RetailCo is experiencing a customer-facing outage window for checkout flows tied to elevated 5xx/429. Impact summary:
- Time window: 07:52–08:20 PST (ongoing intermittent)
- Scope: approx 7% of token generation requests failing; measurable increase in cart abandonments in US west customers
- Root cause (initial): retry amplification + transient DNS lookup spikes upstream of our edge; AWS ALB timed out on several backend nodes.

Customer asks:
1) Urgent timeline of corrective actions and ETA for resolution
2) Whether we can commit to a temporary credit for impacted transactions (RetailCo expects a goodwill gesture)
3) One pager for their leadership on what happened and what we're doing to prevent recurrence

Operational asks for Redwood:
- Confirm whether a full traffic shift to Dedicated (RetailCo-dedicated pool) is required — if yes, we need approval to spin up capacity immediately.
- Approve a customer-facing note and a short executive summary (15–30 mins) for RetailCo leadership.

I'll keep this thread updated with the timeline.

—
Marissa Cole
Customer Success Manager, Redwood Inference
marissa_cole@redwood.com

(On Fri, Feb 27, 2026 at 9:05 AM Sean Gallagher <sean_gallagher@redwood.com> wrote:
> Quick triage summary from infra: ... )

From: Hannah Schmitt <hannah_schmitt@redwood.com>
To: Marissa Cole <marissa_cole@redwood.com>, Ben Carter <ben_carter@redwood.com>, Sean Gallagher <sean_gallagher@redwood.com>, Jonas Weber <jonas_weber@redwood.com>
Date: Fri, 27 Feb 2026 10:15:00 -0800
Subject: Re: RetailCo — escalation request and customer impact summary

Thanks for the quick triage and the clear summary. Actions taken / in progress: 
- AWS case created and I engaged our TAM; AWS is investigating ALB health events in us-west-2 (case 1234567890).
- Contacted DNS provider (primary resolver) and they confirmed a regional peering disruption from 07:50–08:05 PST; they are providing packet captures.
- We have shifted 40% of RetailCo traffic to a pre-warmed dedicated pool in us-west; SREs will continue to shift more if error rate does not fall.
- Reduced client retry policy is live for RetailCo (as Sean rolled out).

Recommended customer comms: short status update to RetailCo leadership acknowledging impact, summary of actions, and expected next update in 60 minutes. We should propose a goodwill credit; I'll defer to Finance on amount but recommend offering a targeted credit for impacted transactions.

I'll draft the one-pager and share within the hour. Please add any gaps.

Hannah Schmitt
VP, Product Operations, Redwood Inference
hannah_schmitt@redwood.com

title: Azure private offer: commission waterfall + SKU bundling clarification
preview:
From: Alex Morgan <alex.morgan@cloudnova.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
Cc: procurement@cloudnova.com
Date: Mon, 23 Feb 2026 09:14:00 -0800
Subject: Azure private offer: reseller commission structure

Hi Ben,

Thanks again for the walkthrough yesterday. Procurement asked me to follow up with a few concrete asks so we can move the PO through our legal/finance stack next week: 

1) We need a clear waterfall for the reseller commission if CloudNova resells through our enterprise channels. We typically expect an initial up-front rebate + ongoing percentage — please confirm Redwood's standard approach for Azure private offers.

2) We're proposing a bundled SKU: Base inference (v1.1) + Priority Support + 50k embedding units/month. Does that map to a single private-offer SKU in the marketplace, or do you recommend separate SKUs with a volume pass-through?

3) Any list-price guidance for public marketplace listing after the private offer window closes (so we can model TCO and renewal pricing).

If you can send a short term sheet or example calculation (per-month and first-year ARR impact) that would be ideal. We're on a tight cadence — procurement review on Thursday.

Appreciate it,
Alex
Alex Morgan
Head of Strategic Partnerships | CloudNova
procurement@cloudnova.com

---
Attachments: none


From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
To: Alex Morgan <alex.morgan@cloudnova.com>
Cc: Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwood.com>, Laura Bennett <laura.bennett@redwood.com>
Date: Mon, 23 Feb 2026 11:02:00 -08:00
Subject: Re: Azure private offer: reseller commission structure

Alex — thanks for the clear list. Putting the short answers below and attaching a draft term sheet and SKU mapping workbook for clarity.

1) Commission waterfall: Redwood typically supports a two-part structure for marketplace private offers: (A) an upfront rebate (one-time credit applied after first invoice) to support GTM investment; plus (B) a recurring resale margin defined as a percent of invoice value. For Azure private offers specifically we can express the rebate as a % of committed ARR (e.g., 5–15% tiered by commit), and the recurring margin as a percent of listed unit price.

Example calculation (very high level):
- List unit price per 1k generation tokens: $0.50
- Reseller recurring margin: 12% -> CloudNova receives $0.06 per unit sold
- Upfront rebate: 8% of first-year committed spend -> applied as credit across first three invoices

2) Bundled SKU approach: Marketplace supports a single private-offer SKU that maps to a bundle (we typically publish a single bundle SKU for the private offer). However, for longer-term analytics and TCO clarity we recommend mapping the components in the SKU mapping workbook (attached) so renewals can optionally move to componentized billing. If CloudNova wants to report usage separately to customers, separate SKUs are possible but increase reconciliation.

3) List-price guidance after private window: we generally recommend anchoring public list price ~10–20% above the effective post-rebate price to preserve margin for Redwood and reseller commissions. We can provide a suggested public list price by SKU once we align on the bundle composition and expected commit.

I attached: Azure_Private_Offer_Term_Sheet_v2.pdf (term sheet w/ waterfall example) and Marketplace_SKU_Mapping.xlsx (bundle->component mapping + sample price model).

Karthik/Laur a — I copied you for the reseller % confirmation and any Marketplace constraints (e.g., commission caps, pass-through fields). Can you confirm the maximum allowed reseller recurring percent for Azure private offers and whether we need to split credits across partner tiers?

Next steps for us: I can update the term sheet with the exact percentages you prefer and send a final DocuSign link. Is a Thursday signature window still ok for procurement?

Best,
Ben Carter
Senior Sales Manager | Redwood Inference
ben.carter@redwood.com | +1 (415) 555-0192

Attachments: Azure_Private_Offer_Term_Sheet_v2.pdf, Marketplace_SKU_Mapping.xlsx


From: Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwood.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>, Alex Morgan <alex.morgan@cloudnova.com>
Cc: Laura Bennett <laura.bennett@redwood.com>
Date: Tue, 24 Feb 2026 08:30:00 -08:00
Subject: Re: Azure private offer: reseller commission structure

Ben/Alex — quick technical/ops notes from Marketplace side: 

- Azure private offers allow an "agent" (reseller) margin field up to 20% in the offer metadata. Anything above that requires an exception and engineering/workflow changes for pass-through billing. So we should target <=20% unless legal/finance want to escalate.

- Upfront rebates are handled as one-off credits tied to the customer invoice (not to partner payouts) — Finance will reconcile and then pay partner commissions per the waterfall in the term sheet. That means we need clear invoice triggers (e.g., credit applied after first payment) so CloudNova can forecast cashflow.

- SKU bundling: public SKU will be the bundle; Marketplace allows mapping of bundle -> component SKUs in metadata but doesn’t expose per-component usage to the customer by default. If CloudNova requires line-item visibility we can enable it, but that adds a reconciliation step at renewal.

Ben: from an operational risk perspective, if CloudNova wants an 18–20% recurring reseller margin plus a mid-single-digit upfront rebate we should get LA/Finance signoff (Laura copied) because that meaningfully reduces Redwood's take-rate in year one.

Karthik
Principal Partnerships Manager | Redwood Inference


From: Laura Bennett <laura.bennett@redwood.com>
To: Alex Morgan <alex.morgan@cloudnova.com>, Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>, Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwood.com>
Date: Wed, 25 Feb 2026 11:03:00 -08:00
Subject: Re: Azure private offer: reseller commission structure

Alex — thanks for the patience. We reviewed the numbers and can confirm the following proposal for CloudNova (summary):

- Upfront rebate: 7% of first-year committed ARR, paid as a credit applied across the first three invoices after activation.
- Recurring reseller margin: 14% of invoice value (falls within Marketplace agent cap).
- Bundle handling: We'll list a single private-offer bundle SKU in Azure marketplace. Redwood will maintain a component mapping internally for reporting; CloudNova can request the per-component export quarterly.
- Public list pricing: we suggest starting public list at 12% higher than the effective price after rebate to preserve renewal flexibility.

Attachments: I am attaching an updated term sheet (Azure_Private_Offer_Term_Sheet_v2.pdf — redlined) and a completed SKU mapping workbook (Marketplace_SKU_Mapping.xlsx). I will also generate a DocuSign for the PO and the partner commission addendum once Ben confirms the final commit numbers.

Next steps:

Subject: Azure private offer: reseller commission structure

Hi Ben,

Thanks again for the walkthrough yesterday. Procurement asked me to follow up with a few concrete asks so we can move the PO through our legal/finance stack next week: 

1) We need a clear waterfall for the reseller commission if CloudNova resells through our enterprise channels. We typically expect an initial up-front rebate + ongoing percentage — please confirm Redwood's standard approach for Azure private offers.

2) We're proposing a bundled SKU: Base inference (v1.1) + Priority Support + 50k embedding units/month. Does that map to a single private-offer SKU in the marketplace, or do you recommend separate SKUs with a volume pass-through?

3) Any list-price guidance for public marketplace listing after the private offer window closes (so we can model TCO and renewal pricing).

If you can send a short term sheet or example calculation (per-month and first-year ARR impact) that would be ideal. We're on a tight cadence — procurement review on Thursday.

Appreciate it,
Alex
Alex Morgan
Head of Strategic Partnerships | CloudNova
procurement@cloudnova.com

Attachments: none


From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
To: Alex Morgan <alex.morgan@cloudnova.com>
Cc: Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwood.com>, Laura Bennett <laura.bennett@redwood.com>
Date: Mon, 23 Feb 2026 11:02:00 -08:00
Subject: Re: Azure private offer: reseller commission structure

Alex — thanks for the clear list. Putting the short answers below and attaching a draft term sheet and SKU mapping workbook for clarity.

1) Commission waterfall: Redwood typically supports a two-part structure for marketplace private offers: (A) an upfront rebate (one-time credit applied after first invoice) to support GTM investment; plus (B) a recurring resale margin defined as a percent of invoice value. For Azure private offers specifically we can express the rebate as a % of committed ARR (e.g., 5–15% tiered by commit), and the recurring margin as a percent of listed unit price.

Example calculation (very high level):
- List unit price per 1k generation tokens: $0.50
- Reseller recurring margin: 12% -> CloudNova receives $0.06 per unit sold
- Upfront rebate: 8% of first-year committed spend -> applied as credit across first three invoices

2) Bundled SKU approach: Marketplace supports a single private-offer SKU that maps to a bundle (we typically publish a single bundle SKU for the private offer). However, for longer-term analytics and TCO clarity we recommend mapping the components in the SKU mapping workbook (attached) so renewals can optionally move to componentized billing. If CloudNova wants to report usage separately to customers, separate SKUs are possible but increase reconciliation.

3) List-price guidance after private window: we generally recommend anchoring public list price ~10–20% above the effective post-rebate price to preserve margin for Redwood and reseller commissions. We can provide a suggested public list price by SKU once we align on the bundle composition and expected commit.

I attached: Azure_Private_Offer_Term_Sheet_v2.pdf (term sheet w/ waterfall example) and Marketplace_SKU_Mapping.xlsx (bundle->component mapping + sample price model).

Karthik/Laur a — I copied you for the reseller % confirmation and any Marketplace constraints (e.g., commission caps, pass-through fields). Can you confirm the maximum allowed reseller recurring percent for Azure private offers and whether we need to split credits across partner tiers?

Next steps for us: I can update the term sheet with the exact percentages you prefer and send a final DocuSign link. Is a Thursday signature window still ok for procurement?

Best,
Ben Carter
Senior Sales Manager | Redwood Inference
ben.carter@redwood.com | +1 (415) 555-0192

Attachments: Azure_Private_Offer_Term_Sheet_v2.pdf, Marketplace_SKU_Mapping.xlsx


From: Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwood.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>, Alex Morgan <alex.morgan@cloudnova.com>
Cc: Laura Bennett <laura.bennett@redwood.com>
Date: Tue, 24 Feb 2026 08:30:00 -08:00
Subject: Re: Azure private offer: reseller commission structure

Ben/Alex — quick technical/ops notes from Marketplace side: 

- Azure private offers allow an "agent" (reseller) margin field up to 20% in the offer metadata. Anything above that requires an exception and engineering/workflow changes for pass-through billing. So we should target <=20% unless legal/finance want to escalate.

- Upfront rebates are handled as one-off credits tied to the customer invoice (not to partner payouts) — Finance will reconcile and then pay partner commissions per the waterfall in the term sheet. That means we need clear invoice triggers (e.g., credit applied after first payment) so CloudNova can forecast cashflow.

- SKU bundling: public SKU will be the bundle; Marketplace allows mapping of bundle -> component SKUs in metadata but doesn’t expose per-component usage to the customer by default. If CloudNova requires line-item visibility we can enable it, but that adds a reconciliation step at renewal.

Ben: from an operational risk perspective, if CloudNova wants an 18–20% recurring reseller margin plus a mid-single-digit upfront rebate we should get LA/Finance signoff (Laura copied) because that meaningfully reduces Redwood's take-rate in year one.

Karthik
Principal Partnerships Manager | Redwood Inference

Alex — thanks for the patience. We reviewed the numbers and can confirm the following proposal for CloudNova (summary):

- Upfront rebate: 7% of first-year committed ARR, paid as a credit applied across the first three invoices after activation.
- Recurring reseller margin: 14% of invoice value (falls within Marketplace agent cap).
- Bundle handling: We'll list a single private-offer bundle SKU in Azure marketplace. Redwood will maintain a component mapping internally for reporting; CloudNova can request the per-component export quarterly.
- Public list pricing: we suggest starting public list at 12% higher than the effective price after rebate to preserve renewal flexibility.

Attachments: I am attaching an updated term sheet (Azure_Private_Offer_Term_Sheet_v2.pdf — redlined) and a completed SKU mapping workbook (Marketplace_SKU_Mapping.xlsx). I will also generate a DocuSign for the PO and the partner commission addendum once Ben confirms the final commit numbers.

title: Subprocessor redline synchronization & exception runbook
preview:
From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>
To: Stacey Hart <stacey.hart@edgenova.com>
Cc: Laura Bennett <laura.bennett@redwood.ai>, Neha Kapoor <neha.kapoor@redwood.ai>, Kimberly Park <kimberly.park@redwood.ai>
Date: Tue, 17 Feb 2026 09:12:00 -0800
Subject: Subprocessor redline synchronization & exception runbook

Stacey —

Quick follow from our call earlier this week. EdgeNova's proposed change to Section 4.2 (subprocessor flow-down) adds two named downstream vendors: LogStream Inc. and TelemetryOps. Before we push this into the MSA/order form, we need to: 

- Confirm whether LogStream handles any persistent customer content (LogStream noted it retains logs for 30 days in their EU cluster).
- Align DPA language to ensure our customer data residency obligations aren't broken by the cascade.
- Capture a short-lived exception path if LogStream requires an alternative SSA (service-specific addendum) for logging.

Attached is the subprocessor list you shared (EdgeNova_Subproc_List.xlsx). Can EdgeNova legal share your redline version of the MSA (tracked changes) and flag any subprocessor-specific controls your team needs for evidence (SOCs, encryption, RBAC reports)?

We'd like to turn this around within 7 business days if possible — legal runway for the order form is tight for DN-4871. If there's a specific clause you want Redwood to accept as an exception, please call it out so Kimberly can triage.

Thanks,
Ben
Ben Carter
Account Executive — Strategic Accounts
Redwood Inference
ben.carter@redwood.ai | +1 (415) 555-0142

[Attachment: EdgeNova_Subproc_List.xlsx (xlsx)]

From: Stacey Hart <stacey.hart@edgenova.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>
Cc: EdgeNova Legal Team <legal@edgenova.com>, Procurement Ops <procurement@edgenova.com>, Laura Bennett <laura.bennett@redwood.ai>, Neha Kapoor <neha.kapoor@redwood.ai>
Date: Wed, 18 Feb 2026 10:35:00 -0800
Subject: Re: Subprocessor redline synchronization & exception runbook

On Tue, Feb 17, 2026 at 9:12 AM Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai> wrote:
> Stacey —
> 
> Quick follow from our call earlier this week...

Hi Ben —

Thanks for the summary and the clear ask. I attached our redline of the MSA with the new subprocessor paragraph and a short cover note (EdgeNova_MSA_redline.docx). The key items from our side: 

- LogStream provides ephemeral log storage (30 days) and will not be a data processor for PII purposes in most of our standard SKUs, but they do require access to request traces (which can include customer identifiers).
- TelemetryOps handles anonymized metrics only.
- We can accept a documented exception path for LogStream that requires an attestations package (SOC2 Type II report + encryption-in-transit confirmation) and a 90-day remediation SLA.

EdgeNova legal is OK with adding a subprocessor notice & objection process modeled on Section 3.5 of our current DPA. We wanted to check Redwood's minimum required language for the flow-down and whether a 90-day remediation SLA is acceptable to you.

I'll let our legal team pick up once Kimberly chimes in. If we can consolidate changes, Procurement will proceed to route the MSA + order form to Finance.

Best,
Stacey Hart
Sr. Contracts Manager, EdgeNova Systems
stacey.hart@edgenova.com | +1 (206) 555-0199

[Attachment: EdgeNova_MSA_redline.docx (docx)]

From: Kimberly Park <kimberly.park@redwood.ai>
To: EdgeNova Legal Team <legal@edgenova.com>, Stacey Hart <stacey.hart@edgenova.com>
Cc: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>, Laura Bennett <laura.bennett@redwood.ai>, Neha Kapoor <neha.kapoor@redwood.ai>
Date: Fri, 20 Feb 2026 08:47:00 -0800
Subject: Re: Subprocessor redline synchronization & exception runbook

Thanks for the background — appreciate the quick turnaround, Stacey. I reviewed EdgeNova_MSA_redline.docx and sketched a short summary + suggested language below. Attached is a two-page Redline_Summary.pdf for your legal team.

High-level position (Redwood): we can accept a notice-and-objection process for new subprocessors, but need three clarifications before approval:

1) Flow-down obligations: Redwood requires that any subprocessor which processes customer content is bound by the same DPA terms as EdgeNova (encryption in transit, SOC2 or equivalent, data deletion on request). Proposed clause text (suggested insertion into Section 4.2):

"Where a Subprocessor will process Customer Content, Supplier shall ensure by contract that such Subprocessor implements and maintains security measures substantially similar to those set forth in the DPA, including but not limited to encryption in transit, access controls, and data deletion upon request of Supplier or Customer. Supplier shall provide Supplier's and Subprocessor's evidence documentation within 10 business days of request."

2) Exception path: we can agree to a temporary exception window (no longer than 120 days) provided EdgeNova accepts a remediation plan and an evidence handoff. Our legal preference is 60-90 days for operational controls; 90 days is acceptable if EdgeNova agrees to a documented remediation SLA and a point of contact.

3) Data residency: if LogStream will persist logs in the EU and those logs can be tied to a Customer subject to EU residency, we need an explicit mapping table and confirmation the logs will be scrubbed/anonymized by default. If not anonymized, Redwood will require either (a) EdgeNova to route those customers to EU-only processing or (b) EdgeNova to provide documented lawful basis and subprocessors' SCCs/adequacy mechanisms.

Action items / timing:

Stacey —

Quick follow from our call earlier this week. EdgeNova's proposed change to Section 4.2 (subprocessor flow-down) adds two named downstream vendors: LogStream Inc. and TelemetryOps. Before we push this into the MSA/order form, we need to: 

- Confirm whether LogStream handles any persistent customer content (LogStream noted it retains logs for 30 days in their EU cluster).
- Align DPA language to ensure our customer data residency obligations aren't broken by the cascade.
- Capture a short-lived exception path if LogStream requires an alternative SSA (service-specific addendum) for logging.

Attached is the subprocessor list you shared (EdgeNova_Subproc_List.xlsx). Can EdgeNova legal share your redline version of the MSA (tracked changes) and flag any subprocessor-specific controls your team needs for evidence (SOCs, encryption, RBAC reports)?

Hi Ben —

Thanks for the summary and the clear ask. I attached our redline of the MSA with the new subprocessor paragraph and a short cover note (EdgeNova_MSA_redline.docx). The key items from our side: 

- LogStream provides ephemeral log storage (30 days) and will not be a data processor for PII purposes in most of our standard SKUs, but they do require access to request traces (which can include customer identifiers).
- TelemetryOps handles anonymized metrics only.

From: Kimberly Park <kimberly.park@redwood.ai>
To: EdgeNova Legal Team <legal@edgenova.com>, Stacey Hart <stacey.hart@edgenova.com>
Cc: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>, Laura Bennett <laura.bennett@redwood.ai>, Neha Kapoor <neha.kapoor@redwood.ai>
Date: Fri, 20 Feb 2026 08:47:00 -0800
Subject: Re: Subprocessor redline synchronization & exception runbook

Thanks for the background — appreciate the quick turnaround, Stacey. I reviewed EdgeNova_MSA_redline.docx and sketched a short summary + suggested language below. Attached is a two-page Redline_Summary.pdf for your legal team.

High-level position (Redwood): we can accept a notice-and-objection process for new subprocessors, but need three clarifications before approval:

1) Flow-down obligations: Redwood requires that any subprocessor which processes customer content is bound by the same DPA terms as EdgeNova (encryption in transit, SOC2 or equivalent, data deletion on request). Proposed clause text (suggested insertion into Section 4.2):

"Where a Subprocessor will process Customer Content, Supplier shall ensure by contract that such Subprocessor implements and maintains security measures substantially similar to those set forth in the DPA, including but not limited to encryption in transit, access controls, and data deletion upon request of Supplier or Customer. Supplier shall provide Supplier's and Subprocessor's evidence documentation within 10 business days of request."

2) Exception path: we can agree to a temporary exception window (no longer than 120 days) provided EdgeNova accepts a remediation plan and an evidence handoff. Our legal preference is 60-90 days for operational controls; 90 days is acceptable if EdgeNova agrees to a documented remediation SLA and a point of contact.

3) Data residency: if LogStream will persist logs in the EU and those logs can be tied to a Customer subject to EU residency, we need an explicit mapping table and confirmation the logs will be scrubbed/anonymized by default. If not anonymized, Redwood will require either (a) EdgeNova to route those customers to EU-only processing or (b) EdgeNova to provide documented lawful basis and subprocessors' SCCs/adequacy mechanisms.

Action items / timing: 
- EdgeNova legal: confirm acceptance of our proposed flow-down sentence and whether you can provide SOC2 snapshot + encryption notes for LogStream within 5 business days.
- EdgeNova procurement: confirm if a 90-day remediation SLA is operationally feasible.
- Ben/Laura: please flag customer DN-4871 as 'hold to acceptance' until evidence is received; sales should notify the customer of expected timelines.

If EdgeNova prefers, we can accept a signed DocuSign addendum that references the exception runbook and includes a remediation plan table. I've placed a placeholder DocuSign link in the related links for convenience.

title: NordicPay international quality roadmap and eval next steps
preview:
From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
To: Alex Nyström <alex.nystrom@nordicpay.com>, Elena Müller <elena.mueller@nordicpay.de>, Kenji Sato <kenji.sato@kuji.co.jp>
Cc: Neha Kapoor <neha.kapoor@redwood.com>, Maya Srinivasan <maya.srinivasan@redwood.com>
Date: Tue, 17 Feb 2026 08:34:00 -0500
Subject: NordicPay international quality roadmap and eval next steps

Hi Alex / Elena / Kenji —

Thanks again for the time today. Quick recap and a proposed path forward so we can get a pilot scoped by end of March: 

Summary from the call
- Goal: Improve conversational and transactional support across EU (de, sv) and APAC (ja, ko) locales for NordicPay customer-facing chat and refunds flow.
- Success metrics: reduction in intent misclassifications, <3% critical hallucination rate, and end-to-end latency under 600ms for 95th percentile on peak queries.

Redwood proposed evaluation plan (high level)
1) Small-scale classifier + generator evaluation using your anonymized sample set (we received 2000 rows via SFTP today).
2) Locale-specific prompt sets and targeted prompt templates (attached: locale_prompt_sets.zip) — these are the starting prompts our QA and prompt engineering teams will run.
3) Model candidates to benchmark (we'll run each across the same prompt suite):
   - redwood/sherpa-7b-v2-q4 (balanced latency / quality)
   - redwood/titan-1.4b-fp16 (low-latency, production-suitable for simple intents)
   - bloomz-mt-7b1 (strong multilingual base, esp. for Japanese)
   - fallback: larger 13B variant for edge cases if latency permit

Preliminary metrics we will compute
- intent accuracy, canonical response match (tokenized), hallucination rate (manual sampling), per-locale throughput and 95/99p latency.

Immediate asks / next steps
- Alex: please confirm if the SFTP dataset includes the Swedish examples we discussed or if that's a separate export.
- Elena: can you share a small set of annotated German utterances (10–20) for initial lab checks?
- Kenji: any constraints around PII redaction or regional data handling (KYC/JP) you want encoded into the eval?

Operational timeline
- Week 1 (now): kick off, ingest sample data, quick smoke on one model.
- Week 2: full prompt-run across 3 model candidates + metric report.
- Week 3: synthesis and recommendation; pilot scope + price estimate.

Attachments: locale_prompt_sets.zip (prompt templates per-locale), pilot_scope_nordicpay.pdf (draft).

If that looks right I will loop Neha to propose pricing and Maya to own the eval scripts.

Thanks,
Ben Carter
Director, Solutions — Redwood Inference
ben.carter@redwood.com



From: Alex Nyström <alex.nystrom@nordicpay.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
Cc: Elena Müller <elena.mueller@nordicpay.de>, Kenji Sato <kenji.sato@kuji.co.jp>
Date: Wed, 18 Feb 2026 09:12:00 +0100
Subject: Re: NordicPay international quality roadmap and eval next steps

Thanks Ben — solid summary. A few clarifications from our side: 

1) Languages & priority
- German (de) = highest priority (payments + disputes)
- Swedish (sv) = next (support chat volume ~30%)
- Japanese (ja) = pilot for APAC; lower volume but high sensitivity around KYC phrases
- Korean (ko) = exploratory (we have ~5% traffic)

2) Data / PII
- The SFTP dataset is pseudonymized but still includes masked IDs; for Japanese we need strict handling (no export of raw tokens outside EEA/Japan without MFA). We can provide an anonymized sample for the eval folder (I'll attach a small sample in my next email).

3) Timing constraints
- We would like a recommendation before March 22 to align with our Q2 roadmap planning.

4) Success thresholds — quick note
- For German: target intent accuracy >= 92% and hallucination <2% on high-risk intents (refunds/payments).

One ask: can Redwood run the initial evaluation inside an EU-hosted environment for the German tests? We prefer EU data residency for German customer PII.

Thanks — Alex
Alex Nyström
Head of Product, NordicPay
alex.nystrom@nordicpay.com

On Tue, 17 Feb 2026 at 08:34, Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com> wrote:
> (original message)


From: Neha Kapoor <neha.kapoor@redwood.com>
To: Alex Nyström <alex.nystrom@nordicpay.com>, Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
Cc: Maya Srinivasan <maya.srinivasan@redwood.com>, Sergio Alvarez <sergio.alvarez@redwood.com>
Date: Thu, 19 Feb 2026 11:03:00 -0500
Subject: Re: NordicPay international quality roadmap and eval next steps

Alex — great to meet you on the call. A few commercial notes while the team scopes the technical pilot: 

- Pilot offer (30 days): includes up to 100k tokens/month across three model candidates, access to Redwood Console metrics for that pilot period, and one proactive tuning review. Estimated pilot cost: $6,500 (list).
- If you need EU-hosted execution for the German tests, we can provide a dedicated EU region host under a short addendum (ask: 30% uplift during pilot to cover region-specific infra).
- Post-pilot license options: committed Dedicated pools or a hybrid reserved + burst plan — I'll prepare a short proposal once we have model recommendations.

Next action I’ll take: send a short mutual action plan (MAP) with milestones and sign-off owners; Ben will own the technical scope and Maya will own the eval.

Ben / Maya: please include any specific compliance guardrails we should list in the MAP (e.g., PII handling, retention limits).

Best,
Neha Kapoor
Account Executive — Redwood Inference
neha.kapoor@redwood.com



From: Maya Srinivasan <maya.srinivasan@redwood.com>
To: Alex Nyström <alex.nystrom@nordicpay.com>, Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
Cc: Neha Kapoor <neha.kapoor@redwood.com>, Sergio Alvarez <sergio.alvarez@redwood.com>
Date: Fri, 20 Feb 2026 15:22:00 -0800
Subject: Re: NordicPay international quality roadmap and eval next steps

Hi all — technical follow-up and recommended pass/fail criteria for the pilots. Apologies for length; wanted to be concrete.

Evaluation mechanics
- We will run the provided 2k sample + the 10k synthetic prompts (per-locale) through all candidates. Outputs will be captured with token-by-token timing in Redwood Console.
- For each locale we will compute: intent accuracy, BLEU (for templated responses), chrF++ (for morphological languages), strict canonical-match %, and a hallucination score (human-sampled checks across the top 200 failures).

Recommended thresholds
- German (de): intent >= 92%, canonical match >= 88%, hallucination < 2%
- Swedish (sv): intent >= 89%, canonical match >= 84%
- Japanese (ja): intent >= 88%, chrF++ >= 0.54 (approx), hallucination < 3%

Model configuration notes
- Use redwood/sherpa-7b-v2-q4 as baseline; run titan-1.4b-fp16 for low-latency comparison. For Japanese we will include bloomz-mt-7b1 as it shows better tokenization for ja.
- Run quantized variants where latency matters but keep a fp16 reference run for quality comparison.
- Enable prefix caching on repeated dialogs; we will estimate cost savings and effect on latency.

Regression and monitoring plan
- We will lock a prompt-suite (attached) and generate a deterministic seed set for regression checks after tuning.
- Set up a daily regression job against the MAP owned examples; fail fast and alert the CS/Eng team if hallucination delta > 0.5% week-over-week on German high-risk intents.

Data residency & compliance
- For German-run experiments we can execute inside an EU region — Ben, I’ll need a short order to pin these runs to eu-central.
- Suggested retention: store only metadata + token hashes for 90 days; raw transcripts remain in your controlled SFTP bucket unless you approve export.

Deliverables we will produce
1) Per-model, per-locale metric report (PDF)
2) Prompt-set and seed corpus used for regression (in Drive)
3) Recommendation memo with cost/latency tradeoffs and suggested production rollout plan

Links: evaluation script (internal): https://drive.google.com/drive/folders/1aB_europe-apac-eval — contains the runbook and scripts we will use.

Maya Srinivasan
Applied ML — Redwood Inference
maya.srinivasan@redwood.com



From: Alex Nyström <alex.nystrom@nordicpay.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>, Neha Kapoor <neha.kapoor@redwood.com>
Cc: Maya Srinivasan <maya.srinivasan@redwood.com>, Elena Müller <elena.mueller@nordicpay.de>, Kenji Sato <kenji.sato@kuji.co.jp>
Date: Mon, 23 Feb 2026 16:45:00 +0100
Subject: Re: NordicPay international quality roadmap and eval next steps

Thanks everyone — this is perfect.

Confirming next steps from our side: 
- I'll upload the German annotated sample (20 records) and a small Swedish slice to the Drive folder by EOD tomorrow.
- I'll share the signed NDA via DocuSign (link below) and attach a small pseudonymized CSV here: nordicpay_sample_records_2026-02-17.csv (attached).
- We agree to EU-hosted runs for German tests. Please include the EU-region cost uplift in the MAP.

DocuSign: https://www.docusign.com/link/ND-4871-NDA

Looking forward to the pilot.

Best,
Alex
Head of Product — NordicPay

title: ClarityHealth: eval specs, dataset format & scoring — next steps
preview:
From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>
To: Sanjay Gupta <sanjay@clarityhealth.com>
Cc: Laura Bennett <laura.bennett@redwood.ai>, Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwood.ai>
Date: Fri, 20 Feb 2026 15:12:00 +0000
Subject: Post-call recap + proposed eval runbook

Hi Sanjay / Erin —

Thanks again for the call earlier today. Quick recap and concrete next steps so we can get a model-fit eval running on Redwood next week: 

1) Goal & success criteria
- Primary: reduce triage time for inbound patient messages by >= 30% while maintaining answer accuracy (target weighted F1 >= 0.88 on the clinical intent set).
- Secondary: maintain acceptability on safety filters (no PHI leakage, hallucination rate < 2% on sampled outputs).

2) Recommended immediate scope for the eval run
- Task: short-answer triage + intent classification + extractive slot tagging for 6 fields (symptom, duration, urgency, meds, referral_needed, contact_pref).
- Dataset size: 1,200 labeled examples for primary benchmark (900 train / 150 dev / 150 test) plus 300 unlabeled customer logs for hidden evaluation.

3) Models we recommend for initial comparison on Redwood (we'll provision variants):
- Redwood-Large-v2 (33B, fp16) — baseline high-quality variant for quality-first runs
- Redwood-Medium-v1 (13B, int4 quant) — cost-efficient candidate with typical production throughput
- OpenChat-7B-Instruct (7B, fp16) — speed/cost baseline for edge cases

4) Data & delivery options (you chose S3 upload or secure Drive)
- Preferred format: JSONL, one object per line with keys: id, input, reference, metadata. See attached template (clarity_eval_template.jsonl).
- If you prefer encrypted upload, we can provide a presigned S3 path or an SFTP endpoint.

5) Scoring & human eval plan
- Primary automated metrics: intent accuracy, weighted F1 (slots), exact-match for critical slots (referral_needed), and ROUGE-L for short-answer alignment.
- Tiered human review: sample 200 model outputs for safety/hallucination labeling; we use that to calibrate automated thresholds.

6) Timeline & owners
- You: confirm dataset delivery method + send sample 50 examples (non-PHI redacted) by Mon 2026-02-23.
- Us (Laura): prepare eval harness & model variants by Tue 2026-02-24.
- Us (Ben/Karthik): run the first blind evaluation and share dashboard + summary by Thu 2026-02-26.

Attachments: clarity_eval_template.jsonl (dataset schema), redwood_model_recommendations.pdf (model tradeoffs), sample_benchmark_results.jsonl (example output).

Next step: Sanjay/Erin — can you confirm you’ll upload the sample 50 records to the Drive link by Monday? If S3 needed, tell us and we’ll send presigned creds.

Thanks,
Ben Carter
Enterprise Sales
Redwood Inference



From: Sanjay Gupta <sanjay@clarityhealth.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>
Cc: Erin Morris <erin.morris@clarityhealth.com>
Date: Mon, 23 Feb 2026 09:04:00 -0500
Subject: Re: Post-call recap + proposed eval runbook

Ben — thanks for the clear notes. Quick clarifications from our side: 

- The 1,200 labeled examples exist but must be stripped of PHI. We can provide a 50-example synthetic-redaction sample today to validate format.
- For uploads: S3 presigned link works best; please send instructions.
- Scoring: weighted F1 target makes sense — for the human review, can you capture whether hallucinations are clinical (i.e., incorrect medical recommendations) vs. generic factual errors? We need separate counts.

Also, a question on model variants: what's the expected token-cost delta between Redwood-Large-v2 (33B, fp16) and Redwood-Medium-v1 (13B, int4) for a 60-token response on our traffic profile? We need that to estimate monthly spend.

If you can share the S3 link and a simple cost delta calc, we'll upload the 50-sample by EOD.

— Sanjay
Director, Product — ClarityHealth



From: Laura Bennett <laura.bennett@redwood.ai>
To: Sanjay Gupta <sanjay@clarityhealth.com>, Erin Morris <erin.morris@clarityhealth.com>
Cc: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>, Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwood.ai>
Date: Mon, 23 Feb 2026 13:20:00 +0000
Subject: Re: Post-call recap + proposed eval runbook

Hi Sanjay / Erin —

Thanks — I uploaded a presigned S3 instruction doc and a small cost sketch to the Drive folder and included the S3 presign below. Steps to proceed: 

1) S3 upload
- Presigned PUT folder (expires 72h): https://s3-presign.example/clarity-eval-uploads/
- Please upload the sample_50.jsonl and name the full file: clarity_eval_1200_redacted.jsonl when ready.

2) Dataset format reminders (template highlights) — see clarity_eval_template.jsonl attached. Required fields: 
- id: unique string
- input: full prompt or raw patient message (redact PHI)
- reference: gold answer (for classification: label string; for extraction: JSON map of slot->value)
- metadata: free-form string (source_channel, timestamp_utc, annotator_id)

Example line (single-line JSON): {"id":"c-0001","input":"patient: I have chest pain for 2 days...","reference":{"intent":"triage","symptom":"chest pain","duration":"48h","urgency":"high","referral_needed":true},"metadata":"channel=portal;ts=2025-11-09T18:23:00Z"}

3) Scoring & rubric — what we will run on Redwood: 
- Intent classification: accuracy + confusion matrix (class-level recall/precision).
- Slot extraction: micro & macro F1; critical slots (referral_needed) get exact-match weight x2.
- Short-answer: ROUGE-L and human acceptability (3-pt scale: Acceptable / Needs Edit / Unusable).
- Hallucination taxonomy: clinical_hallucination, factual_mismatch, hallucinated_entity. We will return counts per category.

4) Benchmarks & examples
- I attached a small sample_benchmark_results.jsonl that shows how Redwood returns per-example scoring and per-model aggregate.
- Model recommendations PDF in the Drive summarizes latency-optimized configs (batching, int4) vs. quality configs (fp16, longer context kernels).

5) Next steps and internal owners
- Laura (me): will spin up eval harness and queue runs once sample_50 is in S3.
- Karthik: will prepare a cost estimate (pp-token + infra delta) and a config suggestion for autoscaling during the test window.
- Ben: will coordinate commercial points and determine whether we should convert this to a Dedicated proof-of-concept if throughput/latency looks promising.

If you want, we can include a short human eval labeling session next Friday — we can ship the human-review UI and a link to label 200 samples.

Cheers,
Laura Bennett
Solutions Engineer, Redwood Inference

Attachments referenced: clarity_eval_template.jsonl, sample_benchmark_results.jsonl, redwood_model_recommendations.pdf

- Human review session: yes, schedule for next Friday — I’ll assign two clinicians to review 200 samples.

From: Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwood.ai>
To: Sanjay Gupta <sanjay@clarityhealth.com>, Erin Morris <erin.morris@clarityhealth.com>
Cc: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>, Laura Bennett <laura.bennett@redwood.ai>
Date: Mon, 23 Feb 2026 16:05:00 +0000
Subject: Re: Post-call recap + proposed eval runbook

Hi all — following up on Sanjay’s cost question. Rough cost delta (ballpark):
- Redwood-Large-v2 (33B, fp16): ~0.045 USD per 1k tokens generated (inference only; depends on concurrency/config).
- Redwood-Medium-v1 (13B, int4): ~0.012 USD per 1k tokens.

For a 60-token average response and ~50k responses/month:
- Large-v2: 50k * 60 tokens = 3M tokens -> ~135 USD (inference cost) * factor for encoder/context -> expect ~400–700 USD/month in raw model tokens for this workload depending on prompt size + context caching.
- Medium-v1: same token count -> ~36 USD raw tokens -> effective ~120–250 USD/month after overheads.

These are roughs; the biggest swing is from prefix caching (we can reduce prompt tokens if you have consistent system prompts) and batching. For the PoC, the 3-month $18k will mainly be consumed by engineering hours + dedicated infra if you want reserved capacity; purely hosted inference costs for the runs above look modest. I’ll provide a tighter estimate after we see the sample_50 and expected context length.

Karthik Iyer
Product Solutions — Redwood Inference

title: Recap + Action Items: SDK auth routing & 5xx repro (FinEdge)
preview:
From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
To: Suresh Iyer <suresh@finedge.co>, Priya Rao <priya@finedge.co>
Cc: Lauren Bishop <lauren.bishop@redwood.com>
Date: 2026-02-20T15:42:00-08:00
Subject: Recap + Action Items: SDK auth routing & 5xx repro (FinEdge)

Hi Suresh / Priya —

Thanks again for taking the demo earlier today. Quick recap and proposed next steps so we can close the integration gap you flagged: the intermittent 5xxs when your backend calls our /v1/generate endpoint (tool-calling/structured outputs + reranker path).

Recap (from the call)
- You’re on our JS SDK v0.9.2, calling Redwood hosted (us-west-2) with API key auth (X-Api-Key).
- 5xxs appear when payloads include tool-calls that expect large structured outputs (>30KB) and when you combine embeddings + reranker flows.
- Symptoms: client sees HTTP 502/500, request_id present in our logs ~abc123xyz sometimes.

Action items (asks from our side)
1) Can you share a representative failing payload and the error snippet from your side (headers + raw response)? Attach if possible.
2) Confirm SDK version + whether you’re setting a custom timeout or using the default 60s.
3) If you can share a failing request_id (from your logs) we’ll correlate on our side.

I’ve attached a minimal sample_payload.json (sanitized) and would appreciate the error log file if you can send it. I’ll loop in our SRE/engineering to reproduce and propose mitigations.

Next: I’ll forward to Dev (eng) to attempt a reproduction and propose immediate mitigations (timeout/backoff + streaming). Lauren — adding you so leadership has visibility for SLA/credits discussion if this is blocking prod.

Thanks,
Ben Carter
Account Executive — Redwood Inference
ben.carter@redwood.com
Mobile: +1 (415) 555-0192

Attachments: sample_payload.json


From: Suresh Iyer <suresh@finedge.co>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
Cc: Priya Rao <priya@finedge.co>
Date: 2026-02-20T17:06:00-08:00
Subject: Re: Recap + Action Items: SDK auth routing & 5xx repro (FinEdge)

Ben —

Appreciate the follow-up. Attaching the log snippet and the sample payload we used in the call. Two data points: request_id=abc123xyz (502) and request_id=def456lmn (timeout/504). We are on SDK 0.9.2 and haven’t overridden timeout (client defaults).

Log excerpt (sanitized):
[2026-02-20T16:45:12Z] request_id=abc123xyz path=/v1/generate status=502 backend=lm-cluster-3 upstream_time=0.452s details=tool_response_truncated
[2026-02-20T16:48:03Z] request_id=def456lmn path=/v1/generate status=504 backend_timeout=60s

Observations from our side:
- Failures spike when the tool-calling response JSON size goes above ~48KB.
- We are concurrently calling embeddings for similarity + then a reranker endpoint in the same request pipeline.

Files attached: finedge_error_log_20260220.txt, sample_payload.json

Thanks,
Suresh Iyer
Head of Engineering — FinEdge Payments
suresh@finedge.co

On Feb 20, 2026, at 15:42, Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com> wrote:
> (see thread)


From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
To: Dev Patel <dev.patel@redwood.com>
Cc: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>, Lauren Bishop <lauren.bishop@redwood.com>
Date: 2026-02-20T19:05:00-08:00
Subject: Fwd: Recap + Action Items: SDK auth routing & 5xx repro (FinEdge)

Forwarding Suresh’s logs and sample payload — can you reproduce? Key asks:
- Repro on SDK v0.9.2 (JS) and on our internal curl path.
- Check whether the 5xx is triggered by: large structured tool outputs, combining embedding+reranker in a single request, or an auth/header mismatch.
- If you can reproduce, outline the minimal config change (timeout, streaming, routing to dedicated pool) we should recommend to FinEdge.

Notes for repro:
- Suresh claims default timeout = 60s. If backend spends >60s during reranker step we might be hitting gateway timeout.
- They are using API key header; confirm we handle x-api-key vs Authorization: Bearer parity.

I’ve attached repro_instructions_for_eng.md (small checklist) — please add repro steps and paste results inline. If it’s a quick patch we can coordinate a targeted canary to FinEdge staging.

Thanks — Ben


From: Dev Patel <dev.patel@redwood.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>
Cc: Lauren Bishop <lauren.bishop@redwood.com>
Date: 2026-02-21T09:38:00-08:00
Subject: Re: Fwd: Recap + Action Items: SDK auth routing & 5xx repro (FinEdge)

Thanks for the ping — I reproduced this locally and on a staging cluster tonight. Summary of findings: reproducible 502 when payload triggers tool-calling with a large tool_response object and simultaneous embedding+reranker calls in the same request path.

Repro steps (high level):
1) curl against /v1/generate with sample_payload.json (attached) and header X-Api-Key: <redacted>
2) Payload includes tools:[{...}] where tool returns a structured JSON ~65KB
3) SDK default timeout 60s — the reranker stage sometimes takes 70-90s under load, causing upstream gateway to return 504/502 depending on where it fails

Root causes identified:
- Under load, the reranker path can exceed the 60s gateway timeout.
- When the tool response size crosses ~64KB we hit an internal buffer limit in the pipeline which produces an upstream 502 (buffer allocation failure).
- In a minority of cases code path expected Content-Type header and malformed client headers (missing charset) triggered a different codepath that did not stream the body, worsening latency.

Immediate mitigations I tested:
- Increase client/server timeout to 120s for FinEdge (temporary).
- Enable streaming on the generate call (prefer_streaming: true) so large tool responses are chunked — removes buffer spike.
- Add retry_on_5xx with exponential backoff (start 250ms, factor 2, max 3 retries).

Suggested next steps for a staged rollout:
- Short-term (canary): enable prefer_streaming + client timeout override (120s) for FinEdge staging; add retry_on_5xx.
- Mid-term: bump buffer thresholds and add defensive checks to reject tool responses >128KB with clear 413 and guidance.
- Long-term: refactor reranker worker to stream partial results to avoid holding full object in memory.

Attachments: repro_steps.md (contains exact curl and node examples).

I’ve included the curl I used:
curl -X POST https://api.redwood.ai/v1/generate
  -H "X-Api-Key: <redacted>"
  -H "Content-Type: application/json; charset=utf-8"
  --data-binary @sample_payload.json

Result: HTTP/1.1 502 Bad Gateway
Response-Id: abc123xyz

Will prepare a canary config patch for staging; want to validate with Suresh before rolling to prod.

— Dev


From: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>, Dev Patel <dev.patel@redwood.com>
Cc: Lauren Bishop <lauren.bishop@redwood.com>, Suresh Iyer <suresh@finedge.co>
Date: 2026-02-22T10:15:00-08:00
Subject: Re: Fwd: Recap + Action Items: SDK auth routing & 5xx repro (FinEdge)

Quick update + rollout plan attached.

Short-term fix (canary for FinEdge staging):
- Enable prefer_streaming on their account + raise client/server timeout to 120s.
- Add retry_on_5xx with backoff.
- Add a guard that rejects tool_response > 128KB with a clear 413 to avoid unbounded memory spikes.

I can push the canary today to the staging pool in us-west-2 and run a synthetic load test against the failing payload. If the canary looks good, we can schedule a controlled rollout to production next week.

Lauren / Ben — can you please confirm sign-off to proceed with the FinEdge staging canary? I’ll coordinate timing with Suresh (cc’d).

Attachments: rollout_plan.pdf

Thanks,
Marissa Cole
Senior Software Engineer — Redwood Inference

From: Dev Patel <dev.patel@redwood.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>
Cc: Lauren Bishop <lauren.bishop@redwood.com>
Date: 2026-02-21T09:38:00-08:00
Subject: Re: Fwd: Recap + Action Items: SDK auth routing & 5xx repro (FinEdge)

Thanks for the ping — I reproduced this locally and on a staging cluster tonight. Summary of findings: reproducible 502 when payload triggers tool-calling with a large tool_response object and simultaneous embedding+reranker calls in the same request path.

Repro steps (high level):
1) curl against /v1/generate with sample_payload.json (attached) and header X-Api-Key: <redacted>
2) Payload includes tools:[{...}] where tool returns a structured JSON ~65KB
3) SDK default timeout 60s — the reranker stage sometimes takes 70-90s under load, causing upstream gateway to return 504/502 depending on where it fails

Root causes identified:
- Under load, the reranker path can exceed the 60s gateway timeout.
- When the tool response size crosses ~64KB we hit an internal buffer limit in the pipeline which produces an upstream 502 (buffer allocation failure).
- In a minority of cases code path expected Content-Type header and malformed client headers (missing charset) triggered a different codepath that did not stream the body, worsening latency.

Immediate mitigations I tested:
- Increase client/server timeout to 120s for FinEdge (temporary).
- Enable streaming on the generate call (prefer_streaming: true) so large tool responses are chunked — removes buffer spike.
- Add retry_on_5xx with exponential backoff (start 250ms, factor 2, max 3 retries).

Suggested next steps for a staged rollout:
- Short-term (canary): enable prefer_streaming + client timeout override (120s) for FinEdge staging; add retry_on_5xx.
- Mid-term: bump buffer thresholds and add defensive checks to reject tool responses >128KB with clear 413 and guidance.
- Long-term: refactor reranker worker to stream partial results to avoid holding full object in memory.

Attachments: repro_steps.md (contains exact curl and node examples).

I’ve included the curl I used:
curl -X POST https://api.redwood.ai/v1/generate
  -H "X-Api-Key: <redacted>"
  -H "Content-Type: application/json; charset=utf-8"
  --data-binary @sample_payload.json

Result: HTTP/1.1 502 Bad Gateway
Response-Id: abc123xyz

Will prepare a canary config patch for staging; want to validate with Suresh before rolling to prod.

— Dev

title: Request: Temporary SLA carve-out for filter/toolchain incident + mitigation proposal
preview:
From: Kristen Holmes <kristen.holmes@acmecorp.com>
To: Aisha Rahman <aisha.rahman@redwood.ai>
Cc: security@acmecorp.com
Date: Wed, 18 Feb 2026 15:03:00 -0800
Subject: Unexpected omissions and tool-call failures — request for temporary SLA carve-out

Hi Aisha,

We observed a production incident yesterday (Feb 17) where our calls to Redwood for an internal customer workflow encountered two concurrent issues: (1) the content filter triggered on ~0.8% of the requests and stripped structured tool-calls in the assistant response, and (2) several downstream tool-calls timed out and returned 504s causing our orchestrator to re-run the prompt leading to elevated latency and duplicate actions. The combined effect pushed our end-to-end P95 latency from ~420ms to ~1.8s and produced missing actions for some users.

We need two things from Redwood:
- A formal confirmation if this class of failure is covered by the standard SLA or if it requires a carve-out (we assume the moderation/filtering pipeline may be considered a runtime dependency).
- A temporary exception or SLA adjustment while we and you implement mitigations, plus a proposed mitigation plan and timeline.

I've attached logs and a short sample trace. Happy to jump on a call — Tom (cc'd) will be the technical lead on our side.

Thanks,
Kristen Holmes
Sr. Security & Trust Engineering
Acme Corp

[Attachment: evidence-filter-logs-20260218.csv (CSV, 204 KB)]

From: Aisha Rahman <aisha.rahman@redwood.ai>
To: Kristen Holmes <kristen.holmes@acmecorp.com>
Cc: Marcus Lin <marcus.lin@redwood.ai>, Olga Petrov <olga.petrov@redwood.ai>, Jonas Weber <jonas.weber@redwood.ai>
Date: Wed, 18 Feb 2026 17:12:00 -0800
Subject: Re: Unexpected omissions and tool-call failures — request for temporary SLA carve-out

Hi Kristen — thanks for the detailed report and the logs. We take incidents that impact action correctness very seriously. High level:

- Short answer on coverage: The filtering and safety pipeline is considered a runtime safeguard; per our standard hosted SLA we do not guarantee output parity when content safeguards actively modify or suppress tokens. That means tool-call truncation/structured-output removal caused by the filter can fall into a carve-out.

- That said, we want to minimize customer impact. Attached is a proposed mitigation checklist and a timeline to close the gaps. We can provision a temporary narrow carve-out for the period required to implement a fix, provided Acme agrees to the mitigation milestones and a joint testing window.

Proposed next steps (quick):
1) Redwood technical deep-dive: Marcus and team to analyze the sample traces and confirm exact filter signatures that led to structured output removal (ETA 24 hours).
2) Short-term: enable conservative fallback mode for your project that will prefer returning a controlled failure token rather than silently stripping tool-call payloads (ETA 72 hours).
3) Medium-term: whitelist/heuristics tuning and a safety-verified bypass path for authenticated tool-calls (ETA 2–3 weeks).
4) Post-implementation: joint acceptance testing window + rollback plan.

I'll get Marcus and Olga looped for the deep-dive today. If acceptable, we can also put a provisional carve-out in writing that limits the exception to the specific filter signatures and date range and includes a requirement for joint testing.

Best,
Aisha
Head of Strategic Accounts — Redwood Inference

[Attachment: proposed-mitigation-checklist.docx (DOCX, 48 KB)]

From: Aisha Rahman <aisha.rahman@redwood.ai>
To: Marcus Lin <marcus.lin@redwood.ai>, Olga Petrov <olga.petrov@redwood.ai>, Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwood.ai>, Jonas Weber <jonas.weber@redwood.ai>
Date: Wed, 18 Feb 2026 17:15:00 -0800
Subject: Fwd: Unexpected omissions and tool-call failures — deep-dive request

Team — forwarding Acme's logs and Kristen's note. TL;DR: structured-output/tool-call payloads are being suppressed by our filter in a small fraction of requests and downstream tool-call timing is amplifying the user-visible impact. Need a scoped root cause and short-term mitigation we can deploy to Acme's project.

Please prioritize:
- Marcus: can you run the attached CSV through the filter-trace analyzer and confirm which rule IDs triggered and whether the suppression was pre/post tokenization?
- Olga: outline the safest conservative fallback (controlled failure token) and the telemetry we'll emit to validate behavior.
- Karthik/Jonas: estimate effort to implement an authenticated bypass path for tool-calls and the safety review steps.

Goal: have a clear recommendation for Acme by EOD tomorrow (Feb 19). Ping me if blocked.

Thanks,
Aisha

From: Marcus Lin <marcus.lin@redwood.ai>
To: Aisha Rahman <aisha.rahman@redwood.ai>, Olga Petrov <olga.petrov@redwood.ai>, Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwood.ai>, Jonas Weber <jonas.weber@redwood.ai>
Date: Thu, 19 Feb 2026 09:08:00 -0800
Subject: Re: Fwd: Unexpected omissions and tool-call failures — deep-dive results

Aisha/Team — quick findings from the sample traces and logs:

- Root cause summary: The structured tool-call signatures were flagged by rule set FS-12 and FS-17. In several cases the filter removed the tokens that encoded the tool-call JSON before the tool-call serializer step executed. Separately, a subset of downstream invocations experienced an intermittent 500/504 due to a transient GC spike on the partner's runtime, which caused our client to retry and hit higher latency windows.

- Impact scope: ~0.8% of requests for Acme's project during the window Feb 17 02:00–06:00 UTC. Most failures resulted in graceful fallback; ~0.03% led to missing actions in the orchestration layer.

- Short-term mitigation (can be shipped as a reversible config):
  * Conservative-fallback mode for project ID acme-prod-1: on detection of a filter signature that would remove structured-output, instead emit a fixed failure token "[TOOL_CALL_BLOCKED]" and add detailed telemetry fields (filter_rule_id, pre/post token hash). This avoids silent stripping and lets the orchestrator handle retries deterministically.
  * Disable automatic retry-on-504 at the client layer for 30s during joint testing so Acme can observe single-failure behavior.

- Estimated timeline:
  * Config change and rollout to Acme's project (staging + prod): ~36–48 hours with standard canary.
  * Whitelist/bypass path (authenticated tool-calls): ~14 business days including safety review and regression tests.

I've documented the exact rule firings in INF-2241 and attached a small CSV with match detail. If Acme agrees to the conservative-fallback path and the telemetry we add, we can request a provisional carve-out limited to Feb 17–Mar 4 while the whitelist is developed.

-Marcus
Principal Reliability Engineer
Redwood Inference

[Attachment: filter-match-detail-20260218.csv (CSV, 42 KB)]

From: Kristen Holmes <kristen.holmes@acmecorp.com>
To: Aisha Rahman <aisha.rahman@redwood.ai>, Marcus Lin <marcus.lin@redwood.ai>
Cc: Tom Nguyen <t.nguyen@acmecorp.com>
Date: Fri, 20 Feb 2026 15:33:00 -0800
Subject: Re: Unexpected omissions and tool-call failures — carve-out confirmation

Aisha/Marcus — thank you for the rapid investigation and the clear mitigation path. Acme is prepared to accept a narrow, time-boxed carve-out under the following conditions:

1) Carve-out scope: only applies to the content-filter-induced structured-output suppression signatures FS-12 and FS-17 for project acme-prod-1 between Feb 17 00:00 UTC and Mar 4 23:59 UTC.
2) Acceptance criteria: Redwood enables the conservative-fallback mode and the additional telemetry for the project within 48 hours and shares a canary run report.
3) Accountability: Redwood will open a joint monitoring dashboard (shared read-only link) with the telemetry fields (filter_rule_id, pre/post token hash, action_taken) and weekly status updates until whitelist/bypass is in production.
4) No blanket SLA waiver: This carve-out does not apply to unrelated runtime outages or other service classes.

If you can confirm the rollout schedule and provide an initial canary report, we will accept the limited carve-out in writing and pause any escalation on our side.

Kristen
Sr. Security & Trust Engineering
Acme Corp

[In-reply-to: INF-2241]
