title: Fieldstone discovery — model recommendations and quant trade-off plan
preview:
From: Ben Carter <ben_carter@redwood.com>
To: Emily Li <emily.li@fieldstone.ai>
Cc: Marco Rossi <marco.rossi@fieldstone.ai>, Lauren Bishop <lauren_bishop@redwood.com>
Date: Thu, 19 Feb 2026 10:15:00 -0800
Subject: Quick recap + next steps from today's demo

Hi Emily / Marco — thanks again for taking the time today. Quick recap from our side and proposed next steps: 

- Problem: you need accurate structured extraction from quarterly earnings call transcripts (~500–2,000 tokens per doc), with throughput to keep up with nightly batch jobs and occasional low-latency queries from your analyst UI.
- Primary success criteria: extraction F1 >= 0.88 on our shared test set, 95th pctile latency < 250ms for small interactive queries, and cost per 1k docs < $X (we'll size precisely once we have usage profile).

Immediate next actions we suggested on the call:
1) We run a focused model-fit evaluation on your anonymized sample (you mentioned ~1.2k transcripts).
2) We provide bench runs across three model variants (Llama3-13B-ish open variant, a tuned LLM with instruction-tuning, and a smaller 7B candidate) using AWQ, GPTQ, and FP8 profiles to show throughput/quality tradeoffs.
3) Share a prompt set and scoring script so your team can reproduce.

I've attached the prompt set we discussed (fieldstone_prompt_set_v2.csv) and a short baseline benchmark link. Lauren, can you take point on the model-fit matrix and AWQ vs GPTQ runs? Stephanie, please advise on Dedicated capacity sizing for the nightly batches.

If this plan looks good, can we get your confirmed dataset S3 path or a secure share link and a target date for starting the POC? Ideally we'd kick off the eval next Tue (2026-02-24).

Thanks — Ben

--
Ben Carter
Sales Lead, Strategic Accounts
Redwood Inference
ben_carter@redwood.com | +1 (415) 555-0194


From: Emily Li <emily.li@fieldstone.ai>
To: Ben Carter <ben_carter@redwood.com>
Cc: Marco Rossi <marco.rossi@fieldstone.ai>
Date: Thu, 19 Feb 2026 12:07:00 -0800
Subject: Re: Quick recap + next steps from today's demo

Thanks Ben — this is helpful. A few clarifications from our side: 

- We can provide a sanitized S3 bucket with 1,200 transcripts (anonymized) by EOD Fri 20 Feb.
- The primary schema we're extracting is: speaker, timestamp, topic tags (multi-label), and numeric KPI mentions (e.g., "revenue up 8%" -> revenue: +8%).
- Risk / concern: we saw earlier that 4-bit quantization occasionally mangles numeric precision for KPI extraction. That would hurt our metrics even if overall output looks similar. We want the bench to specifically flag numeric extraction regressions.

Agree on the plan. If you can provide a small scoring harness that highlights numeric extraction F1 separately, that would speed sign-off. Thanks — Emily

Emily Li
Head of Data Products
Fieldstone Financial
emily.li@fieldstone.ai | +1 (212) 555-0123


From: Lauren Bishop <lauren_bishop@redwood.com>
To: Emily Li <emily.li@fieldstone.ai>, Ben Carter <ben_carter@redwood.com>
Cc: Stephanie Nguyen <stephanie_nguyen@redwood.com>
Date: Thu, 19 Feb 2026 13:45:00 -0800
Subject: Re: Quick recap + next steps from today's demo

Hi all — assigning the model-fit plan and bench matrix here with concrete hypotheses and acceptance checks. Summary of recommendation and why: 

1) Candidate models to include in the run (we'll use Redwood-verified open variants where possible):
   - Model A (13B-ish, instruction-tuned) — expected best balance of extraction quality and cost.
   - Model B (7B distilled) — lower cost, lower quality baseline; good fallback for low-cost batch steps.
   - Model C (13B base with extended context) — test for longer transcripts where context window matters.

2) Quant / runtime profiles to compare per model: AWQ (aggressive 4-bit + AWQ calibration), GPTQ (safer 4/8-bit GPTQ), and FP8 (native mixed precision where supported).

3) Bench metrics (we will publish a matrix):
   - Extraction F1 (overall)
   - Numeric KPI extraction F1 (separate column — Emily's point)
   - 50/95/99 latency for interactive queries (100-token prompt)
   - Throughput tokens/sec for batched jobs (batch sizes: 8, 32, 128)
   - Token/unit cost estimate (raw inference only)

4) Pass/fail heuristics for POC signoff:
   - Numeric KPI F1 drop vs best FP8 run <= 3 absolute points for candidate to be acceptable in production.
   - 95th pctile latency under 250ms for interactive flows on Dedicated baseline (p95 target).

5) Execution plan and timeline:
   - Friday (20 Feb): Fieldstone drops sanitized S3 test set; we validate ingestion and quick sanity checks.
   - Mon-Wed (23–25 Feb): Redwood runs bench matrix and populates the report.
   - Thu (26 Feb): Review call to walk the matrix and pick candidate for a 2-week POC.

Attachments: I uploaded a short benchmark template and the scoring harness (link below) and included the prompt set Ben attached into the run. We'll ensure the harness separates numeric KPI scoring.

Preliminary notes on AWQ vs GPTQ vs FP8 (high level):
- AWQ: best throughput for many workloads, but can introduce subtle calibration shifts for numerics unless we do per-token calibration with representative prompt samples. Good candidate for final cost-cutting if calibration holds.
- GPTQ: more conservative quant, generally better preservation of numeric tokens; slightly higher memory and lower throughput than AWQ in our tests.
- FP8: highest fidelity but higher runtime/memory cost on supported hardware; good for canary or mission-critical endpoints.

Will post the bench link when jobs complete. Ben — let us know if you want Redwood Optimize to run an automated suggestion for batching and cache windows for nightly jobs; that can reduce cost without touching quant profiles.

—Lauren
Lauren Bishop
Applied ML Solutions
Redwood Inference
lauren_bishop@redwood.com


From: Stephanie Nguyen <stephanie_nguyen@redwood.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben_carter@redwood.com>, Lauren Bishop <lauren_bishop@redwood.com>
Cc: Emily Li <emily.li@fieldstone.ai>
Date: Fri, 20 Feb 2026 08:55:00 -0800
Subject: Re: Quick recap + next steps from today's demo

Quick operational note from ops sizing perspective:

- For the nightly batch (1,200 transcripts, avg ~1,100 tokens each), a Dedicated pool sized for sustained throughput at ~40k tokens/sec should complete nightly processing comfortably within your SLA. If we use AWQ with large batches (128) we'll see the best cost profile; if numeric regressions appear we recommend running the numeric-exact extraction on FP8 canary instances for a single replica to validate outputs before committing all nightly runs.

- I'll provision a small Dedicated pool in us-west-2 for the POC (2x A100 equivalent) and enable tracing hooks so we can deliver per-request token/latency/cost breakdowns into the Console. Estimated provisional cost for a 2-week POC: ~USD 6.5k (inclusive of Dedicated baseline, bench runs, and storage I/O), we can refine once benchmark data is in.

Next steps I suggest we lock today: confirm S3 share, Redwood runs bench Mon–Wed, review Thu 26 Feb. Calendar invite sent for 26 Feb 09:00 PT review.

-steph
Stephanie Nguyen
Solutions Operations Manager
Redwood Inference
stephanie_nguyen@redwood.com

Date: Fri, 20 Feb 2026 09:12:00 -0800

title: Recap & next steps: model fit, benchmark links, and regression mitigation
preview:
From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
To: Olivia Chen <olivia.chen@fintech.co>
Cc: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>
Date: Wed, 18 Feb 2026 14:03:00 -0800
Subject: Recap & next steps: model fit, benchmark links, and regression mitigation

Hi Olivia —

Thanks again for taking the time today. Quick recap from our discovery so everyone has the same context and clear next steps.

What we heard from FinTech Co:
- Primary goal: improve customer support auto-triage (intent classification + structured extraction) while keeping SERP-quality answers for escalation paths.
- Typical prompt lengths: short instructions + ~1-2 KB of context (ticket history).
- Latency SLO: P95 < 250ms for 512-token responses during business hours.
- Concern: historical sensitivity to model updates breaking extraction heuristics.

Redwood recommendations (initial):
1) Model candidates for pilot:
   - redwood/model-alpha-1@v2.3.0 (quantized inference profile: q4f16) — best balance of extraction accuracy and 80% of baseline token-cost.
   - open/llama2-70b-chat (32-bit) — fallback for highest-fidelity cases; higher cost.

2) Evals & artifacts shared:
   - eval_prompt_set_v1.jsonl (attached) — 150 representative prompts derived from your sample set we discussed.
   - benchmark_report_Q4.pdf (attached) — our run of model candidates on similar workloads (latency, P95, token-cost, QA F1).
   - quick-run notebook link in the drive: https://drive.google.com/file/d/1a2b3c-benchmark-report

3) Risk mitigations for model/version updates (short):
   - Pin model version for production routes (we do not auto-upgrade).
   - Canary rollouts with automated eval diffs against your critical prompt set; rollback policy triggers on user-defined regressions.
   - Option to enable automatic fallback to a pinned stable variant if drift detected.

Proposed next steps:
- You: share a 1k-2k sample of de-identified tickets (S3 link or Drive) so we can run the eval set on your exact data.
- Us: run a focused diff between v2.3.0 and the most recent v2.4.x candidate (two-week TAT) and share a summary.
- Schedule a 30-min technical checkpoint next week where Marissa walks through the eval diffs and rollout options.

Availability: I can do Tue/Wed next week afternoon PT. Let me know what works.

Attachments: eval_prompt_set_v1.jsonl, benchmark_report_Q4.pdf

Best,
Ben Carter
Account Executive | Redwood Inference
ben.carter@redwood.com | +1 (415) 555-0142



From: Olivia Chen <olivia.chen@fintech.co>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
Cc: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>
Date: Thu, 19 Feb 2026 09:12:00 -0800

Hi Ben / Marissa —

From: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>
To: Olivia Chen <olivia.chen@fintech.co>, Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
Date: Thu, 19 Feb 2026 11:05:00 -0800
Subject: Re: Recap & next steps: model fit, benchmark links, and regression mitigation

Hi Olivia —

Great questions. Short answers first, then an attached example diff + playbook.

1) Detection & alerting:
   - We run automated eval diffs during any model candidate rollout against a pinned golden prompt set (we'll use the eval_prompt_set_v1 you already have + your additional sample).
   - Alerts can be configured on absolute-percent F1 drops or on statistical significance for your chosen metrics (we typically recommend F1 drop > 2.5 points or 95th percentile latency increase > 15%).

2) Rollback & pinning mechanics:
   - Pin: You can pin a specific model artifact (e.g., redwood/model-alpha-1@v2.3.0) at the route level. Pinning prevents automatic upgrades; infra/security patching still applied at the runtime layer.
   - Canary: We support gradual rollouts (1% -> 10% -> 50%) with automatic gate checks. If gates fail, rollout halts and we either keep traffic on the pinned version or fall back to a configured stable variant.
   - Rollback: Automated; operator + API rollback options. We keep model artifacts for 90 days by default (can be extended under Dedicated/Private).

3) Trade-offs:
   - Pinning preserves quality but means you may miss downstream improvements (and new optimizations). If compute/security patches require new binary dependencies, we still apply them but with separate validation against the pinned artifact.

4) Timeline & artifacts:
   - Two-week turnaround is reasonable for the initial diff if we get the sample by Monday EOD.
   - Attached: rollback_playbook.md (high-level ops steps) and a sample diff plot in the benchmark PDF you already received.

Please send the S3 link when ready. For the sample diff format, see: https://docs.redwood.com/guides/rollback-playbook (also attached).

Best,
Marissa Cole
Senior Solutions Engineer | Redwood Inference
marissa.cole@redwood.com

Attachments: rollback_playbook.md

We need the following by COB Mon 22 Feb: a mapping to NIST AI RMF (controls you implement / responsible team), a short statement on how Redwood positions against the EU AI Act (high-level classification + mitigations), and a concrete evaluation / regression monitoring plan (how you detect quality regressions, datasets you run, alerting thresholds, turnaround SLAs for remediation).

Thanks for the clear ask and the deadline. We're attaching an internal outline to show the structure we plan to deliver. Would you be available for a 45-minute call Wed 18 Feb to walk through assumptions (use-case detail, PHI handling, and the remediation SLAs) before we finalize?

From: Lauren Bishop <lauren.bishop@redwood.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>, Neha Kapoor <neha.kapoor@redwood.com>, Marcus Lin <marcus.lin@redwood.com>
Date: Mon, 16 Feb 2026 11:03:00 -0500
Subject: Internal: RFI - evaluation/regression approach notes

Team —

Copied Ben's note below. Quick notes for what I think should go into the evaluation & regression section (we should be conservative in any contractual promises):

- Evaluation architecture (recommended): continuous "eval runner" that executes a scheduled battery every night against a canonical test-suite. Suites: seed clinical summarization set (Aurelius provided + Redwood synthetic), prompt-robustness set, hallucination probes, and latency/cost telemetry.
- Metrics we can commit to show (reporting, not hard SLAs unless in Dedicated): ROUGE/L, Rouge-L F1, factuality score (BLEURT / internally calibrated), hallucination rate (binary detector), latency p95.
- Drift/regression detection: statistical change detection (population shift on embeddings + distributional shift on output lengths / token entropy) and model output regression on seeded checks. Alerts: severity tiering (P1/P2) with automated paging to SEs for P1.
- Remediation process: triage within 4 business hours for P1, containment (fallback to earlier model variant) within 2 hours, and patch rollout plan within agreed timeframe (depends on root cause). We can propose these as "operational targets" for Hosted usage; for Dedicated/Private we can push for contractual SLAs.
- Data & audit: we can provide per-request audit logs with customer-managed retention policy (default 90 days, configurable). For PHI-like workloads we recommend Private deployment or Dedicated with required contractual terms.

Attachments: evaluation-regression-plan.xlsx (worked example of nightly suite + alert thresholds).

Caveats / asks for the RFI response:
- EU AI Act: we should avoid a definitive classification in writing until we see the exact clinical workflow and whether outputs could be classified as autonomous decision-making. Instead, provide a mapping of how our controls map to obligations for high-risk systems and offer a joint classification session.
- Logging/residency: clarify what "auditable" means (retention vs. data residency). If they require EU residency for logs, that's an operational ask (Private or Dedicated in EU).

Happy to draft the evaluation section if Ben wants to own the customer-facing message.

— Lauren
Senior Solutions Engineer | Redwood Inference
lauren.bishop@redwood.com

Date: Tue, 17 Feb 2026 08:27:00 -0500

Thanks Lauren — a few security/compliance constraints to include in the customer-facing RFI:

- Pen test: we can share summaries of our latest external penetration test; full findings are restricted. Put "latest pentest summary available on request."
- KMS / key control: offer customer-managed KMS for Dedicated/Private. For Hosted we can offer envelope encryption + customer-supplied keys in EU regions (with contractual addendum).
- Audit logs & retention: default audit retention 90 days for Hosted. We can offer extended retention or EU residency for logs as a paid add-on or as part of a Dedicated agreement. Avoid promising arbitrary retention windows without procurement sign-off.

If the customer is treating the use-case as high-risk under the EU AI Act, we should recommend Private or Dedicated deployments and an explicit model governance cooperative plan. I recommend Ben include a short paragraph suggesting a "classification workshop" to jointly determine obligations.

preview:
From: Jules Martin <jules.martin@acmehealth.com>
To: aditya.rao@redwood.ai
Cc: compliance@acmehealth.com
Date: Wed, 11 Feb 2026 09:18:00 -0800
Subject: Pilot requirements — retention window, BYOK for PHI audits

Hi Aditya,

Thanks again for the demo yesterday. A quick follow-up from our security and compliance team: for the pilot we need clarity on three items before we sign off for data ingress:

- Retention: can we set retention to 12 months for conversational logs and have an export for audit?
- Audit logs: we need an immutable, exportable audit trail (admin acts, API calls, tokenization events). Sample format would be helpful.
- BYOK / KMS: we prefer customer-managed keys for any PHI data. Is BYOK supported for your hosted product or only in Dedicated/Private?

I'm attaching our current internal retention policy so you can map requirements. We also need an estimate of the engineering effort and a high-level timeline.

Can we get a short write-up before EOD next Tuesday? Jules

Team — forwarding Acme's ask. Short triage: they want 12mo retention + exportable audit + BYOK. Can each of you answer the bullets below by EOD Mon so I can consolidate a single reply to the customer on Tue?

Neha: feasibility/controls and what evidence we can deliver (SOC2 sections, sample logs).
Marcus: product options (hosted vs Dedicated tradeoffs, UI/workflow for retention config, export UX).
Grace: rollout & ops (how this affects routing, caching, cost estimates for 12mo retention).
Laura: any legal/procurement notes (POC terms, minimal contract language for pilot).

Senior Solutions Architect | Redwood Inference
aditya.rao@redwood.ai
(415) 555-0142

From: Neha Kapoor <neha.kapoor@redwood.ai>
To: Aditya Rao <aditya.rao@redwood.ai>, Marcus Lin <marcus.lin@redwood.ai>, Grace O'Connor <grace.oconnor@redwood.ai>, Laura Bennett <laura.bennett@redwood.ai>
Date: Thu, 12 Feb 2026 11:03:00 -08:00
Subject: Re: Pilot requirements — retention window, BYOK for PHI audits

On Wed, Feb 11, 2026 at 9:45 AM Aditya Rao <aditya.rao@redwood.ai> wrote:
> Team — forwarding Acme's ask. Short triage...

Answers (security):

- BYOK: Hosted API does NOT support classic BYOK for customer key rotation today. BYOK is supported for Dedicated and Private deployments where the customer key material can be provisioned into a customer-controlled KMS or HSM. For the pilot, the pragmatic approach is: 1) use Dedicated trial capacity with customer KMS integration, or 2) if they insist on hosted, provide envelope encryption where customer supplies an encrypted key blob (limited guarantee).

- Audit logs: we can produce detailed API-level audit logs (timestamp, actor, API route, request id, resource id) and admin console events. We currently persist audit logs for 90 days in hosted; Dedicated can be configured for longer retention (custom S3 export). We can deliver a CSV export similar to the attached sample.

- Retention & immutability: For immutability/audit-grade retention we recommend exporting logs to customer-controlled storage (S3 with Object Lock or equivalent) for 12 months. We can add a retention config that flags production logs for export.

Risks/notes:
- HIPAA/HITRUST: Hosted short-retention + non-BYOK could be a blocker for regulated PHI customers.
- Evidence: we can provide a runbook + sample logs for compliance review within 3 business days once we have acceptance from Product.

Attaching a sanitized sample log extract.

Neha
Neha Kapoor
Head of Security & Compliance | Redwood Inference
neha.kapoor@redwood.ai
[Attachment: audit-log-sample.csv (CSV, 120KB)]

From: Marcus Lin <marcus.lin@redwood.ai>
To: Aditya Rao <aditya.rao@redwood.ai>, Neha Kapoor <neha.kapoor@redwood.ai>, Grace O'Connor <grace.oconnor@redwood.ai>, Laura Bennett <laura.bennett@redwood.ai>
Date: Fri, 13 Feb 2026 10:22:00 -08:00
Subject: Re: Pilot requirements — retention window, BYOK for PHI audits

Summary from product/roadmap POV:

- MVP approach (minimal scope to unblock pilot):
  1) Add retention UI setting (12mo option) + server-side export job to push logs to a configured S3. No immediate change to hosted DB retention.
  2) Label this as \"Pilot: Dedicated-only BYOK\" — scope Dedicated deployment template + KMS integration docs.
  3) Provide audit log CSV export and a small script to rehydrate logs into a reporting format.

- Estimation: ~3 dev-weeks for retention UI + export pipeline (backend + job), ~2 dev-weeks for KMS integration baseline in Dedicated (we already have a foundation). End-to-end demo in 5 weeks if we start this sprint.

- UX: simplest flow is tenant-level toggle + target S3 + confirm. Avoid per-conversation retention in MVP.

I'll draft a short-fashioned scope document and a timeline; attaching a one-pager.

Marcus
Marcus Lin
Product Manager, Platform
marcus.lin@redwood.ai

> Team — forwarding Acme's ask. Short triage...

Answers (security):

- BYOK: Hosted API does NOT support classic BYOK for customer key rotation today. BYOK is supported for Dedicated and Private deployments where the customer key material can be provisioned into a customer-controlled KMS or HSM. For the pilot, the pragmatic approach is: 1) use Dedicated trial capacity with customer KMS integration, or 2) if they insist on hosted, provide envelope encryption where customer supplies an encrypted key blob (limited guarantee).

- Audit logs: we can produce detailed API-level audit logs (timestamp, actor, API route, request id, resource id) and admin console events. We currently persist audit logs for 90 days in hosted; Dedicated can be configured for longer retention (custom S3 export). We can deliver a CSV export similar to the attached sample.

- Retention & immutability: For immutability/audit-grade retention we recommend exporting logs to customer-controlled storage (S3 with Object Lock or equivalent) for 12 months. We can add a retention config that flags production logs for export.

Risks/notes:
- HIPAA/HITRUST: Hosted short-retention + non-BYOK could be a blocker for regulated PHI customers.
- Evidence: we can provide a runbook + sample logs for compliance review within 3 business days once we have acceptance from Product.

Attaching a sanitized sample log extract.

Neha
Neha Kapoor
Head of Security & Compliance | Redwood Inference
neha.kapoor@redwood.ai

title: Post-call recap: Brightsail head-to-head plan and next steps
preview:
From: Camila Reyes <camila@redwood.com>
To: Suneet Kapoor <suneet@brightsail.com>, Priya Desai <priya@brightsail.com>
Cc: Marcus Lin <marcus@redwood.com>, Karthik Iyer <karthik@redwood.com>
Date: Sun, 15 Feb 2026 16:22:00 -0800
Subject: Post-call recap: Brightsail head-to-head plan and next steps

Hi Suneet & Priya,

Thanks again for the time today — appreciate the candid discussion and the context on Brightsail's current inference bottlenecks. Below is a short recap and a proposed, lightweight head-to-head test plan so you can evaluate objectively across the competing vendors you mentioned. Please flag anything I missed.

Key pain points you shared
- Latency spikes during weekday morning traffic (bursty 40–60 req/s) causing timeouts for analyst workflows
- High per-token inference costs for long-form summarization jobs (avg 900 tokens)
- Data residency & audit requirements for regulated customers in US-East (S3 retention + audit logs)
- Concern about model degradation when using quantized weights for cost savings

Primary goals for the POC
1) Latency: 95th pctile < 200ms for 512-token responses at target throughput
2) Cost: unit cost improvement vs current provider (target ~= 30% lower for our median workloads)
3) Quality: parity on your 200 prompt test set (fidelity on long-form summaries)
4) Compliance: SOC2/TLS and accessible audit logs for storage/retention

Decision criteria (what Brightsail will score vendors on)
- Performance (latency, throughput)
- Cost per 1M tokens at tested config
- Output quality (BLEU/ROUGE + human pass/fail on 200 prompts)
- Security/compliance posture & deployment options
- Ease of integration & timelines for private deployment

Open questions we promised to follow up on
- Effects of 4-bit quantization on your longest prompt set (we'll run quality regressions)
- Timeline for an on-prem/private deployment if you move forward (target: <8 weeks?)
- Exact SLAs required for your analyst-facing endpoints

Proposed head-to-head test plan (2–3 week window)
A) Performance test (w/ traffic replay) — owner: Marcus
   - 50 req/s sustained for 30 minutes, 512-token mode, 95th pctile latency target <200ms
   - Measure cold vs warm starts and KV cache hit rates

B) Quality evaluation — owner: Karthik
   - 200 canonical prompts (you provided sample_prompts.csv) run through each vendor; produce automated metrics + 5-person blind human eval

C) Compliance & logs check — owner: Camila
   - Verify audit-log delivery, retention policy, and KMS integrations on test tenant

Deliverables & timeline
- Week 1: test config & access set up (we'll provide test accounts + short lived tokens)
- Week 2: run perf & quality tests; share preliminary matrix
- Week 3: joint review + vendor scoring and recommendation

Attachments: redwood-bakeoff-matrix.xlsx (initial scoring template), proposed-mop.pdf (method-of-play for tests).

Next steps (proposed)
- Confirm the 200-prompt canonical set (Priya, can you send the cleaned CSV back?)
- Marcus will provision perf endpoints tomorrow and share access by EOD Mon (Feb 16)
- Quick sync: Tue Feb 17, 10:00am ET to kick off — I’ve tentatively held this time.

Thanks again, and looking forward to running the tests.

Cheers,
Camila Reyes
Senior Sales Engineer | Redwood Inference
camila@redwood.com
+1 (415) 555-0199




From: Priya Desai <priya@brightsail.com>
To: Camila Reyes <camila@redwood.com>, Suneet Kapoor <suneet@brightsail.com>

- We'd like Skyline AI inference included as an additional comparator (they're doing an onsite POC next week).
- Can the quality eval include checks for hallucination rate (we have a list of 30 high-risk prompts)?
- For the perf test: can we schedule the 50 req/s run during a maintenance window (Mon 02/23 02:00–04:00 ET)?

I've attached sample_prompts.csv with the 200 prompts (redacted) — please confirm receipt and that the format works. Also, we need to confirm that function-calling / tool integrations are supported in the test environment (important for our ingestion flows).

I'll be OOO Mon morning but available after 1pm ET. Thanks — Priya

--
Priya Desai
Head of Data Products | Brightsail Analytics

From: Marcus Lin <marcus@redwood.com>
To: Suneet Kapoor <suneet@brightsail.com>, Priya Desai <priya@brightsail.com>, Camila Reyes <camila@redwood.com>, Karthik Iyer <karthik@redwood.com>
Date: Mon, 16 Feb 2026 09:12:00 -0800
Subject: Re: Post-call recap: Brightsail head-to-head plan and next steps

Thanks Priya — received the prompts CSV.

Couple quick vendor-comparison notes (first-pass, high level):
- Redwood: strong runtime latency, prefix/KV cache will help warm-throughput; built-in audit logging and VPC/private deployment options.
- CloudGenie: cheaper list price for hosted, but their warm-cold variance on long prompts looks bigger (we'll quantify).
- Skyline AI inference: very aggressive on embeddings + custom tool integrations; unknowns around SOC-type attestations and long-term support SLAs.

I'll take point on the perf runs and will stand up three isolated test endpoints (Redwood, CloudGenie, Skyline) using the same model family and config so results are apples-to-apples. Expected deliverable: vendor-quickscore.csv with latency/cost/quality columns by EOW (next Friday).

Action items I own:
- Provision perf endpoints and share credentials (EOD Mon Feb 16)
- Run 50 req/s replay during your preferred window (confirm Tue 02/23 02:00–04:00 ET)
- Populate quickscore matrix and push initial numbers into the shared drive

If you want, Suneet, we can also include a short smoke test that exercises function-calling for a subset of 30 prompts to validate integration.

— Marcus
Director, Solutions Engineering | Redwood Inference
marcus@redwood.com

title: Security Exhibit — SOC 2 scope, pen test, and audit cadence
preview:
From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
To: Mike Thomson <mike.thomson@acme-corp.com>, Sara Lee <sara.lee@acme-corp.com>
Cc: Priyom Das <priyom.das@redwood.com>, Vivek Kulkarni <vivek.kulkarni@redwood.com>
Date: Sat, 14 Feb 2026 09:12:00 -0800
Subject: Security Exhibit — SOC 2 scope, pen test, and audit cadence

Hi Mike / Sara,

Thanks again for the productive demo yesterday. Per your legal team's ask, I attached our initial MSA redlines and started a short list of items we expect you to want to align on in the Security Exhibit / Audit Rights section:

- SOC 2: confirmation of provider SOC 2 Type II and the cadence for sharing report (redacted copy via NDA vs full report).
- Penetration testing: frequency (annual?), third-party vs internal, and level of evidence (exec summary vs full report).
- Audit rights: notice period, scope limitations (logical access, samples), and cost allocation.
- Evidence cadence: whether we provide rolling monthly/quarterly logs or an on-demand evidence pack.

Please confirm any hard constraints from your side (e.g., required notice period >30 days, mandatory on-site audits, specific log retention windows) so we can loop in Legal & InfoSec and propose concrete language.

Attaching: initial_redlines_MSA_acme_0214.docx (high level redlines + placeholders for security exhibit).

Next steps: if we can get a prioritized list of must-haves vs nice-to-haves from ACME legal by EOD Monday, we can propose redline language by Wednesday.

Thanks,
Ben Carter
Account Executive | Redwood Inference
ben.carter@redwood.com | +1 (415) 555-0199

----
Attachment: initial_redlines_MSA_acme_0214.docx (docx stub)


From: Mike Thomson <mike.thomson@acme-corp.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
Cc: Sara Lee <sara.lee@acme-corp.com>
Date: Sat, 14 Feb 2026 10:05:00 -0800
Subject: Re: Security Exhibit — SOC 2 scope, pen test, and audit cadence

Ben, thanks — and appreciate the clear list. A few notes from our side:

- Attached our team’s redlines to the MSA (ACME_MSA_redlines_0214.pdf). They flag: tighter audit rights language, a requirement for third-party pen test reports within 60 days of completion, and a request to see raw logs for a rolling 90-day window on demand.
- On SOC 2: ACME requires the most recent SOC 2 Type II report covering the control period within the last 12 months. Ideally we get the report and the associated management response if any critical issues.
- On pen tests: we prefer an annual third-party penetration test with evidence (summary + remediation timeline). Full pen test reports can be redacted; we need enough detail to verify scope and critical findings.
- Audit cadence: ACME typically expects rights to audit up to once per 12 months; 30 days notice is standard for us but we can be flexible.

We’ll review the redlines you sent and can prioritize must-haves vs nice-to-haves internally. Sending ACME legal’s redlines now.

Thanks,
Mike Thomson
Security Program Manager, Acme Corporation

From: Laura Bennett <laura.bennett@redwood.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>, Priyom Das <priyom.das@redwood.com>, Vivek Kulkarni <vivek.kulkarni@redwood.com>
Cc: Mike Thomson <mike.thomson@acme-corp.com>, Sara Lee <sara.lee@acme-corp.com>
Date: Sun, 15 Feb 2026 14:20:00 -0800
Subject: Re: Security Exhibit — SOC 2 scope, pen test, and audit cadence

Ben / team — quick draft language and operational commitments below. I tried to balance ACME’s asks with standard provider constraints. Please review and flag any operational concerns (Vivek).

Proposed Security Exhibit snippets (to insert into MSA):

1) SOC 2
"Provider shall maintain a SOC 2 Type II report covering the most recent 12-month period. Upon execution of a mutual NDA, Provider shall make a redacted copy of the SOC 2 Type II report available to Customer within ten (10) business days. Provider may redact sensitive infrastructure details and third-party vendor identifiers. Provider is not required to disclose internal management responses except where material findings remain open."

2) Penetration Testing
"Provider conducts an annual third-party penetration test covering internet-facing services and internal critical controls. Provider will provide Customer an executive summary of findings and a remediation timeline within thirty (30) days of test completion. Full reports may be provided subject to mutual NDA and redaction of sensitive forensic detail."

3) Audit Rights
"Customer may exercise audit rights no more than once per 12-month period, and must provide Provider with no less than thirty (30) days' prior written notice. Audits shall be limited in scope to the systems and controls relevant to the Services and may be conducted remotely unless on-site access is expressly agreed. If an audit identifies a material non-conformance with Provider’s obligations, Provider shall cooperate to remediate; costs of any required on-site audit shall be borne by Customer unless the audit demonstrates material non-conformance attributable to Provider."

4) Evidence Cadence and Logs
"Provider will supply quarterly compliance evidence packages (SOC 2 summary, configuration snapshots, and control attestations) via a secure shared location. Provider will make available aggregated or redacted logs covering the prior 90 days upon receipt of a valid audit request and NDA; live raw logs are not provided as a standard offering.

Operational notes (InfoSec):
- Vivek: confirm we can provide exec summaries within 30 days and prepare quarterly evidence packs. If we need additional time for SOC 2 disclosures, call it out now.
- Priyom: sales ops to confirm whether a 30-day notice commitment is acceptable to ACME as standard.

If no strong objections, I recommend proposing the above as our baseline and negotiating any customer-specific changes.

Laura Bennett
Senior Counsel | Redwood Inference
laura.bennett@redwood.com

----
Attachment: proposed_security_exhibit_snippets.txt (text stub)


From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
To: Mike Thomson <mike.thomson@acme-corp.com>, Sara Lee <sara.lee@acme-corp.com>
Cc: Laura Bennett <laura.bennett@redwood.com>, Vivek Kulkarni <vivek.kulkarni@redwood.com>, Priyom Das <priyom.das@redwood.com>
Date: Mon, 16 Feb 2026 16:24:00 -0800

Subject: Re: Security Exhibit — SOC 2 scope, pen test, and audit cadence

Ben / team — quick draft language and operational commitments below. I tried to balance ACME’s asks with standard provider constraints. Please review and flag any operational concerns (Vivek).

Proposed Security Exhibit snippets (to insert into MSA):

1) SOC 2
"Provider shall maintain a SOC 2 Type II report covering the most recent 12-month period. Upon execution of a mutual NDA, Provider shall make a redacted copy of the SOC 2 Type II report available to Customer within ten (10) business days. Provider may redact sensitive infrastructure details and third-party vendor identifiers. Provider is not required to disclose internal management responses except where material findings remain open."

2) Penetration Testing
"Provider conducts an annual third-party penetration test covering internet-facing services and internal critical controls. Provider will provide Customer an executive summary of findings and a remediation timeline within thirty (30) days of test completion. Full reports may be provided subject to mutual NDA and redaction of sensitive forensic detail."

3) Audit Rights
"Customer may exercise audit rights no more than once per 12-month period, and must provide Provider with no less than thirty (30) days' prior written notice. Audits shall be limited in scope to the systems and controls relevant to the Services and may be conducted remotely unless on-site access is expressly agreed. If an audit identifies a material non-conformance with Provider’s obligations, Provider shall cooperate to remediate; costs of any required on-site audit shall be borne by Customer unless the audit demonstrates material non-conformance attributable to Provider."

4) Evidence Cadence and Logs
"Provider will supply quarterly compliance evidence packages (SOC 2 summary, configuration snapshots, and control attestations) via a secure shared location. Provider will make available aggregated or redacted logs covering the prior 90 days upon receipt of a valid audit request and NDA; live raw logs are not provided as a standard offering.

Operational notes (InfoSec):
- Vivek: confirm we can provide exec summaries within 30 days and prepare quarterly evidence packs. If we need additional time for SOC 2 disclosures, call it out now.
- Priyom: sales ops to confirm whether a 30-day notice commitment is acceptable to ACME as standard.

If no strong objections, I recommend proposing the above as our baseline and negotiating any customer-specific changes.

title: ExpoCloud West — lead onramp & consent routing
preview:
From: Sara Kline <sara.kline@expotech.com>
To: Aditya Rao <aditya@redwood.com>
Date: Tue, 10 Feb 2026 09:14:00 -0800
Subject: ExpoCloud West — lead capture & consent clarifications

Hi Aditya,

Thanks again for coordinating with ExpoTech on lead ingestion for ExpoCloud West (Mar 2-4). Two quick items before we lock the integration:

1) Formats: our badge scanners export a nightly CSV with the following columns: badge_id, first_name, last_name, email (optional), company, title, checkin_time. We can also include a hashed email field instead of plain email.
2) Consent: we need to make sure your HubSpot sync preserves the opt-in state and that event attendees receive the correct marketing preference.

I've attached the event spec and a sample export. Can you confirm whether Redwood can:
- Accept hashed emails as the dedupe key if email is present;
- Push contact creation to HubSpot with a source property \"expo_cloudwest\"; and
- Store raw scan exports for a short retention window for debugging?

We have a technical ops lead on our side (expo-ops@expotech.com) who can provide sample outputs if helpful.

Appreciate any notes on data retention and consent language we should surface on the onsite forms.

Best,
Sara Kline
Senior Events Manager
ExpoTech Events

Attachment: event_spec_ExpoCloudWest.pdf (pdf, 240KB)

From: Aditya Rao <aditya@redwood.com>
To: Sara Kline <sara.kline@expotech.com>
Cc: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com>, Grace O'Connor <grace@redwood.com>
Date: Tue, 10 Feb 2026 10:02:00 -0800
Subject: Re: ExpoCloud West — lead capture & consent clarifications

Hi Sara — thanks for the clear summary and the spec. Looping in Ben (field ops) and Grace (privacy/compliance). A few quick answers and next steps: 

- Yes, we can accept a hashed_email field as the primary dedupe key; when plain email is present we prefer to store both but only push the hashed value to HubSpot unless the customer explicitly opts in.
- We can create contacts in HubSpot with a \"lead_source\" property set to \"expo_cloudwest\" and tag the contact with campaign id when available.
- Short-term retention (30 days) for raw CSVs is workable for debugging; we prefer not to keep raw PII longer than necessary.

Can Ben outline the suggested HubSpot object mapping and dedupe rules? Grace — can you share the minimum consent language we should require on the onsite form so Sara can update their scanner UI?

If it helps, I'm free for a 30-min sync Thursday 02/12 11:00 PT.

Thanks,
Aditya Rao
Product Manager, Events Integrations
Redwood Inference

Attachment: redwood_onramp_checklist.pdf (pdf, 85KB)

From: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com>
To: Aditya Rao <aditya@redwood.com>, Sara Kline <sara.kline@expotech.com>
Cc: Diego Fernandez <diego@redwood.com>
Date: Wed, 11 Feb 2026 14:37:00 -0800
Subject: Re: ExpoCloud West — lead capture & consent clarifications

Thanks — dug into the sample spec. High-level plan (technical):

1) Ingest: ExpoTech SFTP drops nightly CSV to our ingest bucket. We run a lightweight ETL that normalizes columns. Preferred input columns (we'll accept variants): badge_id, first_name, last_name, email, hashed_email, company, title, checkin_time, device_id.

2) Dedupe: If email is present, our dedupe uses email (normalize lowercasing). If only hashed_email is present, we use hashed_email + badge_id as a composite key for temporary staging, and we recommend mapping hashed_email to HubSpot "email" only if the customer has opted in and provided consent mapping.

3) HubSpot mapping (Contact + Engagement):
- Contact fields: firstname -> first_name, lastname -> last_name, company -> company, jobtitle -> title, email -> email (or hashed_email as fallback).
- Contact property: lead_source = expo_cloudwest; event_date = 2026-03-02
- Engagement: create a timeline event 'expo_checkin' with metadata {badge_id, device_id, checkin_time}.

4) Idempotency: we will use contact.external_id set to badge_id for event-specific dedupe if that is zone-specific and should not overwrite global contact records.

I've attached a starter mapping CSV for your scanner team. Diego, can you validate the device_id field mapping and confirm whether our parser will accept checkin_time in ISO8601?

Ben Carter
Field Ops Engineer, Redwood Inference

Attachment: badge_field_map_v1.csv (csv, 4KB)

From: Grace O'Connor <grace@redwood.com>
To: Sara Kline <sara.kline@expotech.com>, Aditya Rao <aditya@redwood.com>, Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com>
Date: Thu, 12 Feb 2026 08:55:00 -0800
Subject: Re: ExpoCloud West — lead capture & consent clarifications

Hi all — quick compliance notes and proposed onsite consent language.

Consent summary (minimum acceptable):
- Required copy to display near the scanner/button: \"By providing your badge or contact details, you agree that ExpoTech and Redwood Inference may process your contact information for event follow-up and marketing. You may opt out at any time.\"
- On ingestion: mark a boolean property contact.marketing_consent = true/false. Only when true should we push plain email to HubSpot and add to marketing lists.

Data retention:
- Raw CSVs with PII: retain for 30 days for troubleshooting; automatically delete after retention expiry.
- Derived/hashes for dedupe: may be kept for 12 months to support de-duplication across events, unless the customer requests deletion.

Security: ensure SFTP uploads are over TLS with key-based auth; transport-level encryption only (no PII in logs). For SOC 2 alignment we need an audit trail of pushes to HubSpot and proof that hashed_email cannot be reversed by Redwood.

Attachment: consent-language-v2.docx (docx, 6KB)

Thanks,
Grace O'Connor
Privacy & Compliance Lead
Redwood Inference

From: Sara Kline <sara.kline@expotech.com>
To: Aditya Rao <aditya@redwood.com>, Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com>, Grace O'Connor <grace@redwood.com>
Date: Sat, 14 Feb 2026 16:20:00 -0800
Subject: Re: ExpoCloud West — lead capture & consent clarifications

Thanks everyone — all good on our side. We can generate hashed_email (SHA256) in the scanner firmware and will include plain email only when attendees explicitly opt in.

I've attached a fresh sample CSV that matches Ben's mapping. I'm also sending a short DocuSign to sign the data-transfer addendum that specifies 30-day raw retention and HubSpot push behavior. Link: https://www.docusign.net/EXTERNAL/1234abcdef

Attachment: expo_sample_export.csv (csv, 12KB)

—Sara

On a separate note: will your on-site team be able to provide a live retry if an SFTP drop fails? We want a quick SLA window for the day-of support.

To: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com>, Grace O'Connor <grace@redwood.com>, Diego Fernandez <diego@redwood.com>
Date: Mon, 16 Feb 2026 11:30:00 -0800
Subject: Re: ExpoCloud West — lead capture & consent clarifications

- HubSpot: create contact with lead_source=expo_cloudwest and create engagement 'expo_checkin'.

From: Mira Desai <mira@latticeflow.ai>

Cc: ops@latticeflow.ai
Date: Fri, Feb 14, 2026 at 5:58 PM UTC
Subject: URGENT: Streaming responses hanging (partial outputs)

Hi Ava,
We’re seeing streamed chat completions hang part way through. The connection stays open but no more events arrive. Users get partial answers.

This started today. We’re on stream=true (SSE).
Example request_id: req_9e2c... around 18:12 UTC.

Can you escalate?

— Mira


From: Ava Chen <ava@redwoodinference.com>
To: Mira Desai <mira@latticeflow.ai>
Date: Fri, Feb 14, 2026 at 6:07 PM UTC
Subject: Re: URGENT: Streaming responses hanging (partial outputs)

Thanks Mira — escalating now. Can you confirm region + whether you’re going through any proxy (Cloudflare, ALB, etc.)?

I’m opening a support ticket so we can track it: SUP-1842.

— Ava
Customer Success
Redwood Inference


From: Mira Desai <mira@latticeflow.ai>
To: Ava Chen <ava@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Fri, Feb 14, 2026 at 6:15 PM UTC
Subject: Re: URGENT: Streaming responses hanging (partial outputs)

Region is us-east. We’re using our own ALB in front of the client service but no special proxy for the Redwood call.

Sometimes it looks like it “freezes” ~20s in. No error.


From: Ben Carter <ben@redwoodinference.com>
To: Mira Desai <mira@latticeflow.ai>
Cc: Ava Chen <ava@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Sat, Feb 15, 2026 at 2:32 AM UTC
Subject: Re: URGENT: Streaming responses hanging (partial outputs)

Jumping in from Eng. We’re investigating gateway flushing for SSE.

Two quick asks:
1) Are you using HTTP/2 from the client?
2) Can you share if you see the TCP connection close at the client watchdog timeout, or does it remain open until you cancel?


From: Ava Chen <ava@redwoodinference.com>
To: Mira Desai <mira@latticeflow.ai>
Date: Sun, Feb 16, 2026 at 9:10 AM UTC
Subject: Re: URGENT: Streaming responses hanging (partial outputs)

Update: we rolled a mitigation in us-east that sends periodic keepalive comments on the stream to avoid intermediary buffering.

Can you retest and let us know if the hangs stop?


From: Mira Desai <mira@latticeflow.ai>
To: Ava Chen <ava@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Sun, Feb 16, 2026 at 9:21 AM UTC
Subject: Re: URGENT: Streaming responses hanging (partial outputs)

title: Hyperstack evaluation — unit economics & proposed test matrix
preview:
From: Aditya Rao <aditya@redwood.com>
To: Sarah Ng <sarah@hyperstack.com>
Cc: Marissa Cole <marissa@redwood.com>
Date: Thu, 12 Feb 2026 15:03:00 +0000
Subject: Hyperstack evaluation — unit economics & proposed test matrix

Sarah — thanks for the call earlier. Quick recap and proposed next steps so we have a concrete test plan we can run next week.

Recap (what we heard)
- Primary evaluation lens: unit cost per 1M tokens under realistic production mix (short chat messages + occasional long generations).
- Constraints: sub-150ms median latency target, p95 < 400ms, and ability to preserve conversational state (KV cache) for prompt reuse.
- Hyperscaler interest: cheaper headline price but unclear how caching, batching, and model variants affect real-world effective cost.

Redwood baseline (ballpark numbers for planning)
- Hosted rwd-small / rwd-medium options: baseline modeled cost ~ $25–28 per 1M tokens at low batching (1–4) and no cache.
- With continuous batching + conservative 30% prefix cache hit: effective cost falls to ~$17–19 per 1M tokens.
- Dedicated/reserved pool (pinned capacity) can push that to ~$12–15 per 1M tokens for sustained steady throughput given committed GPUs and quantized kernels.

Hyperscaler claim (from call): $15 per 1M tokens. Open questions: what batch sizes and latency targets underpin that number? Do they expose KV/prefix caching (transparent to app) or require custom infra?

Proposed measurable experiments (what we want to run)
1) 100k token warmup run (simulate ramp) — measure median/p95/p99 latencies, tcp time, and token throughput.
2) 1M token economic pass with three axes: batch sizes [1, 4, 16], cache hit rates [0%, 30%, 60%] (we'll seed prefix cache where possible), and model variant [fp16, int8].
3) Failure/fallback resilience: induce transient GPU saturation and confirm automated fallback latency/regression.

Deliverables from Redwood for the test
- Test harness (we provide a script that emits the token mix and collects per-request token counts + latency).
- Precomputed cost model spreadsheet (attached) with per-step assumptions and sensitivity knobs.
- 2-week MAP PDF with timing and responsibilities (attached).

What we need from you
- A representative 1,000–2,000 prompt sample (anonymized) and approximate concurrency target (RPS) — helps pick batch windows.
- Any constraints on where tests must run (cloud region, VPC peering only, or can we run hosted?).

Proposed timing
- Prep this week (get prompts + region) — run experiments early next week — deliver consolidated results + recommendation within 48 hours of finish.

Next step: if this looks good I’ll share the test harness and put together the low-friction access keys for a short hosted canary (or provision a small dedicated pool if you prefer to test on reserved capacity).

Attachments: redwood-econ-scenarios-1M.xlsx, mutual-action-plan-hyperstack.pdf

—
Aditya Rao
Senior Solutions Engineer | Redwood Inference
aditya@redwood.com | +1 555-0184




From: Sarah Ng <sarah@hyperstack.com>
To: Aditya Rao <aditya@redwood.com>
Cc: procurement@hyperstack.com
Date: Thu, 12 Feb 2026 18:21:00 +0000
Subject: Re: Hyperstack evaluation — unit economics & proposed test matrix

Thanks, Aditya — this is exactly the level of detail we need. A few quick clarifications: 

- We'll provide a 1,200-sample anonymized prompt set (mix of short chat ~75% and long generations ~25%) and target steady-state concurrency ~35 RPS.
- Region: US-east preferred; we can accept hosted but will need the option to run via our cloud account if we replicate later.
- Procurement is copied to coordinate any temporary testing agreement/credits.

Two asks from our side: can you include an explicit latency-to-cost sensitivity (e.g., if we must hit 100ms median instead of 150ms how does cost change)? And can you call out any model-level quality deltas you expect across fp16 vs int8?

If the attached MAP includes roles, please add our infra lead Tim (tim@cloudfast.io) — he’ll validate network/peering constraints.

I’m available to kick off the test planning call Friday morning (US ET) or Monday.

—
Sarah Ng
Head of AI | Hyperstack Inc.
sarah@hyperstack.com



From: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com>
To: Aditya Rao <aditya@redwood.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa@redwood.com>
Date: Fri, 13 Feb 2026 09:10:00 +0000
Subject: RE: Hyperstack evaluation — unit economics & proposed test matrix (internal)

Looping internal notes here so we’re aligned before we commit credits: 

- Pricing/commercial: recommend offering a short-term test credit covering the hosted run up to 1.2M tokens plus a 25% evaluation discount on any Dedicated trial capacity (covering 2 weeks). Put the numbers in the MAP so procurement has a clear ask.
- Technical ask: push to run both hosted and a dedicated canary (if they have a short-lived reserved box) — that shows the range of effective costs. Hyperscalers often quote peak-efficiency numbers that assume sustained single-model throughput; our strength is autoscaling + graceful fallbacks and cache effectiveness on real traffic.
- Differentiators to highlight in test report: prefix/KV cache hit amplification, continuous batching with latency SLO knobs, and model-variant fallbacks (auto switch to quantized runtime without client changes).

I’ll draft a short pricing appendix for the MAP and hand off to you, Aditya, to include in the PDF.

—Ben
VP, Sales | Redwood Inference


From: Aditya Rao <aditya@redwood.com>
To: Sarah Ng <sarah@hyperstack.com>, Tim <tim@cloudfast.io>
Cc: Marissa Cole <marissa@redwood.com>, Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com>, procurement@hyperstack.com
Date: Mon, 16 Feb 2026 08:04:00 +0000
Subject: Re: Hyperstack evaluation — unit economics & proposed test matrix

Sarah / Tim — thanks for confirming. Attached are: 1) redwood-econ-scenarios-1M.xlsx (cost model with sensitivity knobs for latency, batch size, and cache hit rate), and 2) mutual-action-plan-hyperstack.pdf (roles, timeline, and the credit offer Ben mentioned).

Quick answers to your asks:
- Latency-to-cost sensitivity: included in the workbook — short story: moving median target from 150ms -> 100ms increases effective cost by ~18–30% in many mixed workloads because it constrains batching windows and reduces achievable cache effectiveness. It’s in the "Latency Sensitivity" tab.
- FP16 vs INT8: we show expected per-token throughput and a quality note — INT8 can reduce cost by ~20–35% vs fp16 for generative workloads but expect a small delta on rare tokens; we recommend using eval prompts to validate quality prior to sweeping quantization at scale.

Proposed immediate schedule:
- Tue 17 Feb: receive prompt set + region confirmation.
- Wed 18 Feb: Redwood turnkey run (hosted) — 100k warmup then 1M economic pass.
- Thu 19 Feb: analysis and joint readout + recommendation (hosted vs dedicated decision).

Tim — can you confirm if Hyperstack wants the dedicated run to be provisioned in their cloud account or if a hosted dedicated sandbox is easier for the initial test? If you prefer the cloud account, we’ll share infra runbook and IAM scopes needed.

Link to MAP + HubSpot record: https://app.hubspot.com/contacts/1234567/company/98765

Regards,
Aditya


From: Sarah Ng <sarah@hyperstack.com>
To: Aditya Rao <aditya@redwood.com>, Tim <tim@cloudfast.io>
Cc: Marissa Cole <marissa@redwood.com>, Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com>, procurement@hyperstack.com
Date: Mon, 16 Feb 2026 09:12:00 +0000
Subject: Re: Hyperstack evaluation — unit economics & proposed test matrix

Sounds good — we’ll send the prompt set today and confirm US-east. Tim prefers we provision in our cloud account for the dedicated run (so he can validate networking), but hosted first is fine.

Friday morning call doesn’t work for us; Monday 17 Feb 14:00 US ET (18:00 UTC) works to kick off prep and sync on keys.

Thanks — looking forward to the test.

—
Sarah

Quick answers to your asks:
- Latency-to-cost sensitivity: included in the workbook — short story: moving median target from 150ms -> 100ms increases effective cost by ~18–30% in many mixed workloads because it constrains batching windows and reduces achievable cache effectiveness. It’s in the "Latency Sensitivity" tab.
- FP16 vs INT8: we show expected per-token throughput and a quality note — INT8 can reduce cost by ~20–35% vs fp16 for generative workloads but expect a small delta on rare tokens; we recommend using eval prompts to validate quality prior to sweeping quantization at scale.

Proposed immediate schedule:
- Tue 17 Feb: receive prompt set + region confirmation.
- Wed 18 Feb: Redwood turnkey run (hosted) — 100k warmup then 1M economic pass.
- Thu 19 Feb: analysis and joint readout + recommendation (hosted vs dedicated decision).

Tim — can you confirm if Hyperstack wants the dedicated run to be provisioned in their cloud account or if a hosted dedicated sandbox is easier for the initial test? If you prefer the cloud account, we’ll share infra runbook and IAM scopes needed.

Link to MAP + HubSpot record: https://app.hubspot.com/contacts/1234567/company/98765

Aditya

- Monthly commit: $145,000

- Security bundle: $15,000 one-time

- Single-tenant premium: +15%

- Single-tenant premium covers dedicated control plane, isolated metadata store, per-customer audit logging retained 12 months, and integration effort for KMS/HSM (we assume standard KMS — HSM adds an uplift).

title: Re: Redwood benchmarking methodology — ACME performance questionnaire
preview:
From: Samantha Reed <samantha.reed@acme.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben_carter@redwo od.ai>, Lauren Bishop <lauren_bishop@redwood.ai>
Cc: infra-ops@acme-corp.com
Date: Tue, 10 Feb 2026 09:12:00 -0800
Subject: ACME performance questionnaire — initial items

Ben / Lauren,

Thanks for taking this on. Attached is the ACME performance questionnaire — the section our infra team cares most about is the benchmarking methodology and the concrete numbers for latency and throughput under representative load. We need:

- Test harness and scripts used (so we can re-run)
- Exact model/config used (quantization, batch size, sequence length)
- VM/GPU instance types and GPU utilization during tests
- P50/P90/P99 latency for both single-request and batched inferencing
- Throughput (tokens/sec and reqs/sec) at steady state and during scale events
- Notes on caching, KV cache warm-up behavior, and cold-start variance
- Any observed degradation at sustained high concurrency (errors/queueing)

Can you get this back by EOD Thursday? Our procurement needs the numbers before the final evaluation.

Best,
Samantha Reed
Senior SRE, ACME Corp

Attachment: acme_performance_questions_v2.xlsx (spreadsheet)

From: Ben Carter <ben_carter@redwood.ai>
To: Samantha Reed <samantha.reed@acme.com>
Cc: lauren_bishop@redwood.ai, rishi_malhotra@redwood.ai
Date: Tue, 10 Feb 2026 10:02:00 -0800
Subject: Re: ACME performance questionnaire — initial items

Samantha — thanks for the clear list. We can provide a reproducible test package and a short report. Quick notes:

- We’ll include the exact test harness (JMeter + our load driver + the benchmark scripts) and a Docker Compose to replicate locally.
- Report will include p50/p90/p99 measured at seq lengths 64 and 512 and at batch sizes 1, 8, 32.
- Hardware: results on NVIDIA A100 and an 8xA40 dedicated pool (we’ll specify VM flavors and driver versions).
- We’ll call out differences when using 8-bit quantization vs FP16 — that typically changes tokens/sec by ~1.6–2x and shifts p99 slightly.

Lauren and Rishi — can you assemble the artifacts and a one-page methodology so we can send before EOD Thursday (2026-02-12)?

Thanks,
Ben Carter
Enterprise Sales
Redwood Inference



From: Lauren Bishop <lauren_bishop@redwood.ai>
To: Samantha Reed <samantha.reed@acme.com>, Ben Carter <ben_carter@redwood.ai>
Cc: rishi_malhotra@redwood.ai
Date: Thu, 12 Feb 2026 14:19:00 -0800
Subject: Re: ACME performance questionnaire — test methodology & results (draft)

Samantha / Ben — attaching our draft report and the test scripts bundle. High-level summary first (see attached PDF for tables and charts):

- Test harness: custom benchmark runner (python) + HTTP/2 streaming client. Scripts included in benchmark_test_scripts_v1.tar.gz. We used a synthetic load generator to control QPS and concurrent in-flight requests.
- Model and config: gpt-neox-20b (Redwood-tuned), served in two variants: FP16 and 8-bit-quantized (QLoRA style). KV cache warm-up measured by sending a 128-token prefix before measured inference.
- Hardware: NVIDIA A100 80GB (hosted), measured on both single-GPU and an 8-GPU dedicated pool (N instances of a dedicated service). OS/driver: Ubuntu 22.04, CUDA 11.8, cuDNN 8.4.
- Key numbers (representative, median of 5 runs):
  - Single-request, seq=64, batch=1: FP16 p50=18 ms, p90=35 ms, p99=72 ms; 8-bit p50=10 ms, p90=22 ms, p99=58 ms.
  - Batched (batch=8), seq=64: FP16 throughput=420 tokens/sec/GPU, 8-bit=760 tokens/sec/GPU.
  - Long seq (seq=512), batch=1: FP16 p50=120 ms, p90=260 ms, p99=520 ms.
- Scaling behavior: linear throughput scaling observed up to 6 GPUs for this workload (sustained token stream). After ~6–8 GPUs we saw queueing due to network transfer overhead in our dedicated pool; we document mitigations (sharded routing and request pinning).
- Cold start & KV cache: cold-start median penalty ~85–120 ms for the first request after idle; warmed KV cache removes ~70% of incremental latency for subsequent requests.
- Error rates: under sustained 95th percentile load we observed <0.1% 5xx errors; at 110% of target QPS error rate climbed to ~1.4% with increased queue latency.

Attachments: redwood_throughput_latency_report_Q1_2026.pdf (tables + graphs), benchmark_test_scripts_v1.tar.gz (scripts), rfi_mapping_sheet.xlsx (Q->answer map).

Let me know if you want raw CSV outputs or the consolidated JMeter logs; we can drop them in a shared Drive link.

Thanks,
Lauren Bishop
Technical Sales Engineer
Redwood Inference



From: Rishi Malhotra <rishi_malhotra@redwood.ai>
To: Samantha Reed <samantha.reed@acme.com>, Lauren Bishop <lauren_bishop@redwood.ai>, Ben Carter <ben_carter@redwood.ai>
Date: Fri, 13 Feb 2026 11:07:00 -0800
Subject: Re: ACME performance questionnaire — notes on kernel selection & quantization impacts

Samantha — quick technical addendum to Lauren’s report:

- Kernel selection: our runtime chooses attention kernels based on sequence length heuristics. For seq<=128 we use a fused attention kernel that reduces memory copies; that explains much of the p90 improvement at short sequences. We’ll add an explicit table in the PDF mapping kernels to latency deltas.
- Quantization caveats: 8-bit results are dependent on per-layer calibration and can vary with prompt distribution. The numbers we gave are for general conversational prompts; for heavy numeric/SQL-style prompts you may see a <10% degradation vs our average.
- Batching vs latency: while batching increases tokens/sec significantly for throughput-oriented workloads, it increases tail latency for single interactive requests — we recommend an adaptive small-batch policy (1–4) for mixed workloads, with a fast-path single-request lane for p99-sensitive calls.

If ACME wants a bespoke run (your actual dataset + prompts), we can schedule a dedicated run in our sandbox to generate apples-to-apples numbers. That would also allow us to provide the raw perf traces.

—Rishi
Senior Applied ML Engineer
Redwood Inference



From: Ben Carter <ben_carter@redwood.ai>
To: Samantha Reed <samantha.reed@acme.com>
Cc: lauren_bishop@redwood.ai, rishi_malhotra@redwood.ai, infra-ops@acme-corp.com
Date: Sun, 15 Feb 2026 16:46:00 -0800
Subject: Re: ACME performance questionnaire — deliverables & next steps

Samantha — looping back with the consolidated artifacts (attached). Action items:

1) We uploaded the full CSV traces and the driver scripts to the Drive link in the email metadata; you should have access via the hubspot link if you need raw logs.
2) If you want the bespoke run Rishi mentioned, propose two 2-hour slots next week and we’ll reserve a dedicated 4-GPU node for ACME to ensure isolation.
3) Please confirm whether procurement needs a shorter summary table only (one page) or the full report for evaluation. We can condense into a single-page benchmark summary if that’s preferable.

Happy to hop on a 30-minute call to walk through the methodology and answer any questions — I’m free Tue/Wed 10–2 PT.

Best regards,
Ben Carter
Enterprise Sales
Redwood Inference

Attachments: redwood_throughput_latency_report_Q1_2026.pdf, benchmark_test_scripts_v1.tar.gz, rfi_mapping_sheet.xlsx

Let me know if you want raw CSV outputs or the consolidated JMeter logs; we can drop them in a shared Drive link.

Lauren Bishop
Technical Sales Engineer
Redwood Inference



From: Rishi Malhotra <rishi_malhotra@redwood.ai>
To: Samantha Reed <samantha.reed@acme.com>, Lauren Bishop <lauren_bishop@redwood.ai>, Ben Carter <ben_carter@redwood.ai>
Date: Fri, 13 Feb 2026 11:07:00 -0800
Subject: Re: ACME performance questionnaire — notes on kernel selection & quantization impacts

Samantha — quick technical addendum to Lauren’s report:

- Kernel selection: our runtime chooses attention kernels based on sequence length heuristics. For seq<=128 we use a fused attention kernel that reduces memory copies; that explains much of the p90 improvement at short sequences. We’ll add an explicit table in the PDF mapping kernels to latency deltas.
- Quantization caveats: 8-bit results are dependent on per-layer calibration and can vary with prompt distribution. The numbers we gave are for general conversational prompts; for heavy numeric/SQL-style prompts you may see a <10% degradation vs our average.
- Batching vs latency: while batching increases tokens/sec significantly for throughput-oriented workloads, it increases tail latency for single interactive requests — we recommend an adaptive small-batch policy (1–4) for mixed workloads, with a fast-path single-request lane for p99-sensitive calls.

If ACME wants a bespoke run (your actual dataset + prompts), we can schedule a dedicated run in our sandbox to generate apples-to-apples numbers. That would also allow us to provide the raw perf traces.

Subject: Packaging options and downgrade/upgrade paths for inference platform

Hi Ben,

Thanks for the demo last week — Helix is excited about the latency gains we saw on your hosted endpoints. We're starting to scope a migration path where one of our core products moves to Dedicated/Reserved capacity, while a couple of experimental services remain on Hosted.

Before we commit, procurement has three key asks:
- Clear upgrade/downgrade mechanics (if usage drops after a quarter, what happens to reserved capacity and credits?)
- Recommended feature bundles (autoscaling, observability retention, KV cache/caching recommendations, quantization guidance) and how those map to pricing tiers
- Typical discount thresholds for 12/24 month commits and whether onboarding credits are available for pilot/POC phases

We're aiming to get a formal quote and a redline-friendly term sheet we can circulate with procurement next week. Can you outline what a conservative upgrade path + fallback would look like and share any example bundles?

Anna

Helix AI

(attachments: none in this message)

From: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com>
To: Anna Li <anna@helix.ai>
Cc: Aditya Rao <aditya@redwood.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa@redwood.com>
Date: Wed, 11 Feb 2026 14:30:00 -0800
Subject: Re: Packaging options and downgrade/upgrade paths for inference platform

Anna — thanks for the clear list. High-level answers first, then I'll follow up with a proposed quote and loop in Aditya/Marissa for the contract mechanics and technical bundling.

1) Upgrade/downgrade mechanics
- Dedicated (reserved capacity) is committed with a start date and a minimum term. For most customers we offer a 30-day notice window for reduced throughput tiers and a 60-day notice window for reserved GPU pool reductions (these windows align with our capacity planning).
- Proration: when a downgrade occurs we prorate the remaining reserved term and apply a credit to future usage (credits cannot be refunded). For example, midway through a 12-month reserved term any unused committed capacity value is pro-rated and applied as platform credit.

2) Bundles we commonly recommend
- Standard: Hosted + Redwood Console (30d retention observability)
- Growth: Dedicated 2x pool, autoscaling burst, 90d observability, prefix/KV caching enabled, standard quantization profile
- Enterprise: Dedicated + Private control plane option, 12mo retention for logs, audit log exports, HSM/KMS integration, plus prioritized support

3) Discount bands (typical)
- 12 month commit: ~10–15% off list (depends on committed throughput)
- 24 month commit: ~15–20% off list
- Pilot/POC: we can provide onboarding credits for short trials (we often do $10k–$25k credits depending on the scope)

I've attached a quick packing offer and our feature matrix so you can see what maps to each tier. Can Aditya handle a shot at a staged commercial that includes a pilot credit and a 12 month option? Marissa, can you mark any technical caveats for keeping some services on Hosted while routing core traffic to Dedicated (cache warming, prefix routing notes)?

Best,
Ben Carter
Senior Solutions Sales
Redwood Inference

(attachments: pack_offer_v1.pdf, redwood-feature-matrix.xlsx)

title: Co-sell: mapping Seller-of-Record & invoice path (W-8/W-9, VAT)
preview:
From: Mateo Alvarez <mateo@cloudpartner.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.ai>
Cc: accounts-payable@prospectco.com
Date: 2026-02-10T09:12:00-08:00
Subject: Co-sell listing — invoice routing & documentation needed

Ben — thanks for the demo yesterday. Procurement at ProspectCo needs clarity on how invoices will appear and which legal entity will be the seller-of-record for AWS Marketplace purchases under our co-sell listing. They also asked what tax forms and VAT evidence you need from us so AP can route payments correctly.

Quick list of questions from their AP team (copied):
- If we buy via AWS Marketplace, do we get an invoice from Redwood or from CloudPartner (ourselves as reseller)?
- Which tax entity should ProspectCo reference on the PO?
- Do you need a W‑8/W‑9 from us, and where should we upload it?
- For EU customers, do you support reverse-charge VAT evidence?

Attaching their PO and our standard partner agreement for reference. Can your finance/legal confirm next steps?

— Mateo
Head of Partnerships, CloudPartner Ltd


From: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.ai>
To: Mateo Alvarez <mateo@cloudpartner.com>
Cc: Vanessa Ortiz <vanessa@redwood.ai>, Laura Bennett <laura@redwood.ai>, accounts-payable@prospectco.com
Date: 2026-02-10T10:05:00-08:00
Subject: Re: Co-sell listing — invoice routing & documentation needed

Thanks Mateo — good questions. Below is our pragmatic checklist and how different cases map to invoice flow. I’ll loop in Finance and Legal for any edge cases.

Short answers up front:
- Seller-of-Record (SOR) can be configured per listing. For AWS Marketplace co-sell we support both: Redwood as SOR (our marketplace invoice) or partner as SOR (you invoice). Which one you want determines the documentation needed.
- If Redwood is SOR: ProspectCo will receive an invoice from Redwood; we need a completed W‑9 (US) or W‑8 BEN‑E (non-US) from the invoice party (ProspectCo) for tax records.
- If partner is SOR: CloudPartner issues the invoice to ProspectCo — Redwood will provide a marketplace usage report and the partner will remit per the partner contract.
- EU VAT: we support reverse-charge evidence (VAT IDs + self-billing docs) but it requires mapping the buyer VAT ID to the invoice entity before first billing cycle.

Actionable checklist (please confirm which SOR path you want):
1) Confirm SOR: Redwood or CloudPartner (one-line reply).
2) If Redwood SOR: ProspectCo to send PO referencing Redwood legal entity: Redwood Inference, Inc. (EIN: 12-3456789). We will send a Redwood invoice and need either a W‑9 (if ProspectCo is US) or VAT ID + company registration (EU) / W‑8 BEN‑E if ProspectCo is non-US claiming exemption.
3) If Partner SOR: CloudPartner to confirm billing entity and provide partner invoice template; Redwood will provide usage report and marketplace invoice reconciliation file.
4) All cases: upload tax forms to our secure SFTP link (I’ll send separately) and share DocuSign envelope when available for any marketplace seller agreements.

I’m attaching the Partner SOR decision matrix (lightweight) and a sample AWS marketplace invoice layout. If that looks good I’ll get Finance to open a ticket with AWS Marketplace Ops to set the SOR flag.

Next: Vanessa — can you confirm whether we need any extra AP evidence for ProspectCo (PO matching rules)? Laura — FYI on pricing/regulatory mapping.

Thanks,
Ben Carter
Senior Sales Manager, Redwood Inference


From: Vanessa Ortiz <vanessa@redwood.ai>
To: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.ai>, Mateo Alvarez <mateo@cloudpartner.com>
Cc: Laura Bennett <laura@redwood.ai>, accounts-payable@prospectco.com
Date: 2026-02-10T11:03:00-08:00
Subject: Re: Co-sell listing — invoice routing & documentation needed

On Tue, Feb 10, 2026 at 10:05 AM Ben Carter <ben@redwood.ai> wrote:
> I'm attaching the Partner SOR decision matrix (lightweight) and a sample AWS marketplace invoice layout.

Folks — quick compliance notes from Legal/Finance:
- For US buyers: W‑9 from the buyer is stored for our tax records when Redwood invoices them. We do not require a W‑8 from the buyer unless they claim foreign status; W‑8 is typically provided by foreign payees to claim treaty benefits.
- For non-US buyers: we need W‑8 BEN‑E only where the buyer is an entity claiming foreign status AND there is US-source income subject to withholding. Typically marketplace software purchases are not US-source services, but we do keep the W‑8 on file where the buyer requests withholding relief.
- VAT reverse-charge: we will accept the buyer VAT ID and a signed self‑billing acknowledgment. If CloudPartner will act as reseller (SOR), CloudPartner must provide the VAT registration evidence and confirm tax treatment for each EU country involved.
- Withholding: AP teams sometimes ask for an IRS W‑8/BEN-E for the payee to avoid backup withholding when Redwood pays partners. If ProspectCo is paying the partner, partner should provide the correct form to ProspectCo.

Operational ask: Mateo — please confirm SOR by end of day tomorrow. If you choose Redwood SOR we will: (a) create a seller listing in AWS Marketplace, (b) request ProspectCo W‑9/W‑VAT evidence, (c) open a ticket with Marketplace Ops to ensure invoice entity mapping. If you choose partner SOR, share partner billing details and TAX IDs.

I'll attach the secure upload link once Mateo confirms.

Vanessa Ortiz
Head of Partnerships Operations, Redwood Inference

Cc: accounts-payable@prospectco.com

Subject: Re: Co-sell listing — invoice routing & documentation needed

Thanks — we discussed internally and want CloudPartner to remain Seller-of-Record for this account (ProspectCo prefers a single vendor on the invoice). Two follow-ups:
1) We will need the Redwood usage reconciliation file format so our AP can automatically ingest usage lines into our AR system. Can you send the CSV schema?
2) ProspectCo is EU-registered (VAT ID provided in PO). They want to apply reverse-charge. Since CloudPartner is SOR, do you require any invoice-level evidence from ProspectCo for Redwood reporting?

We can provide: partner VAT registration (attached), partner invoice template, and a completed W‑8 BEN‑E for CloudPartner (we are US LLC but claim foreign tax status for some flows). I'll upload the partner docs to the link once you provide it.

Thanks Mateo — good questions. Below is our pragmatic checklist and how different cases map to invoice flow. I’ll loop in Finance and Legal for any edge cases.

Short answers up front:
- Seller-of-Record (SOR) can be configured per listing. For AWS Marketplace co-sell we support both: Redwood as SOR (our marketplace invoice) or partner as SOR (you invoice). Which one you want determines the documentation needed.
- If Redwood is SOR: ProspectCo will receive an invoice from Redwood; we need a completed W‑9 (US) or W‑8 BEN‑E (non-US) from the invoice party (ProspectCo) for tax records.
- If partner is SOR: CloudPartner issues the invoice to ProspectCo — Redwood will provide a marketplace usage report and the partner will remit per the partner contract.
- EU VAT: we support reverse-charge evidence (VAT IDs + self-billing docs) but it requires mapping the buyer VAT ID to the invoice entity before first billing cycle.

Actionable checklist (please confirm which SOR path you want):
1) Confirm SOR: Redwood or CloudPartner (one-line reply).
2) If Redwood SOR: ProspectCo to send PO referencing Redwood legal entity: Redwood Inference, Inc. (EIN: 12-3456789). We will send a Redwood invoice and need either a W‑9 (if ProspectCo is US) or VAT ID + company registration (EU) / W‑8 BEN‑E if ProspectCo is non-US claiming exemption.
3) If Partner SOR: CloudPartner to confirm billing entity and provide partner invoice template; Redwood will provide usage report and marketplace invoice reconciliation file.
4) All cases: upload tax forms to our secure SFTP link (I’ll send separately) and share DocuSign envelope when available for any marketplace seller agreements.

I’m attaching the Partner SOR decision matrix (lightweight) and a sample AWS marketplace invoice layout. If that looks good I’ll get Finance to open a ticket with AWS Marketplace Ops to set the SOR flag.

Next: Vanessa — can you confirm whether we need any extra AP evidence for ProspectCo (PO matching rules)? Laura — FYI on pricing/regulatory mapping.

Thanks,
Ben Carter
Senior Sales Manager, Redwood Inference

> I'm attaching the Partner SOR decision matrix (lightweight) and a sample AWS marketplace invoice layout.

Folks — quick compliance notes from Legal/Finance:
- For US buyers: W‑9 from the buyer is stored for our tax records when Redwood invoices them. We do not require a W‑8 from the buyer unless they claim foreign status; W‑8 is typically provided by foreign payees to claim treaty benefits.
- For non-US buyers: we need W‑8 BEN‑E only where the buyer is an entity claiming foreign status AND there is US-source income subject to withholding. Typically marketplace software purchases are not US-source services, but we do keep the W‑8 on file where the buyer requests withholding relief.
- VAT reverse-charge: we will accept the buyer VAT ID and a signed self‑billing acknowledgment. If CloudPartner will act as reseller (SOR), CloudPartner must provide the VAT registration evidence and confirm tax treatment for each EU country involved.
- Withholding: AP teams sometimes ask for an IRS W‑8/BEN-E for the payee to avoid backup withholding when Redwood pays partners. If ProspectCo is paying the partner, partner should provide the correct form to ProspectCo.

Operational ask: Mateo — please confirm SOR by end of day tomorrow. If you choose Redwood SOR we will: (a) create a seller listing in AWS Marketplace, (b) request ProspectCo W‑9/W‑VAT evidence, (c) open a ticket with Marketplace Ops to ensure invoice entity mapping. If you choose partner SOR, share partner billing details and TAX IDs.

I'll attach the secure upload link once Mateo confirms.

Vanessa Ortiz
Head of Partnerships Operations, Redwood Inference
